During the remediation process, HVAC vents and ducts should be sealed off to prevent spores from being carried into other areas. Air scrubbers with HEPA filtration should be used to ensure that any spores in the system are captured before the air is reintroduced into the space.

Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) is also an important aspect of containment. Restoration workers should wear appropriate PPE to protect themselves from inhaling airborne mould spores. This includes respirators equipped with HEPA filters, protective suits, gloves, and goggles. PPE helps prevent contamination from spreading outside the contained area and ensures the safety of those working in the affected zone.

By using these containment strategies, professionals can significantly reduce the risk of airborne mould spores contaminating areas outside of the affected zone. These methods are essential for managing IAQ during mould remediation and ensuring that the work environment remains safe for everyone involved.

modules.title: Remediation Techniques for Mould and Airborne Spores

lessons.title: Remediation Techniques for Mould and Airborne Spores — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Once mould has been contained, the next step is remediation—removing the contamination to restore safe and healthy indoor air quality. Remediation involves both physical removal of mould and decontamination of the environment to ensure that spores no longer pose a threat to IAQ. It is critical that all aspects of the remediation process are carefully managed to prevent further spread of airborne mould.

The first step in the remediation process is the removal of contaminated materials. This includes any building materials or furnishings that are heavily affected by mould, such as drywall, carpeting, insulation, and wooden beams. These materials should be removed and sealed in plastic bags to ensure that spores are not released into the air during removal. The sealed materials should be disposed of safely, in accordance with local regulations, to prevent further contamination.

In addition to removing contaminated materials, cleaning surfaces with mould-killing agents is necessary. For less severely contaminated areas, cleaning with anti-microbial solutions will help eliminate the mould growth on surfaces. This process requires the use of brushes, scrapers, and other tools to physically remove mould from surfaces. It is crucial to ensure that the cleaning process does not disturb mould spores in a way that could cause them to become airborne. After cleaning, the affected areas must be dried thoroughly to prevent the re-growth of mould.

Air filtration is a key part of the remediation process. HEPA air scrubbers are often used to capture airborne spores and improve IAQ during remediation. These machines are equipped with filters that can trap even the smallest of mould spores, preventing them from circulating in the air. In addition, HEPA vacuums are used to clean up dust and debris from surfaces, ensuring that the mould contamination is fully removed.

Once the area has been cleaned and decontaminated, drying is essential to preventing the mould from returning. Using dehumidifiers and air movers, restoration professionals can reduce moisture levels in the air and in the materials themselves. Keeping humidity levels below 60% is key to preventing mould from returning, as mould thrives in moist environments.

Finally, it may be necessary to apply a mould-resistant sealant to vulnerable surfaces after remediation. This helps to prevent the regrowth of mould and ensures that IAQ remains safe and healthy for occupants.

modules.title: Monitoring and Maintaining IAQ After Mould Remediation

lessons.title: Monitoring and Maintaining IAQ After Mould Remediation — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Once mould remediation is complete, it is important to continue monitoring and maintaining Indoor Air Quality to ensure the building remains free from mould and other contaminants. Regular inspection, air quality testing, and moisture monitoring are essential steps in ensuring that IAQ remains at safe levels after remediation.

Post-remediation air testing should be conducted to confirm that all airborne spores have been removed. Air sampling can be done using specialised equipment to measure the concentration of mould spores in the air. Testing will help confirm that the remediation process has been successful and that spore levels are within safe limits.

Additionally, continuous humidity monitoring is essential to prevent the return of mould. Humidity sensors should be installed to track moisture levels in the building, particularly in high-risk areas like bathrooms and kitchens. Keeping humidity levels consistently low will help prevent mould from regrowing and ensure that IAQ remains at healthy levels.

It is also important to conduct regular inspections of the building after remediation. This includes checking for visible signs of mould growth, inspecting HVAC systems, and monitoring any areas that are prone to moisture accumulation. Inspections will help identify potential problems before they escalate and lead to further contamination.

Finally, ongoing air filtration should continue to be used, especially in the immediate aftermath of remediation. HEPA filters can be used in air purifiers and HVAC systems to continue filtering out any remaining spores and improve IAQ.

By following these steps, professionals can ensure that the building remains mould-free and that Indoor Air Quality remains at safe, healthy levels.

Key takeaways

  • Mould is a primary contributor to poor Indoor Air Quality after water damage; its airborne spores can trigger reactions ranging from sneezing and coughing to asthma attacks, and some moulds produce mycotoxins.
  • Moisture is the most important factor in mould spread; humidity above 60% favours growth, and spores travel readily through airflow, people's movement, and HVAC systems.
  • Containment relies on negative pressure, sealed plastic barriers, isolating the HVAC system, and wearing HEPA respirators, suits, gloves and goggles.
  • Remediation combines removing and bagging contaminated materials, cleaning with anti-microbial agents, HEPA filtration, thorough drying to keep humidity below 60%, and applying mould-resistant sealants.
  • After the job, ongoing air testing, humidity monitoring, regular inspections, and HEPA filtration keep IAQ at safe levels.

In summary, controlling airborne mould means understanding how spores spread, containing the affected zone, remediating thoroughly, and then verifying and maintaining Indoor Air Quality over time.

courses.title: Introduction to Improving Indoor Air Quality After Water Damage
courses.description:
Welcome to the course “Improving Indoor Air Quality After Water Damage.” In the cleaning and restoration industry, dealing with water damage is a common challenge, and one of the often-overlooked aspects of water damage remediation is the impact on indoor air quality. Water damage can introduce a range of harmful contaminants into the indoor environment, from mould growth to bacteria and volatile organic compounds (VOCs), all of which can severely affect the health of building occupants and workers.

The air quality of a building after water damage needs to be carefully monitored and improved to prevent long-term health risks and ensure that the space is habitable and safe. This course will provide you with essential knowledge about how to identify and address air quality issues caused by water damage. You will learn about the contaminants that commonly appear after water damage, the tools and methods to assess air quality, and best practices for mitigating these contaminants to restore safe and healthy indoor air.

By the end of this course, you will be equipped to effectively improve indoor air quality in water-damaged buildings, ensuring that your restoration efforts go beyond structural repairs to create a healthy environment for everyone involved.
courses.shortDescription: Understand how water damage affects indoor air quality and how to assess, remediate, and document air quality improvements.

modules.title: The Impact of Water Damage on Indoor Air Quality

lessons.title: The Impact of Water Damage on Indoor Air Quality — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this course you will be able to:

  • Explain how water damage affects indoor air quality and identify the common contaminants it introduces, including mould, bacteria, VOCs and allergens.
  • Recognise the tools used to assess indoor air quality, such as moisture meters, infrared cameras, air sampling equipment, Photoionisation Detectors (PIDs) and temperature and humidity sensors.
  • Apply remediation techniques including mould removal, dehumidification, HEPA air filtration, activated carbon filtration, negative air pressure containment and ventilation to improve air quality.
  • Document and report air quality improvements using before-and-after measurements for clients, insurers and relevant authorities.

Water damage has far-reaching effects on indoor air quality. When water infiltrates a building, it can cause structural damage and lead to the introduction of various contaminants into the indoor environment. These contaminants can significantly degrade air quality and pose serious health risks to building occupants and restoration professionals alike.

The most common contaminant found after water damage is mould. When water is absorbed into building materials such as drywall, insulation, carpets, or wood, it creates the perfect breeding ground for mould. Mould spores are microscopic and can travel through the air, making it easy for them to spread throughout the building. Mould growth can cause allergic reactions, respiratory issues, and other health problems, especially for individuals with asthma or weakened immune systems. These spores can also be a long-term problem if not properly mitigated.

Bacteria are another concern after water damage. Water that contains biological contaminants, such as floodwater, can introduce harmful bacteria into the indoor environment. Bacteria thrive in damp, humid conditions and can lead to infections, particularly when exposed to areas of the body like the skin, eyes, or respiratory system.

In addition to mould and bacteria, volatile organic compounds (VOCs) can also contribute to poor air quality. VOCs are gases that are emitted from certain building materials, cleaning products, and other chemicals, and they can be released when materials are exposed to water. Common sources of VOCs after water damage include treated wood, adhesives, paints, and insulation materials. Prolonged exposure to high levels of VOCs can cause headaches, dizziness, nausea, and irritation of the eyes, nose, and throat.

Furthermore, dust mites and other allergens can proliferate in damp conditions. Dust mites feed on organic matter such as dead skin cells, and they thrive in high humidity. When water damage occurs, moisture can cause dust mites to multiply rapidly, exacerbating allergic reactions in susceptible individuals.

The first step in addressing indoor air quality after water damage is understanding the types of contaminants that may be present. This understanding will inform the methods used to assess and improve air quality during the restoration process.

modules.title: Identifying Common Contaminants After Water Damage

lessons.title: Identifying Common Contaminants After Water Damage — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
After water damage, various contaminants can infiltrate indoor environments, but the most common ones are mould, bacteria, VOCs, and allergens. Identifying these contaminants early is essential to effectively address air quality issues and prevent further damage.

Mould is the most prevalent problem that arises after water damage. As mentioned earlier, mould thrives in damp, humid conditions, and it can start growing within 24 to 48 hours of water exposure. Mould can grow on a wide range of surfaces, including drywall, carpets, wooden beams, and insulation. Common signs of mould include visible mould growth (often black, green, or white in appearance) and a musty smell. However, mould can also be present without visible signs, as it may be hidden within walls, ceilings, or under flooring.

To identify mould, restoration professionals should use moisture meters and infrared thermography to detect hidden moisture that may be conducive to mould growth. Additionally, air sampling for airborne mould spores can help confirm the presence of mould and guide the remediation process.

Bacteria are another concern after water damage, particularly when the water is contaminated with sewage, floodwater, or other biological materials. Bacteria can thrive in standing water and damp environments, leading to the potential spread of harmful pathogens such as Salmonella, E. coli, and Legionella. Bacteria can be particularly hazardous when they contaminate indoor surfaces, such as countertops, floors, or fabrics. Symptoms of bacterial exposure include gastrointestinal issues, respiratory problems, and skin infections.

One way to detect bacterial contamination is through the use of ATP (adenosine triphosphate) testers, which can measure the presence of biological contaminants on surfaces. While this method doesn’t specifically identify the type of bacteria, it can provide a quick indication of contamination levels.

Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs) are released when certain materials are exposed to water. Common sources of VOCs after water damage include paints, adhesives, sealants, and flooring materials. VOCs can be harmful to both short- and long-term health, causing irritation to the eyes, nose, throat, and skin, as well as headaches, dizziness, and nausea. In some cases, exposure to high levels of VOCs may even result in damage to the liver or kidneys.

To identify VOCs, professionals can use Photoionisation Detectors (PIDs). These portable tools can measure VOC concentrations in the air, allowing restoration professionals to determine the level of VOCs present on-site and take necessary action to reduce exposure.

Finally, dust mites and allergens can also become a problem after water damage. As mentioned, these organisms thrive in damp, humid conditions, and they can lead to allergic reactions such as sneezing, itching, and respiratory distress. Regular cleaning, air filtration, and moisture control are critical to reducing dust mite populations and minimising their impact on air quality.

Previous Lesson

modules.title: Tools and Techniques for Assessing Indoor Air Quality

lessons.title: Tools and Techniques for Assessing Indoor Air Quality — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Properly assessing indoor air quality is a key part of improving it after water damage. By using the right tools and techniques, restoration professionals can gather accurate data and take the appropriate steps to rectify the situation.

One of the most important tools in air quality assessment is the moisture meter. Moisture meters measure the amount of water in materials, helping to pinpoint areas where water damage may have occurred. By identifying hidden moisture, restoration professionals can assess the risk of mould growth and determine which materials need to be removed or dried.

Another useful tool for detecting moisture is the infrared camera. This non-invasive tool can identify temperature differences in surfaces, which often correlate with the presence of moisture. Infrared thermography allows professionals to see moisture hidden within walls, ceilings, or floors that may not be visible to the naked eye.

Once moisture has been identified, air sampling equipment can be used to measure the levels of airborne contaminants such as mould spores and VOCs. There are several types of air sampling methods, including spore traps, impaction devices, and bioaerosol sampling pumps, which can capture samples of airborne particles for laboratory analysis. The results of these tests will help determine the severity of the contamination and guide the next steps for remediation.

For detecting VOCs, a Photoionisation Detector (PID) is the ideal tool. A PID is a portable device that can measure the concentration of VOCs in the air in real-time. By using a PID, restoration professionals can quickly assess the level of VOCs and determine whether additional ventilation or air filtration is required to reduce their concentration.

To assess the overall environmental conditions, temperature and humidity sensors should be used to monitor the indoor climate. Humidity is a key factor in mould growth, so maintaining relative humidity levels below 60% is crucial in preventing further damage to air quality. These sensors can help professionals track changes in humidity and take corrective actions, such as activating dehumidifiers or increasing ventilation.

modules.title: Remediation Techniques for Improving Indoor Air Quality

lessons.title: Remediation Techniques for Improving Indoor Air Quality — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Once the contaminants have been identified and measured, remediation techniques must be applied to improve indoor air quality. Depending on the type of contamination, different methods may be required to remove or reduce pollutants.

Mould remediation is one of the most critical aspects of improving air quality after water damage. If mould growth is detected, it is essential to remove the affected materials, such as drywall, carpets, or insulation, that have been contaminated by mould. In some cases, the materials may be cleaned and treated with mould-resistant agents, but extensive mould growth often requires full removal and replacement.

For smaller areas of mould growth, professionals may use specialised cleaning agents or mould-killing solutions, but it’s important to always follow safety guidelines to avoid spreading spores throughout the building. Negative air pressure systems can help contain the mould and prevent spores from migrating to other areas of the building during the remediation process.

Dehumidification is another key component in improving air quality after water damage. Dehumidifiers help to reduce moisture in the air and materials, preventing the growth of mould and dust mites. By maintaining proper humidity levels, professionals can help stabilise the indoor environment and reduce the risk of further contamination.

For VOCs and other airborne contaminants, air filtration systems such as HEPA air scrubbers are essential. These devices remove fine particles and mould spores from the air, improving overall air quality. In cases where VOCs are a concern, activated carbon filters can be used to absorb chemical pollutants, ensuring that the air is safe for occupants.

Ventilation plays a critical role in improving air quality as well. Ensuring proper airflow through the building helps to dilute and remove contaminants from indoor spaces. In some cases, ventilation fans and air movers may be used to speed up the drying process and improve the removal of airborne particles.

modules.title: Documenting and Reporting Air Quality Improvements

lessons.title: Documenting and Reporting Air Quality Improvements — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
After remediation efforts have been completed, documenting the improvements made to indoor air quality is essential. Proper documentation serves as a record of the actions taken to restore air quality and ensures that all necessary steps were followed in accordance with safety regulations.

Documentation should include before-and-after air quality measurements to show the improvements made. This data should include readings from moisture meters, air sampling results, VOC concentrations, and humidity levels. It’s also important to record any steps taken to address contaminants, such as mould removal, air filtration, and dehumidification.

Reports should be clear and concise, outlining the specific actions taken and any equipment used. These reports should be provided to the client, insurance company, and relevant authorities, ensuring that the restoration process is fully documented.

Additionally, regular follow-up inspections may be necessary to ensure that air quality remains at acceptable levels. This may involve re-testing the air and continuing to monitor moisture levels until the environment is completely dry and safe.

By thoroughly documenting and reporting air quality improvements, you help ensure the success of the restoration project and protect the health and safety of everyone involved.

Key takeaways

  • Mould is the most common contaminant after water damage and can begin growing within 24 to 48 hours; bacteria, VOCs and allergens are also key concerns.
  • Keeping relative humidity below 60% helps prevent further mould growth and stabilises the indoor environment.
  • Accurate assessment relies on the right tools: moisture meters and infrared cameras for hidden moisture, air sampling for spores, PIDs for VOCs, and ATP testers for surface bacteria.
  • Effective remediation combines removing contaminated materials, dehumidification, HEPA and activated carbon filtration, negative air pressure containment and ventilation.
  • Thorough before-and-after documentation records the work performed and helps protect the health and safety of everyone involved.

In summary, improving indoor air quality after water damage means identifying contaminants early, assessing conditions with the correct tools, remediating with appropriate techniques, and documenting the results.

courses.title: Introduction to Infrared Thermography for Drying
courses.description: This course aims to provide a basic but comprehensive understanding of thermal energy concepts and infrared thermography for moisture detection and drying in restoration. It covers heat transfer principles, surface temperature dynamics, and emissivity effects, alongside infrared camera technologies and moisture detection applications. Participants will also learn about thermography safety protocols, result analysis, and the integration of thermographic data into remediation strategies for precise drying and moisture management.
courses.shortDescription: Understand how infrared cameras detect moisture and how to read thermal images to guide drying in water damage restoration.

By the end of this course you will be able to:

  • Explain how conduction, convection and radiation move thermal energy, and why cooler surface temperatures indicate higher retained moisture.
  • Choose an infrared camera by weighing sensor resolution, infrared band, qualitative versus quantitative output and physical durability.
  • Use thermography to map saturation extents, verify drying uniformity, locate hidden leak sources and identify insulation voids.
  • Apply cameras safely by following operator protocols, documenting metadata and recognising limitations such as contact barriers and dense materials.
  • Analyse and present findings using software overlays, heatmap animations and complementary tools like moisture meters and hygrometers.

lessons.title: Safely Applying Thermography — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Following Proper Protocols
To ensure the accuracy, safety, and efficacy of moisture camera operation, it is imperative to strictly adhere to established protocols and guidelines. Operator manuals should be meticulously followed to ensure proper sensor calibration durations and to avoid inadvertent laser eye exposures. Additionally, understanding the recommended working distance intervals for the selected focal lengths is crucial for achieving the necessary image clarity for thermographic analysis. By following these established protocols, users can maximise the effectiveness of moisture cameras and obtain reliable data for informed decision-making.

Metadata Documentation
To ensure the admissibility and authenticity of drying advancement pace claims in the event of future litigation, meticulous documentation of all relevant metadata is essential. This includes recording precise scanning distances, sensor resolutions, calibration averages, equipment model specifications, software versions used, and environmental humidity values. Additionally, screen captures should be chronologically embedded within image frame bastions to provide visual evidence of the drying process. This comprehensive documentation will enable the replayability of the drying process at any given point, allowing for accurate verification of drying advancement claims should they be disputed.

Comparing Multiple Scans
When comparing multiple scans, it is crucial to carefully analyse any discrepancies that arise from varying view-finding angles, hot-reflective glares, and emittance variances. These discrepancies can potentially indicate anomalies or inconsistencies that warrant further investigation. To enhance the reliability of the comparison, additional confirmatory flybys may be necessary to gather more comprehensive data and improve overall consistency. If minor discrepancies persist and cannot be readily explained, they should be thoroughly investigated to determine their underlying cause. Verifying these discrepancies is essential to ensure the accuracy and integrity of the scan comparison process.

Understanding Limitations
It is essential to acknowledge and address the limitations inherent in moisture camera technology. Contact barriers, such as vinyl wallpaper, can impede moisture evaporation, while dense materials can attenuate heat, hindering the camera’s ability to penetrate beyond surface readings. Consequently, moisture cameras often provide limited insight into interstitial layers, the spaces between individual components or materials. To overcome these limitations, physical modifications to the environment may be necessary to allow the camera to obtain readings from deeper within the structure. This enhanced access can provide more comprehensive and accurate information about moisture distribution, particularly in areas concealed by contact barriers or dense materials.

Use bundled software tools overlaying quantified thermography imagery atop architectural drawings with moisture sensor pin flags denoting risky humidity holdouts directing desiccant pack intervention placements reaching cavities impractical for active drying equipment to access confined behind walls at each discovery interval through months as restorations continue.

Complementary Techniques

Key takeaways

  • On a thermogram, cooler surface temperatures point to higher retained moisture, and variable emissivity across materials means calibration adjustments are needed for consistent images.
  • Camera selection depends on detector resolution, the correct infrared wavelength band, whether outputs are quantitative, and durability for restoration job sites.
  • Thermography guides drying equipment placement, confirms drying progress against benchmarks, and reveals hidden leaks and insulation voids that plain photos cannot show.
  • Cameras have limits: contact barriers such as vinyl wallpaper and dense materials restrict readings, so thorough metadata documentation and complementary methods (moisture meters, hygrometers, tracer dyes) are essential.

In summary, infrared thermography is a supporting diagnostic tool that, when used correctly and backed by validation, helps direct drying decisions across a restoration project.

courses.title: Introduction to Recovery of Submerged Items and Contents
courses.description:
This course aims to provide a comprehensive framework for evaluating and restoring assets affected by water damage, guiding participants through the critical stages of restoration.

It covers assessing materials’ restorability based on composition, contamination, and damage history, alongside recovery logistics such as safety protocols, specialised equipment, and transport protection.

Additionally, it delves into preservation techniques, including drying conditions, packaging, and monitoring. Finally, it addresses effective cleaning methods and functionality validation, ensuring long-term viability and stability for restored assets.

courses.title: Introduction to Restoration of Antiques and Fine Furnishings
courses.description:
This course aims to provide a basic but comprehensive understanding of the risks and techniques involved in the restoration of antique and delicate furniture exposed to moisture and environmental fluctuations.

Covering material integrity challenges like bonding damage, fungal colonisation, and chemical degradation, it also includes handling precautions, cleaning methods, and restorative techniques.

With a focus on maintaining authenticity, the course emphasises meticulous procedures for preserving historical value, ensuring safe handling, and documenting provenance to uphold both aesthetic and market value.
courses.shortDescription: Understand the risks and methods for restoring moisture-affected antique furniture, covering safe handling, cleaning, and documenting provenance.

  • Apply safe handling precautions when securing, moving and packing fragile antique items after water exposure.
  • Select cleaning techniques that protect original finishes, patina and gilding rather than aggressive solvents or abrasion.
  • Choose restorative methods that preserve structural integrity and authenticity instead of wholesale replacement.
  • Recognise the documentation, provenance and appraisal steps needed to support authentication and valuation.

courses.title: Introduction to Ultraviolet Light and Fluorescence
courses.description:
This course aims to provide a basic but comprehensive understanding of ultraviolet light spectrum applications and safety in water loss inspections.

It covers the properties of UVA, UVB, and UVC light, their fluorescence principles, and the protective measures required for safe use.

Participants will also explore equipment types, moisture detection, microbial growth identification, and complementary technologies such as infrared thermography and moisture mapping to enhance inspection accuracy and efficiency.
courses.shortDescription: Understand how ultraviolet light and fluorescence detect moisture and microbial growth in water loss inspections, and how to use UV equipment safely.

lessons.title: Ultraviolet Light Spectrum — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this course you will be able to:

  • Distinguish the UVA, UVB and UVC bands by their wavelength ranges and their relative hazard and precaution levels.
  • Explain how fluorescence occurs when a material absorbs ultraviolet energy and re-emits it as longer-wavelength visible light.
  • Select appropriate lamps, filters and imaging equipment for inspection tasks, and set brightness to avoid unnecessary overexposure.
  • Apply UV light and dye tracers to trace leaks, indicate porous saturation, verify drying progress and identify microbial growth on water losses.
  • Combine UV inspection with complementary methods such as infrared thermography, moisture mapping, chemical testing and borescope imaging, while following UV safety precautions.

UVA

UVB

UVC

courses.title: Introduction to Water Damage Litigation Support
courses.description:
This course aims to provide a basic but comprehensive understanding of forensic investigations in the restoration industry.
Participants will explore moisture source tracing, material failure analysis, and code compliance assessments. Through data gathering techniques like psychrometric records and sensor mapping, alongside scope analysis and report creation, the course offers insights into legal proceedings, evidence management, and expert testimony preparation, ensuring a thorough approach to restoration case investigations.
courses.shortDescription: Learn how forensic water damage investigation supports litigation—tracing moisture sources, analysing scope, writing reports, and preparing expert testimony.

courses.title: Moisture, Mould, and Indoor Air Quality: Understanding the Link
courses.description:
Welcome to Moisture, Mould, and Indoor Air Quality: Understanding the Link — a comprehensive course designed for professionals in the restoration, building, environmental health, and facilities management industries who are committed to creating healthier indoor environments.

Mould is more than just an unsightly nuisance — it’s a biological contaminant that thrives in moist environments and can have serious consequences for both building materials and occupant health. Understanding the science behind moisture intrusion, mould growth, and their impact on indoor air quality is critical to preventing costly damage and protecting public health.

This course brings together the essential knowledge and tools needed to identify, investigate, and manage moisture and mould in buildings. Whether you’re responding to water damage, improving air quality, or ensuring compliance with industry standards, this training will strengthen your foundation and improve your on-the-job decision-making.

Throughout the course, you’ll explore topics such as:

The biological behaviour of mould spores and their activation by moisture

Common sources of hidden and visible moisture in buildings

How mould impacts air quality, including chemical and biological emissions

High-risk building materials and HVAC system vulnerabilities

Health effects of exposure to mould-contaminated air

Inspection protocols, air sampling, and moisture detection tools

Drying strategies to prevent regrowth after water damage

Safe remediation practices and techniques for air quality recovery

Clear communication and professional documentation practices

This is more than a technical training—it’s a guide to better outcomes for clients, healthier indoor spaces, and professional excellence in the field of restoration and environmental health.

Let’s get started.
courses.shortDescription: Understand how moisture drives mould growth and affects indoor air quality, and how inspection, drying and remediation restore healthy air.

lessons.title: The Moisture-Mould Connection — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this course you will be able to:

  • Explain how moisture activates dormant mould spores and why controlling moisture is the cornerstone of mould prevention and remediation.
  • Identify common visible and hidden moisture sources, and select detection tools such as moisture meters, thermal imaging cameras and hygrometers.
  • Describe how mould degrades indoor air quality through spores, hyphal fragments and microbial volatile organic compounds (MVOCs).
  • Recognise the short-term and long-term health effects of mould exposure and the populations most at risk.
  • Apply drying, remediation and documentation practices that prevent mould regrowth and verify air quality recovery.

The Nature of Mould Spores and Activation

Mould spores are microscopic reproductive units found naturally in the environment—both indoors and outdoors. They are lightweight, resilient, and can easily travel through the air, settling on surfaces throughout buildings. These spores are largely harmless when dry and inactive, existing in a dormant state until the environment becomes hospitable. Moisture is the critical factor that activates these spores, enabling them to germinate and grow.

Once moisture is introduced—whether through leaks, condensation, or high humidity—spores absorb water, triggering cellular processes that allow them to develop into mature mould colonies. This growth can occur rapidly, often within 24 to 48 hours, especially in warm, nutrient-rich conditions.

Sources and Types of Moisture That Encourage Growth

Moisture can infiltrate indoor environments through many routes. Common sources include plumbing leaks, roof leaks, floodwater intrusion, condensation on cold surfaces, and elevated indoor humidity caused by insufficient ventilation or occupant activities such as cooking, bathing, and drying clothes indoors.

Not all moisture is visible to the naked eye. For example, capillary action can draw groundwater up into foundation walls, a process known as rising damp. Condensation can accumulate inside wall cavities, behind insulation, or in ductwork, creating hidden reservoirs of moisture where mould can thrive unnoticed for long periods.

The Impact of Moisture on Mould Growth and Air Quality

Active mould colonies do more than just affect surfaces—they become a persistent source of airborne contamination. As mould grows, it sheds spores and fragments into the air. These particles are microscopic and easily inhaled, posing health risks to building occupants. Additionally, mould releases microbial volatile organic compounds (MVOCs), which contribute to musty odours and chemical irritation.

Because spores can travel through air currents and HVAC systems, mould contamination is rarely limited to the visibly affected areas. The presence of moisture thus indirectly compromises the entire building’s indoor air quality. This is why controlling moisture is the cornerstone of mould prevention and remediation strategies.

lessons.title: Identifying Common Moisture Sources — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Visible Signs of Moisture Intrusion

Water stains on ceilings, walls, or floors are classic indicators of moisture problems. Bubbling or peeling paint, warped or sagging drywall, and visible mould growth all suggest ongoing moisture issues. Leaks around plumbing fixtures, basements with wet floors, and areas near sump pumps or drainage systems are typical hotspots.

Regular inspections for these signs can help catch moisture problems early, preventing mould from gaining a foothold. However, relying solely on visual signs is insufficient, as many moisture issues remain hidden.

Hidden and Subtle Moisture Problems

Many moisture issues are concealed within building assemblies. Water can enter through small cracks in foundations, behind siding, or through poorly sealed window frames. Inside walls, damp insulation or wall cavities can harbour moisture for months or years, feeding hidden mould growth.

HVAC systems are also a common culprit. Condensation can form on cold surfaces like evaporator coils or inside drain pans, causing microbial growth that can disperse spores throughout the building’s ventilation system. Poor ventilation exacerbates these issues by trapping moisture inside the building envelope.

Tools and Technology for Detecting Moisture

Moisture detection requires a combination of specialised equipment and expertise. Moisture meters, including pin and pinless types, measure moisture content within materials, providing immediate data to assess water presence and severity. Thermal imaging cameras detect temperature differences that often correlate with moisture intrusion.

Hygrometers measure the relative humidity (RH) of indoor air. Sustained RH above 60% is highly conducive to mould growth, so monitoring these levels is essential in both detecting existing problems and preventing future issues. When combined with thorough visual inspection, these tools provide a robust strategy for identifying moisture sources before mould spreads.

modules.title: How Mould Affects Indoor Air Quality

lessons.title: How Mould Affects Indoor Air Quality — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Airborne Biological Contaminants From Mould Growth

Mould produces a variety of biological contaminants that impact air quality. These include spores, tiny reproductive structures that become airborne and inhaled, and hyphal fragments—small pieces of mould colonies that break off and become airborne. Both can cause allergic reactions and respiratory irritation.

Some mould species produce mycotoxins—secondary metabolites toxic to humans and animals. Mycotoxins can exacerbate health issues and pose particular risks to vulnerable populations such as children, the elderly, and those with compromised immune systems. While not all moulds produce mycotoxins, their presence adds a layer of complexity and hazard to indoor air quality.

Chemical Byproducts: Microbial Volatile Organic Compounds (MVOCs)

Mould metabolism emits MVOCs, which are responsible for the musty, earthy odours often associated with damp buildings. MVOCs are volatile chemicals that can cause irritation of the eyes, nose, and throat. Prolonged exposure to these compounds may worsen symptoms in sensitive individuals and reduce overall comfort in indoor spaces.

The presence of persistent musty odours is often an important clue indicating hidden mould growth or ongoing moisture problems—even if mould is not visible. Addressing MVOCs usually requires eliminating the source of mould growth and thorough cleaning, not just masking the odours.

Air Quality Impact and Spread Within Buildings

Mould spores and MVOCs do not stay localised; they travel through air currents and ventilation systems, spreading contamination. In poorly maintained HVAC systems, spores can be distributed throughout multiple rooms, exacerbating exposure and making remediation more difficult.

Understanding how mould affects indoor air quality highlights why simply cleaning visible mould is insufficient. Comprehensive remediation includes moisture control, air filtration, and HVAC system assessment to prevent the ongoing spread of contaminants.

lessons.title: Building Materials and Mould Susceptibility — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
High-Risk Materials

Certain materials in buildings are particularly prone to mould growth. These include:

Drywall: Absorbs moisture quickly and provides cellulose for mould to feed on.
Carpet and padding: Trap moisture and organic particles, creating ideal mould habitats.
Insulation: Especially fibreglass and cellulose, which can trap water and support microbial growth.
Timber and particle board: Wood-based products absorb and retain moisture, often leading to decay and structural damage.
Once these materials are saturated and colonised, remediation often requires removal rather than cleaning. Their porous nature means mould grows into the material, not just on the surface.

Resistant Surfaces

Non-porous materials like glass, metal, and certain plastics are less supportive of mould, but they aren’t immune. Dust, dirt, and organic residues on these surfaces can still provide a foothold for microbial growth. Regular cleaning and controlling humidity are essential to keep even these materials mould-free.

In construction and renovation, using moisture-resistant materials in high-risk areas (e.g., cement board in bathrooms) can greatly reduce the potential for future mould issues.

modules.title: The Role of Humidity and HVAC Systems

lessons.title: The Role of Humidity and HVAC Systems — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Understanding Relative Humidity and Its Impact on Mould Growth

Relative humidity (RH) measures the amount of moisture in the air relative to the maximum it can hold at a given temperature. It plays a critical role in determining whether mould can thrive indoors. When indoor RH consistently exceeds 60%, conditions become highly favourable for mould colonisation. At these levels, moisture readily condenses on cooler surfaces, saturating porous materials such as drywall, wood, and carpet—creating ideal environments for fungal growth.

Maintaining indoor RH between 30% and 50% is widely recommended to limit mould proliferation and maintain occupant comfort. However, achieving and sustaining this balance can be challenging in climates with high outdoor humidity or in buildings with poor ventilation or water intrusion issues.

Everyday Activities and Their Influence on Indoor Humidity

Everyday activities within a building can significantly increase indoor humidity levels, often unnoticed. Cooking, showering, boiling water, drying clothes indoors, and even breathing all release water vapour into the air. In tightly sealed, energy-efficient buildings, this moisture can accumulate rapidly if ventilation is insufficient.

Without proper airflow or exhaust systems, humidity can rise throughout a home or commercial space, particularly in bathrooms, kitchens, and laundry areas. These are common hotspots for mould growth due to their moisture-generating activities. Understanding how occupant behaviour affects humidity levels is vital for managing indoor air quality.

HVAC Systems: Double-Edged Swords for Moisture and Mould Control

Heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC) systems have a complex relationship with indoor moisture and mould. On the one hand, properly designed and maintained HVAC systems help regulate temperature, control humidity, and filter airborne contaminants. On the other hand, poorly maintained or improperly installed systems can exacerbate moisture problems and contribute to mould spread.

Issues such as clogged or dirty filters, blocked drain pans, and leaky ductwork can cause condensation and microbial growth within HVAC components. For example, evaporator coils that become excessively cold can accumulate condensation, providing a breeding ground for mould. This contamination can then be dispersed throughout the building, negatively impacting air quality.

Strategies for Controlling Humidity Through HVAC Maintenance and Design

Regular HVAC maintenance is crucial to controlling moisture levels indoors. Key strategies include:

Routine filter replacement: Dirty filters reduce airflow, cause system strain, and can become reservoirs for dust and microbes.
Cleaning coils and drain pans: Keeping these components clean prevents water accumulation and microbial growth.
Ensuring proper drainage: Drain pans and condensate lines must be clear and functional to remove moisture efficiently.
Balancing ventilation: Properly designed ventilation introduces fresh air and exhausts moist indoor air, preventing humidity buildup.
Incorporating dehumidification: In humid climates or buildings prone to moisture problems, integrating dehumidifiers with HVAC systems helps maintain RH within safe levels.
The Role of HVAC Filters and Air Quality

HVAC filters play a significant role in trapping airborne particles, including mould spores. High-efficiency particulate air (HEPA) filters or filters with a Minimum Efficiency Reporting Value (MERV) rating appropriate for the building can reduce the concentration of biological contaminants in circulated air.

Selecting and maintaining appropriate filtration media ensures that spores and other allergens are captured before air is distributed, improving overall indoor air quality. However, filters must be replaced regularly to maintain effectiveness and avoid becoming a source of contamination themselves.

lessons.title: Health Effects of Mould Exposure — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Understanding the Risks

Mould affects indoor air quality in ways that have direct and sometimes severe consequences for human health. These health effects range from minor irritation to chronic illness, depending on an individual’s sensitivity and the level of exposure. Mould spores, once airborne, can be inhaled deep into the lungs or come into contact with eyes and skin.

Short-Term and Long-Term Symptoms

Short-term effects can include symptoms such as sneezing, coughing, itchy or watery eyes, skin rashes, and nasal congestion. These are often mistaken for seasonal allergies, but if symptoms improve when the person leaves the environment, mould may be the underlying cause.

More prolonged or repeated exposure can lead to chronic respiratory conditions, including asthma and bronchitis. In severe cases—particularly for those with weakened immune systems—exposure to mould can result in fungal infections that affect the lungs or other organs.

High-Risk Populations

Some groups are more susceptible to the effects of mould exposure:

Infants and young children
Elderly individuals
People with asthma or respiratory illnesses
Immunocompromised individuals (e.g., cancer patients or transplant recipients)
For these populations, maintaining excellent indoor air quality is not just a matter of comfort but a critical aspect of health protection.

lessons.title: Inspection Protocols and Air Sampling — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Conducting a Thorough Visual Inspection

Effective mould remediation begins with a comprehensive inspection. This step helps identify both visible signs of mould and potential sources of moisture that may not be immediately apparent. Technicians should look for:

Staining or discolouration on ceilings, walls, and floors
Warped or swollen materials
Peeling paint or wallpaper
Persistent musty odours
Particular attention should be paid to areas with limited ventilation, such as closets, behind furniture, and within HVAC components.

Air Sampling Techniques and Interpretation

Air sampling is used to determine whether mould is present in the air at elevated levels. This process involves drawing air through specialised cassettes that capture spores for lab analysis. The results are compared against outdoor air samples to determine if mould levels inside the building are abnormally high.

It’s important to understand that not all mould problems require air sampling. In many cases, visible mould is sufficient to warrant remediation. However, sampling is especially helpful in real estate transactions, legal cases, or when there’s suspicion of hidden mould.

Results must be interpreted by a qualified professional, as high spore counts do not always correlate with health complaints, and low counts don’t guarantee a clean environment. The type, amount, and pattern of spores are all key to drawing conclusions.

modules.title: Drying Strategies to Prevent Mould Regrowth

lessons.title: Drying Strategies to Prevent Mould Regrowth — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Drying as a First Line of Defence

Drying is the most important and time-sensitive component of any water damage response. The quicker moisture is removed from materials and the air, the lower the chance for mould to grow. Restoration professionals must understand the science behind drying to carry it out effectively.

Effective drying involves reducing the moisture content of materials to acceptable levels. This typically includes the use of air movers to encourage evaporation, dehumidifiers to remove water from the air, and heat to increase the rate of evaporation. The balance of these elements is key.

Monitoring Progress and Verifying Results

Throughout the drying process, technicians must monitor:

Moisture levels in affected materials
Temperature and relative humidity
Equipment performance
Tools such as non-invasive moisture meters, thermal hygrometers, and infrared cameras help track drying progress. Documentation of these readings is crucial for verifying that materials are dry and the environment is stable.

Drying must be continued until moisture levels reach acceptable industry standards. Removing equipment too early can allow materials to reabsorb moisture or allow dormant mould spores to reactivate.

modules.title: Remediation Approaches and Air Quality Recovery

lessons.title: Remediation Approaches and Air Quality Recovery — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Removing Affected Materials Safely

When materials are extensively contaminated or cannot be dried effectively, removal is often the only option. This includes drywall, carpeting, insulation, and ceiling tiles. These items must be carefully bagged and disposed of to prevent the spread of spores.

Proper containment using plastic sheeting, zip walls, and negative air machines is essential during removal. These measures protect clean areas from cross-contamination and maintain safe air quality throughout the process.

Cleaning and Air Filtration

After removal, all surfaces in the affected area must be thoroughly cleaned using HEPA vacuuming, damp wiping with antimicrobial solutions, and air scrubbing. AFDs (air filtration devices) are used during and after remediation to capture airborne particles.

Technicians may also use encapsulants—special coatings designed to seal surfaces and prevent residual spores from becoming airborne. However, encapsulants should only be applied after thorough cleaning and drying have been verified.

Final Clearance and Air Quality Verification

Once remediation is complete, clearance testing is often performed to verify that spore counts have returned to normal. This may involve:

Post-remediation air sampling
Surface sampling
Visual inspection for residual mould
This final step confirms that the air is clean, the moisture problem is resolved, and the space is safe for reoccupation.

lessons.title: Communication and Documentation — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
The Importance of Clear Communication

Clients may not always understand the dangers of mould or the need for extensive remediation. It’s essential to explain the reasons for your actions in terms they can relate to—emphasising health, property value, and peace of mind.

Visual aids such as moisture maps, infrared images, and air sampling reports can help convey the severity of the issue. Answering questions clearly and confidently helps build trust.

Professional Documentation

A well-documented project is essential for insurance claims, legal protection, and client satisfaction. Good documentation should include:

Before and after photos
Moisture readings and drying logs
Air sampling reports (if conducted)
Equipment usage logs and placement maps
Work authorisation and signed remediation plans
Proper documentation is more than just paperwork—it reflects the professionalism and thoroughness of your work.

Educating for Prevention

Finally, take time to educate clients on how to prevent future mould growth. This includes tips on ventilation, dehumidifier use, regular maintenance, and prompt response to water intrusion.

By closing the project with clear advice and documentation, you position yourself as a trusted expert and improve long-term outcomes for the client.

Key takeaways

  • Moisture is the trigger. Dormant spores can develop into mature colonies within 24 to 48 hours in warm, nutrient-rich conditions, so moisture control is the priority.
  • Manage humidity. Keeping indoor relative humidity between 30% and 50% limits growth, while sustained relative humidity above 60% is highly conducive to mould.
  • Porous materials are high risk. Drywall, carpet, insulation and timber often must be removed rather than cleaned once colonised, because mould grows into the material.
  • Remediation is more than cleaning. Effective work combines containment, HEPA-based cleaning, air filtration and drying, followed by clearance verification before reoccupation.
  • Communicate and document. Clear explanations and thorough records — photos, moisture logs, drying logs and any sampling reports — protect the client and evidence professional work.

In summary, understanding the link between moisture, mould and indoor air quality means finding and removing moisture early, controlling humidity, remediating contaminated materials safely, and verifying and documenting a healthy indoor environment.

courses.title: Using Air Scrubbers and AFDs to Improve Job Site Air Quality
courses.description:
Welcome to Using Air Scrubbers and AFDs to Improve Job Site Air Quality — a practical, in-depth course designed to equip restoration professionals, contractors, and environmental technicians with the knowledge and skills to effectively manage airborne contaminants on job sites.

Indoor air quality is a critical factor in restoration, remediation, and construction projects. Whether you’re addressing mould contamination, fire and smoke damage, asbestos, or general dust and debris, controlling the air environment is essential for health, safety, and compliance.

Air Filtration Devices (AFDs), commonly known as air scrubbers, are the front line in protecting workers and building occupants from airborne hazards. This course demystifies how they work, when and where to use them, and how to get the most out of these essential tools.

In this course, you’ll learn how to:

Understand the components, mechanics, and modes of operation of air scrubbers

Choose and manage filters (HEPA, carbon, pre-filters) for different job scenarios

Set up units effectively on site for both recirculation and negative pressure

Calculate Air Changes per Hour (ACH) to meet air quality standards

Maintain equipment and manage filters for long-term performance

Ensure safety, compliance, and proper documentation throughout every project

Communicate the value of clean air strategies to clients and stakeholders

This is more than just an equipment overview — it’s a course that builds confidence and competency in using AFDs to improve job performance, protect health, and meet regulatory demands.

By the end of the training, you’ll not only understand how to operate air scrubbers effectively — you’ll be able to justify their use with measurable results and communicate your process clearly to clients and insurers alike.

Let’s get started and breathe cleaner, safer air into every job site.
courses.shortDescription: Set up, operate, and maintain air filtration devices (air scrubbers) to control airborne contaminants and verify air quality on job sites.

modules.title: Introduction to Air Filtration Devices (AFDs) and Air Scrubbers

lessons.title: Introduction to Air Filtration Devices (AFDs) and Air Scrubbers — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this course you will be able to:

  • Identify the main components of an air scrubber — pre-filter, HEPA filter, blower and housing — and describe how each stage helps remove airborne contaminants.
  • Distinguish between recirculation and negative pressure modes and select the correct mode for continuous air cleaning or for containment.
  • Calculate Air Changes per Hour (ACH) from the airflow rate and room volume, and apply the target ACH ranges for different project types.
  • Position units and set up ducting to maximise airflow across the contaminated area and maintain an effective containment.
  • Maintain filters, monitor performance with instruments, and document your work to support compliance and clear client communication.

Understanding AFDs and Air Scrubbers

Air Filtration Devices (AFDs), commonly referred to as air scrubbers, are essential tools in the cleaning, restoration, and construction industries. These devices are designed to remove contaminants from the air, making the indoor environment safer for both workers and occupants. They function by drawing in air, filtering it through various filtration stages, and then releasing clean air either back into the space or outside the structure.

Air scrubbers are widely used during remediation efforts involving mould, asbestos, lead dust, or general construction dust. Their importance has grown as awareness of airborne health hazards has increased. Beyond particle removal, they also help reduce odours and chemical vapours when equipped with the right filters.

The Role of AFDs in Job Site Safety

Maintaining clean air on job sites is not only a matter of comfort but of health and regulatory compliance. Contaminants such as fine dust, mould spores, or off-gassed chemicals can pose serious respiratory risks. Prolonged exposure can lead to health complications for workers, and may also result in legal or financial repercussions for companies if safety standards are not met. Deploying AFDs demonstrates a proactive approach to occupational health and can enhance worker productivity by providing a cleaner, safer environment.

When and Where AFDs Are Needed

AFDs are necessary in any environment where airborne contaminants may be present. These include post-flood environments where mould can flourish, fire restoration projects where smoke particles linger, and during renovations or demolitions where building materials are disturbed. AFDs also play a critical role in sensitive environments such as healthcare settings or clean rooms, where even low levels of airborne contaminants can pose serious risks.

lessons.title: Components and Mechanics of AFDs — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
The Inner Workings of an Air Scrubber

An air scrubber is made up of several integral parts that work together to purify air. The process begins at the air intake, where contaminated air is drawn into the machine. As the air moves through the unit, it encounters a series of filters. The first filter, often called the pre-filter, is designed to catch larger particles like dust and hair. This protects the more sensitive filters downstream and prolongs their lifespan.

Next is the HEPA filter, which is the core filtration component. High-Efficiency Particulate Air (HEPA) filters are capable of capturing 99.97% of airborne particles that are 0.3 microns or larger. This includes many harmful contaminants such as mould spores, bacteria, and fine dust. Some units may also include an activated carbon filter to address odours and volatile organic compounds (VOCs).

Air Movement and Design

The blower or fan is another crucial component, responsible for moving air through the device and ensuring consistent airflow. The housing unit, usually made from durable plastic or metal, keeps all internal parts secure and ensures safe operation. Understanding how these parts function together helps technicians operate and troubleshoot air scrubbers effectively on the job.

Built-in Safety and Efficiency Features

Modern AFDs often include features that enhance usability and safety. These might include variable speed settings, filter change indicators, and built-in hour meters. These features not only help in maintaining efficiency but also ensure that the devices are being used correctly. Units may also include protective grilles to prevent contact with the fan or internal components, and ergonomic handles or wheels for easier transport.

modules.title: Types of Filters and Their Functions

lessons.title: Types of Filters and Their Functions — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Multi-Stage Filtration Strategy

Effective air scrubbing depends on the use of multiple filtration stages, each with a distinct purpose. The first layer, the pre-filter, plays a foundational role by capturing larger debris and reducing the particle load that reaches the finer filters. Pre-filters are usually inexpensive and require frequent replacement, sometimes daily, depending on job site conditions.

The HEPA filter, located after the pre-filter, is responsible for capturing the most dangerous fine particles. Its tight mesh construction traps microscopic contaminants that are invisible to the naked eye. These filters are indispensable on job sites involving mould remediation, fire restoration, or lead dust.

Additional Filter Options

Some air scrubbers are equipped with additional filters designed for specific tasks. Activated carbon filters absorb VOCs and neutralise strong odours, making them ideal for chemical spills or smoke-damaged buildings. In more advanced models, there may be optional UV light features for microbial deactivation, or electrostatic filters to help trap charged particles. Understanding the options available allows technicians to select the right filter combination for each specific task.

Filter Performance and Ratings

It’s important to understand how filters are rated for performance. HEPA filters are standardised to meet specific efficiency thresholds, but other filters may vary in quality and durability. Understanding MERV ratings (Minimum Efficiency Reporting Value) can help in evaluating non-HEPA filters. Higher MERV ratings indicate a filter’s ability to capture smaller particles, although not all MERV-rated filters are suitable for use in AFDs. Always consult the manufacturer’s specifications.

modules.title: Modes of Operation: Recirculation vs Negative Pressure

lessons.title: Modes of Operation: Recirculation vs Negative Pressure — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Recirculation Mode Explained

In recirculation mode, an AFD pulls air from the room, filters it, and then discharges the clean air back into the same space. This mode is particularly useful when you need to continuously purify the air in an enclosed environment, such as during sanding, demolition, or cleaning activities. Recirculation helps reduce overall particulate concentrations but does not prevent contaminants from moving to adjacent areas.

This mode is especially helpful during ongoing work where dust and fine debris are continuously being generated. Recirculating the air through the scrubber allows for continuous removal of airborne particles and contributes to a more comfortable working environment. However, it is important to note that recirculation does not control the direction of airflow in a room, and should not be relied on for containment.

Establishing Negative Pressure

Negative pressure mode is used when the goal is containment. By venting filtered air to the outside, the AFD creates a pressure differential between the workspace and surrounding areas. This causes air to flow into the work area rather than allowing contaminated air to escape. Negative pressure is essential during mould remediation, asbestos abatement, or infectious disease control.

Creating an effective negative pressure environment requires more than just attaching a duct to the exhaust. Seals around doorways, windows, and barriers must be airtight to prevent leaks. The airflow rate must be sufficient to maintain the pressure difference, and continuous monitoring is needed to confirm that the system is working correctly.

Making the Right Choice for Your Job

Choosing between these modes depends on the specific risks present and the need for containment. In many cases, jobs begin with recirculation and transition to negative pressure once containment barriers are in place. Understanding when and how to use each mode is a key competency for any technician working in air quality or restoration.

modules.title: Proper Setup and Placement on the Job Site

lessons.title: Proper Setup and Placement on the Job Site — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Strategic Positioning of Units

Effective use of AFDs requires thoughtful placement. Air scrubbers should be positioned to maximise airflow across the contaminated area. Avoid placing units directly against walls or in corners where airflow is restricted. Ideally, the intake side should face the area of highest contamination, while the clean air exhaust should be directed away from workers.

Understanding the layout of the space and the workflow of the project is essential. Airflow should be managed in a way that contaminants are drawn away from occupied zones or toward containment barriers. Sometimes multiple AFDs are needed in larger spaces or complex layouts. The goal is to create a clean air pathway that continuously moves contaminated air toward the scrubber and away from sensitive areas.

Use of Ducting for Ventilation

When negative pressure is required, ducting becomes essential. Ducts allow filtered air to be safely vented outside the containment zone. Proper ducting setup involves securely attaching the duct to the exhaust port and sealing all joints to prevent leaks. The length and diameter of the ducting can impact airflow, so choosing the right size is important.

Sharp bends or excessive lengths can reduce efficiency and create back pressure, which affects the performance of the AFD. Ducting should be inspected regularly to ensure it remains clear and properly connected. In some cases, it may be necessary to route ducting through windows, access hatches, or temporary barriers.

Safety and Practical Considerations

On active job sites, it’s crucial to prevent the air scrubber from becoming a hazard itself. Place units on stable, level surfaces and use caution signage to keep pathways clear. Ensure that electrical cords do not create tripping hazards, and avoid areas where water may pose a shock risk.

In high-traffic environments, consider using barricades or placing the AFD behind existing structures to avoid accidental damage. Protecting the unit also ensures continued operation and prevents delays that can arise from equipment failure.

lessons.title: Calculating Air Changes per Hour (ACH) — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
What is ACH and Why Does It Matter?

Air Changes per Hour (ACH) is a metric used to determine how many times the air in a given space is completely replaced with filtered air within one hour. It’s a foundational concept in indoor air quality management, especially when using AFDs or air scrubbers in restoration and remediation work.

Higher ACH rates mean that airborne contaminants are being removed more frequently, which reduces exposure for occupants and speeds up cleaning and remediation. Understanding and calculating ACH ensures you’re using your air scrubbers effectively.

How to Calculate ACH

To determine ACH, you need two pieces of information: the airflow rate (in cubic feet per minute or CFM) of your air scrubber, and the volume of the room in cubic feet.

Use the formula: ACH = (CFM × 60) / Room Volume (in cubic feet)

For example, if your scrubber delivers 500 CFM and your room is 10,000 cubic feet: (500 × 60) / 10,000 = 3 ACH

This tells you the air in the room is being fully replaced three times per hour.

Target ACH Rates by Project Type

Different types of jobs require different ACH goals:

General construction or cleaning: 2–4 ACH
Mould remediation: 4–6 ACH
Asbestos abatement or infection control: 6–12 ACH
Real-World Application

Accurately calculating ACH helps justify equipment use, ensures compliance with industry guidelines (e.g., IICRC S520), and provides clients with measurable results. It also helps identify if additional units are needed to meet air quality targets

lessons.title: Maintenance and Filter Management — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Importance of Routine Maintenance

Maintaining your AFDs is crucial to ensure they perform at their highest capacity throughout the duration of each job. Regular maintenance helps prevent filter overload, protects internal components, and reduces the risk of recirculating contaminated air. A poorly maintained air scrubber can become ineffective—or worse, it can become a source of contamination itself.

Proper maintenance not only protects workers and clients, but also reduces operating costs over time. Early detection of wear, tear, or clogging allows for timely intervention and extends the unit’s life span.

Filter Management: Lifespan and Replacement Guidelines

AFDs generally utilise a multi-stage filtration system:

Pre-filters: Designed to trap large debris and prolong the life of the HEPA filter. These filters are relatively inexpensive and often need to be replaced daily on dusty sites.
HEPA filters: These high-efficiency filters require careful monitoring. While they can last multiple jobs, they should be replaced when airflow noticeably drops, contamination persists, or manufacturer timelines recommend it.
Carbon filters (if used): These help reduce odours and VOCs. Their lifespan depends on the type and intensity of contaminants being captured.
Signs a filter may need replacing include:

Visible soiling or discolouration
Reduced suction or airflow
Changes in pressure readings
Persistent odours or air quality complaints
Cleaning Procedures and Storage Protocol

To prevent cross-contamination between job sites, units must be thoroughly cleaned after each use. Turn off and unplug the unit. Remove filters (with PPE) and seal them in plastic for proper disposal. Disinfect the exterior and interior components using a suitable antimicrobial solution. Pay close attention to fan blades, housing surfaces, and corners where dust tends to accumulate.

After cleaning, allow the unit to dry completely before storage. Store AFDs in a dry, temperature-controlled area to prevent moisture buildup or microbial growth. Units should be stacked or shelved carefully to avoid structural damage.

Record-Keeping and Scheduling

Keeping a detailed log of filter changes, maintenance activities, and unit usage is a best practice. This helps with warranty tracking, ensures accountability among technicians, and assists in providing documentation for clients or regulators.

Sample maintenance log entries may include:

Date and time of filter change
Job site location
Name of technician performing maintenance
Observed issues or repairs completed
Proper maintenance habits directly impact performance and compliance, and they reflect the professionalism of your team.

lessons.title: Safety Considerations and Compliance — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
AFDs and Worker Safety

Air scrubbers contribute to a safer worksite, but they must be integrated into a broader health and safety strategy. Technicians must not assume that the presence of an AFD eliminates all airborne hazards. Appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE)—including N95 or P100 respirators, gloves, goggles, and disposable coveralls—must still be worn, especially during demolition or cleanup involving hazardous materials.

Understanding the limits of an AFD is vital. While effective at particle removal, AFDs do not eliminate all risks, such as direct contact with mould or chemical residues. They should be viewed as part of a layered protection plan.

Electrical Safety Precautions

Given that AFDs are powered devices often used in high-moisture environments, electrical safety is critical. Units should be connected only to grounded outlets. Extension cords, if used, must be heavy-duty and rated for the amperage draw of the equipment.

Key safety measures include:

Inspecting cords before each use for frays, exposed wires, or loose plugs
Never operating units near standing water
Avoiding daisy-chaining multiple devices on a single outlet or power board
During long jobs, cables should be secured to prevent tripping hazards. Using cord covers or safety cones is recommended on high-traffic job sites.

Understanding and Meeting Compliance Requirements

Different restoration scenarios are subject to distinct regulatory and procedural standards. Technicians must be familiar with the governing bodies and standards that apply to their specific project type.

Examples include:

IICRC S520: Standards for mould remediation projects
Safe Work Australia / state WHS regulator and EPA requirements: For asbestos abatement and lead removal
State or local health regulations: For healthcare facility construction or hazardous material handling
Failure to comply can result in fines, shutdowns, or legal action. More importantly, it places the health of workers and building occupants at risk.

Documentation and Liability Protection

Documentation plays a vital role in demonstrating regulatory compliance and best practices. Maintain detailed records of:

Equipment used, including model and serial numbers
Filter installation and replacement dates
Negative pressure monitoring logs
Safety inspections or incident reports
These records are often required in insurance claims or third-party audits and provide crucial legal protection for your company.

Maintaining a culture of safety around AFDs not only protects lives but also strengthens your brand’s reputation as a responsible and knowledgeable service provider.

lessons.title: Monitoring Effectiveness on Site — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Verifying Performance with Instruments

The effectiveness of air scrubbers should be measured using tools, not assumptions. Particle counters can detect concentrations of PM10 and PM2.5 in real time, showing a clear before-and-after comparison.

Manometers or pressure gauges verify that negative pressure has been achieved in a containment setup. Aim for a pressure differential of -5 Pa (-0.02 inches of water column) or greater.

Visual and Practical Observations

Technicians should also look for visual cues:

Reduced visible dust and haze in the air
Reduced musty or chemical odours
Quieter fan noise indicating reduced airflow (potential sign of clogged filters)
Adjustments and Documentation

If the unit’s impact is lower than expected:

Check for obstructions at the intake or exhaust
Replace pre-filters
Ensure containment seals are intact
All findings should be documented in the job report, including:

Initial air quality levels
Time of activation
ACH calculations
Final readings or conditions

modules.title: Communicating with Clients and Documenting Your Work

lessons.title: Communicating with Clients and Documenting Your Work — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Explaining the Role of AFDs

Clients may not immediately understand the purpose of a large, noisy machine in their space. Clear communication is key. Explain that the AFD:

Removes harmful particles and improves air quality
Helps speed up remediation or drying
Contributes to safety and health compliance
Providing Transparent Reports

A thorough air scrubber log should include:

Date of deployment
Filter types used and change dates
ACH target and actual readings
Photos of setup (if needed for insurance or documentation)
These records demonstrate professionalism, support billing, and provide a layer of protection in the event of disputes.

Building Trust and Long-Term Relationships

Taking the time to show clients the equipment, walk them through air quality readings, and explain your reasoning for placement or pressure targets builds credibility. Clients are more likely to recommend and rehire contractors who offer transparency, data-driven insights, and visible efforts to protect their health.

Key takeaways

  • HEPA filters capture 99.97% of airborne particles 0.3 microns or larger, while an inexpensive pre-filter traps larger debris and extends the HEPA filter's life.
  • Recirculation mode cleans the air within a space but does not contain contaminants; negative pressure mode vents filtered air outside to create a pressure differential and hold containment.
  • ACH is calculated as (CFM × 60) / room volume, and higher-risk work such as asbestos abatement or infection control targets 6–12 ACH.
  • Effectiveness should be measured with instruments — particle counters for PM10 and PM2.5, and a manometer to confirm a pressure differential of -5 Pa or greater — not assumptions.
  • Routine maintenance, timely filter replacement, and detailed record-keeping keep units effective and support regulatory compliance.

In summary, choosing the right mode, sizing airflow to the required ACH, placing units well, and verifying and documenting results are the core skills for using AFDs to improve job site air quality.

courses.title: Water Damage Restoration — Essentials
courses.description: A concise, CEC-accredited introduction to water damage restoration fundamentals: categories of water, classes of loss, safety, and the core drying workflow.
courses.shortDescription: Introduces the fundamentals of water damage restoration: categories of water, classes of loss, safety, and the core drying workflow.

lessons.title: Categories and Classes of Water Damage — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this course you will be able to:

  • Explain why water damage restoration begins with correctly classifying the loss.
  • Describe how water is grouped into three categories according to contamination level.
  • Describe how affected materials are grouped into classes according to the rate of evaporation required.
  • Explain how these distinctions guide safe handling, drying strategy, and documentation throughout a job.

Water damage restoration begins with correctly classifying the loss. Water is grouped into three categories by contamination level and the affected materials into classes by the rate of evaporation required. Understanding these distinctions guides safe handling, drying strategy, and documentation throughout the job.
