Key takeaways

In summary, correctly classifying a water loss is the foundation of a safe, well-documented restoration job.

  • Water damage restoration begins with correctly classifying the loss.
  • Water is grouped into three categories by contamination level.
  • Affected materials are grouped into classes by the rate of evaporation required.
  • These distinctions guide safe handling, drying strategy, and documentation throughout the job.

lessons.title: Orientation and safe work on loss sites
lessons.contentBody:
Professional scope

Before any drying plan is written, technicians must establish a safe, controlled work zone. This module frames how CARSI aligns practical field procedures with widely recognised industry expectations used in Australia and internationally.

You will treat every site as a dynamic environment: hazards change as materials are disturbed, services are capped, and equipment is introduced.

  • Identify slip/trip risks, electrical hazards, and compromised structures before work begins.
  • Use appropriate PPE for Category 1–3 conditions as indicated by assessment (gloves, respirators where required, eye protection).
  • Communicate clearly with occupants about access limits, noise, and equipment placement.

Documentation mindset

Accurate notes and photographs support insurance review, dispute resolution, and defensible technical decisions. Record moisture readings, equipment serials, and changes to the drying strategy as the project evolves.

lessons.title: Understanding contamination and porous assemblies
lessons.contentBody:
Categories of water

Industry training commonly classifies water by contamination potential. Clean water can degrade as it contacts soils, waste systems, or exterior flood sources. Your assessment determines what may be dried in place versus removed.

  • Anticipate capillary rise in gypsum, timber framing, and insulation cavities.
  • Recognise when carpet underlay or porous trim must be removed to protect indoor air quality.

lessons.title: Systematic inspection and baseline readings
lessons.contentBody:
Inspection workflow

Start wide, then narrow: building envelope, services, adjoining rooms, and subfloor voids. Use a combination of visual cues, non-penetrating meters, and targeted probing where appropriate.

Sketch or digitise the floor plan with wet zones, dry standards, and equipment locations — this becomes the backbone of your drying log.

lessons.title: Removing liquid water efficiently
lessons.contentBody:
Extraction first

Evaporation is slower and more energy-intensive than physical removal. On many losses, improving extraction from carpet and pad (where salvageable) directly reduces drying time and energy cost.

  • Match wand or ride-on tools to pile type and salvage goals.
  • Document when pad is extracted versus removed.

lessons.title: Balancing airflow and grain depression
lessons.contentBody:
The drying triangle

Increase evaporation rate with controlled airflow to wet surfaces, remove moisture from air via dehumidification, and manage temperature within equipment limits and comfort/safety constraints.

lessons.title: Air movers, LGRs, and heat — when and why
lessons.contentBody:
Practical placement

Position air movers to create laminar flow across wet materials. Place low-grain-refrigerant dehumidifiers to process the airstream you are conditioning, not short-circuiting outdoor air unless intentional.

lessons.title: Reading conditions and adjusting the plan
lessons.contentBody:
What to log

Track temperature and relative humidity at least daily at representative points: affected rooms, unaffected reference, and dehumidifier discharge where useful. Compare to drying goals rather than guessing completion.

lessons.title: Defensible close-out
lessons.contentBody:
Sign-off

Define “dry” against material-specific targets and document equipment removal, final readings, and any recommendations for third-party trades (e.g., electrical, cabinetry).

Professional reporting reduces callbacks and supports transparent relationships with insurers and property owners.

courses.title: Commercial Floor Care for Schools & Childcare Contracts
courses.description:
Schools and childcare centres are among the most demanding floor-care environments in Australia: the work is done around children, under tight occupancy and term-break windows, and against real compliance expectations. This course takes an experienced cleaner or small cleaning-business owner from technique to a contract-ready service across the four jobs that win this work — carpet cleaning, tile and grout, vinyl stripping and sealing, and pressure washing.

Each service area is paired with the Australian regulatory frame that a school or childcare contract assumes you already understand: your WHS duties and Safety Data Sheet (SDS) obligations, child-safe and low-tox product selection, verifying slip resistance after sealing (AS 4663), keeping pressure-wash run-off out of stormwater, and the scheduling and documentation a contract expects. Product and chemical choices are grounded in a real Australian catalogue with current SDS, so what you learn maps to what you actually buy and use.

This is professional development and contract-readiness training. It supports your judgement — it does not replace Safety Data Sheets, manufacturer technical data, Australian Standards, or your own qualified assessment of each site.
courses.shortDescription: Carpet, tile & grout, vinyl strip & seal and pressure washing for Australian school and childcare contracts — technique plus WHS/SDS, child-safety, slip resistance and run-off compliance.

meta.audience: Australian commercial cleaning operators/technicians and small business owners servicing schools & childcare
meta.positioning: Professional development / contract-readiness — not IICRC CEC accredited

lessons.title: Welcome: winning and keeping school & childcare floor-care work
lessons.contentBody:
Welcome aboard. This course is built for Australian commercial cleaning operators, technicians and small cleaning-business owners who want to win and reliably service floor-care contracts in schools and childcare centres. You may already have plenty of hands-on cleaning experience. What this course adds is the compliance, child-safety and documentation layer that separates a casual job from a contract a client keeps renewing.

Across the course we work through four jobs that make up the bulk of school and childcare floor care:

  • Carpet cleaning — extraction and low-moisture methods in occupied rooms.
  • Tile & grout cleaning — including slip-resistance considerations on finished floors.
  • Vinyl strip & seal — stripping resilient floors and applying sealer/finish coats.
  • Pressure washing — hard outdoor surfaces, with run-off controlled so it never reaches stormwater.

Here is the thesis that runs through every module. Most floor-care training teaches technique. The contract value in schools and childcare is technique + compliance + child-safety + documentation. A school or childcare client is not just buying a clean floor. They are buying a contractor who can show current Safety Data Sheets, choose lower-tox products, keep children away from wet or treated areas, and produce the paperwork an audit or a parent question might demand. Get those four things right and you do not just win work — you keep it.

So we pair each of the four service areas with the Australian regulatory frame around it: work health and safety and SDS duties, slip resistance, wastewater run-off, and the childcare and school standards that sit over the top. We ground product and chemical choices in a real Australian catalogue with current SDS, so when we name a stripper or a prespray we point you at the actual SDS code to read before you use it.

A few expectations up front. This is professional development and contract-readiness training. It is not IICRC CEC accredited and it does not award IICRC Continuing Education Credits (CEC). Where we reference an industry standard such as IICRC S100 for carpet, treat it as a recognised standard of care — guidance that supports your judgement — not as law. This course supports your decisions; it does not replace a product's SDS or TDS, the relevant standards, a qualified professional, or a site's own rules.

You will also notice we are deliberately careful with numbers. We will not give you fixed dry, cure or re-entry times, and we will not quote specific slip-rating values. Those depend on the exact product or the exact location, so we send you to the right source every time: the product SDS/TDS, or the relevant standard. That discipline is itself part of looking professional to a client.

Module 0 lays the foundation everything else builds on: your WHS duties around hazardous chemicals, how to read a Safety Data Sheet and build a chemicals register, and how to choose child-safe, low-tox products for occupied rooms. Work through the lessons in order, and finish with a short knowledge check. By the end you will understand why the documentation matters, not just that it does — and that understanding is what makes the rest of the course land. Let us get started.

resources.label: The four floor-care jobs — infographic (SVG download)

lessons.title: Your WHS duties: current SDS, the chemicals register & labelling
lessons.contentBody:
Floor-care work means handling hazardous chemicals — strippers, presprays, spotters, sealers. With those products come legal duties under work health and safety law. This lesson sets out what those duties are, so you know what a school or childcare contract expects you to have in place before you ever open a container.

First, the framework itself. Australia has a model WHS Act and Regulations (Safe Work Australia, 2011), but the model laws only have legal effect once each state, territory or the Commonwealth adopts them. Most jurisdictions have. The big exception to flag is Victoria, which operates under its own OHS Act and Regulations, not the model WHS framework. The duties are broadly similar, but the wording and references differ — so for a VIC contract, work to OHS and check with WorkSafe Victoria. Wherever you are, direct yourself to your state regulator (for example SafeWork NSW, WorkSafe Victoria, or Workplace Health and Safety Queensland) for the version that actually applies to you.

Now the core duties. If you are a person conducting a business or undertaking (a PCBU) and you use, handle or store hazardous chemicals, you must:

  • Obtain and keep a current SDS for each hazardous chemical, and keep it accessible to workers at the workplace. A Safety Data Sheet is the manufacturer's document describing the chemical's hazards and safe handling.
  • Make sure each SDS is current. SDS must be reviewed and, if needed, revised at least every 5 years. "Current" means not more than five years old — and each SDS states its last-reviewed date, so you can check at a glance. An out-of-date SDS is a compliance gap a client can spot.
  • Prepare and maintain a hazardous chemicals register, with a current SDS for each chemical you keep, and keep it accessible. This is required under model WHS Regulation 346.

Labelling matters too. Hazardous chemicals must be correctly labelled per the model WHS Regulations — and that includes any container you decant or transfer product into. If you pour a stripper from its drum into a spray bottle or bucket for use, that secondary container still needs a correct label. An unlabelled mystery bottle on a childcare site is exactly the kind of thing that loses contracts and creates real risk.

One duty that often causes confusion is the manifest. A manifest is only required above the Schedule 11 threshold quantities for placarding/manifest. The volumes a floor-care contractor typically carries and uses are well below those thresholds, so ordinary floor-care quantities generally do not trigger a manifest requirement. Do not confuse the everyday register (which you do need) with a manifest (which large-quantity sites need). If you are ever genuinely storing in bulk, check Schedule 11 against what you hold.

Penalties for breaches exist and can be significant, but treat any dollar figures you see as illustrative and jurisdiction-specific — verify against the current Act that applies to you. The takeaway for this lesson is the system, not the fine: current SDS, a maintained register under Reg 346, correct labels including on decanted containers, and an awareness that VIC plays by its own OHS rules. Get that scaffolding in place and you are ready for the next lesson, where we actually read an SDS and build the register.

resources.label: Safe Work Australia — Safety data sheets
Safe Work Australia — Hazardous chemicals register

lessons.title: How to read a Safety Data Sheet (and build your register)
lessons.contentBody:
A Safety Data Sheet (SDS) is the single most useful safety document you carry. Once you can read one quickly, you can choose products, brief your crew, and answer a client's questions with confidence. This lesson covers what an SDS tells you, the GHS hazard system at a high level, and how to assemble those SDS into a working register.

An SDS is a structured document — the same headings appear in roughly the same order on every product, which is what makes them quick to scan once you know the layout. The information you will lean on most:

  • Hazards — what the product can do to people or surfaces, and the hazard classification.
  • Handling and storage — how to use and store it safely, including incompatibilities.
  • PPE — the gloves, eye protection and ventilation the manufacturer expects you to use.
  • First aid — what to do for skin, eye, inhalation or ingestion exposure.
  • Disposal — how to get rid of product and run-off responsibly.

Hazards are communicated through the Globally Harmonised System (GHS). You do not need to memorise the chemistry, but you should recognise the system: GHS sorts chemicals into hazard classes (for example, corrosive, irritant, flammable) and shows them with standard pictograms — the red-bordered diamonds, such as the corrosion symbol or the exclamation mark. When you see a corrosion pictogram on a stripper, that is your cue to take the PPE and ventilation sections seriously.

Let us make this real with products from the course catalogue. Actichem Instastrip (SDS AP400) is a vinyl-floor stripper. Strippers like this are typically alkaline or caustic, so its SDS is where you confirm the hazard class, the PPE, and the ventilation the manufacturer requires before you spread it across a floor. Compare that with a carpet prespray such as Actichem Clean Force (SDS AP453) — a different job, a different hazard profile, and its own handling and first-aid guidance. Reading both before you bring them onto a childcare site is exactly the habit this course is trying to build.

Crucially, the SDS (alongside the product's Technical Data Sheet, or TDS) is also where any product-specific numbers live — dilution ratios, and where relevant any drying or re-entry guidance. We do not give those as fixed figures in this course because they vary by product; instead, the rule is simple: follow the product SDS/TDS for that product. When a client asks "how should this be diluted?", the professional answer points at the SDS/TDS, not a number from memory.

Now the register. Building one is straightforward once you have the SDS in hand:

  • List every hazardous chemical you use, handle or store for the contract.
  • Attach a current SDS to each entry — remember, current means reviewed within the last 5 years, and the SDS shows its last-reviewed date.
  • Keep the register accessible at the workplace, as required under model WHS Regulation 346, so workers (and a client) can find an SDS fast.
  • Review it whenever you add or change a product, and refresh any SDS that is approaching five years old.

One practical caution: a handful of catalogue items may not have a current SDS available (for example some sealers or accessories). For anything like that, do not invent or guess a code — the correct step is to obtain the current SDS from the supplier before you add it to your register or take it on site. A register is only as good as the documents behind it, and "current and complete" is the standard a school or childcare contract will hold you to.

resources.label: Safe Work Australia — Safety data sheets
Safe Work Australia — GHS labelling of workplace hazardous chemicals

lessons.title: Choosing child-safe, low-tox products and ventilating occupied rooms
lessons.contentBody:
Schools and especially childcare centres are sensitive environments. Children spend long hours on and near the floors you clean, they are physically closer to surfaces, and they are more vulnerable to chemical exposure and to infection. This lesson covers why child-safe selection matters, and the practical steps to clean responsibly in occupied rooms.

The "why" comes from the National Quality Framework (NQF), which operates under the Education and Care Services National Law and National Regulations and is overseen by ACECQA. Within the NQF sits the National Quality Standard, and the part most relevant to you is Quality Area 2: Children's health and safety. It covers minimising risk and protecting children from harm and injury, and specifically includes protection from infection (Standard 2.1 Health and Standard 2.2 Safety). These elements map to enforceable provisions of the National Regulations — so the centre you are working for has real obligations here, and your work either supports them or undermines them. When you help a centre control infection risk and avoid unnecessary chemical exposure, you are directly helping them meet Quality Area 2. That is a strong selling point as well as the right thing to do.

So how do you put it into practice?

  • Select lower-tox products where you can. Choose products suited to occupied, child-occupied spaces rather than the harshest option available, and read the SDS for each so you understand its hazards and PPE before it comes on site. Match the product to the job — a gentle approach for routine work, reserving stronger chemistry (like a caustic stripper) for periodic deep-clean work scheduled outside child contact.
  • Dilute per the label. Use the dilution the manufacturer specifies on the label/SDS/TDS — no stronger "to be sure". Over-concentration raises both the hazard and the residue left behind, and it is precisely the SDS/TDS you should follow for the right ratio.
  • Ventilate. Bring in fresh air while you work and while products dry, particularly with strippers and sealers, which can release fumes. Open windows, use fans to move air, and keep doors managing airflow rather than channelling fumes toward children.
  • Isolate wet and treated areas from children. A wet, freshly stripped or freshly sealed floor is both a slip hazard and a chemical-contact hazard. Physically separate the work zone, sign it, and keep children out until the area is genuinely ready. Because safe re-entry timing is product-specific, do not rely on a generic time — follow the product SDS/TDS for that finish, and coordinate with the centre on access.

Scheduling is part of child-safety too. Heavier jobs — hard-floor strip and seal, deep carpet extraction — are typically planned into deep-clean or holiday programs rather than squeezed into a normal day with children present. Building that into your quote shows a client you understand their environment, not just your equipment.

A note on people, not just products: contractors doing child-related work at schools and childcare are generally required to hold a Working With Children check, and these are administered per state and territory (names and rules vary, and they are usually not transferable between states). Treat a WWC check as a starting point, not the whole picture — a site may also require its own child-safe induction. We cover this properly later; for now, hold it as part of the same child-safety mindset that drives your product choices.

resources.label: ACECQA — Quality Area 2: Children's health and safety
ACECQA — Guide to the National Quality Framework

lessons.title: Knowledge check: foundations
lessons.contentBody:
Use this short self-check to confirm the foundations from Module 0. Pick one answer (A–D) for each question, then check yourself against the answer key below.

Questions

1. Under the model WHS framework, how often must a Safety Data Sheet be reviewed for it to be considered "current"?

  • A. Every 12 months
  • B. At least every 5 years
  • C. Only when the product changes
  • D. Every 10 years

2. Which statement about Victoria is correct?

  • A. Victoria has not adopted any chemical safety laws
  • B. Victoria operates under its own OHS Act and Regulations rather than the model WHS framework
  • C. Victoria's rules are identical to the model WHS Regulations in every respect
  • D. Victoria does not require Safety Data Sheets

3. You decant a floor stripper from its drum into a spray bottle for the day's work. What must you do?

  • A. Nothing — only the original drum needs a label
  • B. Correctly label the spray bottle (the decanted container) as well
  • C. Add it to a manifest
  • D. Dispose of it the same day so no label is needed

4. Which Quality Area of the National Quality Standard is the main "why" for choosing child-safe, low-tox products and controlling infection in childcare?

  • A. Quality Area 1 (Educational program and practice)
  • B. Quality Area 2 (Children's health and safety)
  • C. Quality Area 5 (Relationships with children)
  • D. Quality Area 7 (Governance and leadership)

5. A client asks for the exact dilution ratio and safe re-entry time for a sealer. What is the correct professional response?

  • A. Quote a standard ratio and time from memory
  • B. Follow the product SDS/TDS, because these are product-specific
  • C. Use the figures set by AS 4586/4663
  • D. There is no way to find out

Answers

  • 1 — B. SDS must be reviewed at least every 5 years; "current" means not more than five years old, and each SDS shows its last-reviewed date.
  • 2 — B. Victoria uses its own OHS Act and Regulations, not the model WHS framework, so always check WorkSafe Victoria for VIC contracts.
  • 3 — B. Decanted or transferred containers must be correctly labelled under the model WHS Regulations — not just the original container.
  • 4 — B. NQS Quality Area 2 (Children's health and safety) covers protecting children from harm and infection, the reason behind child-safe selection.
  • 5 — B. Dilution and re-entry are product-specific, so the answer is always to follow the product SDS/TDS; standards like AS 4586/4663 classify or measure slip resistance and do not set re-entry times.

lessons.title: Carpet, fibre and soil: an CEC-accredited overview
lessons.contentBody:
Before you touch a wand or a machine, it helps to understand what you are actually cleaning and the framework professionals work to. For textile floor coverings, the recognised standard of care is ANSI/IICRC S100 (current edition S100-2021). It pulls together chemistry, fibre and construction knowledge, soil management, equipment, methods, safety and pre and post inspection, and it deliberately accommodates more than one cleaning method.

One thing to be clear about from the start: the IICRC is an industry certification body, not a government regulator. S100 is a respected standard of care that supports your judgement; it is not law and it is not mandatory in the way a WHS regulation is. Treat it as best practice that informs how you work, not as something a regulator will hold you to.

Fibre and construction basics

Carpet fibre and the way the carpet is built shape every decision you make. Different fibres respond differently to heat, moisture and chemistry, and the construction (cut pile, loop, density, backing) affects how soil is held and how quickly the carpet releases moisture. You do not need to memorise a chemistry textbook, but you do need to identify what is in front of you so you can match method and product to the carpet rather than forcing one approach onto everything.

Understanding soil

Soil is not one thing. It ranges from dry particulate soil (grit and dust tracked in on shoes) through to oily and greasy residues, water-soluble spills, and spots that need targeted treatment. Dry particulate soil is the bulk of what lands in a school or childcare carpet, and a large share of it can be removed by thorough vacuuming before any wet process. Recognising the soil type tells you whether the answer is mechanical removal, the right chemistry, or both.

Why pre-inspection matters

S100 treats pre and post inspection as part of the job, not an optional extra. A pre-inspection lets you identify the fibre and construction, read the soil load, find pre-existing damage or staining, and spot anything that changes your plan, such as delamination, prior repairs, or wear patterns. In an occupied school or childcare setting it also lets you flag realistic outcomes to the client before you begin, so there are no surprises afterwards.

Pre-inspection protects you commercially as well. Noting existing damage or permanent staining up front means a pre-existing problem is not later blamed on your visit. It is also where you decide method and sequencing, which matters when rooms are occupied and you cannot simply close the whole site.

The takeaway

Carpet cleaning done well is a deliberate process: identify the fibre and construction, read the soil, inspect before you start, and choose a method that suits the carpet and the setting. S100 gives you a coherent framework for all of that. Keep it as your reference point for the standard of care, while remembering it sits alongside, not above, your WHS duties and the site's own rules.

resources.label: ANSI/IICRC S100 — Standard for Professional Cleaning of Textile Floor Coverings

lessons.title: Hot water extraction vs encapsulation: the dry-time trade-off
lessons.contentBody:
S100 accommodates multiple cleaning methods, and for occupied schools and childcare centres the two you will weigh most often are hot water extraction (HWE) and encapsulation. Neither is simply better; they sit at different points on a trade-off between how much soil you remove and how quickly the carpet returns to service.

Hot water extraction

HWE applies cleaning solution and then extracts it, along with suspended soil and moisture, in the same pass. Its strength is depth: it is the method associated with deeper soil removal, which makes it well suited to heavily soiled carpet or a periodic deep clean. The trade-off is that it puts more moisture into the carpet, so the surface needs longer before it is back in normal use. How long depends on the carpet, the airflow, temperature and humidity on the day, so it is genuinely site-dependent and not something to promise as a fixed number.

Encapsulation

Encapsulation is a low-moisture method. The chemistry surrounds soil particles and crystallises, and the encapsulated soil is then removed by vacuuming. Because far less water goes into the carpet, the carpet returns to service faster, which is exactly why it suits occupied facilities where you cannot take a room out of action for long. The trade-off is that, as a lower-moisture surface clean, it is generally less aggressive on heavy embedded soil than a full extraction.

Framing it as a trade-off

The honest way to think about this is dry time versus soil load. Where the soil load is high and you have a window to let the carpet dry, HWE earns its keep. Where the priority is getting children and staff back into a classroom or care room quickly, encapsulation's faster return to service is the deciding factor, accepting that you are doing a lighter clean. Many operators use both across a site and a calendar: encapsulation for regular low-disruption maintenance during term, HWE for periodic deep cleans scheduled into holiday or deep-clean programs.

What not to promise

Resist the temptation to quote a fixed dry time. Drying depends on the carpet's fibre and construction, how much moisture you applied, airflow, temperature and humidity. Give the client a realistic, conditions-based explanation and manage airflow on site rather than committing to a number you cannot guarantee. The same goes for outcomes: set expectations against the soil load and method you have chosen.

The takeaway

Choose method by matching soil load against how fast the space must reopen. HWE for depth when you have a drying window; encapsulation for speed and low disruption in occupied rooms. Decide deliberately, explain the trade-off to the client, and let the conditions on the day, not a fixed figure, govern drying.

resources.label: ANSI/IICRC S100 — Standard for Professional Cleaning of Textile Floor Coverings

lessons.title: Pre-spray, spotting and protection with the CCW/Actichem range
lessons.contentBody:
Good carpet cleaning is as much about the right chemistry as the right machine. The CCW/Actichem range gives you a practical Australian product set for pre-spray and extraction, targeted spotting, and protection. Whatever you reach for, the rule is the same: read the Safety Data Sheet (SDS) before use, and dilute strictly per the product label. The notes below tell you what each product is for; the SDS and label govern how you actually use it.

Pre-spray and extraction

A pre-spray loosens and suspends soil ahead of extraction. From the Actichem range you have Clean Force AP453 and Extracta Pro AP457 as pre-spray and extraction products, Oxyboost Plus AP446 as an oxy booster, and the Foamex line (Foamex Liquid AP435 and Foamex Powder AP436). Hydramaster Release with Oxybreak is a further pre-spray and extraction option. Match the product to the soil and fibre you identified at pre-inspection, and confirm dilution from the label every time rather than from memory.

Spotting

Spots need targeted chemistry, not a blanket approach. The range covers common cases: Brownaway AP494 for browning, Bust AP496 as a general spotter, and Conquer AP462 and Conquer O2 AP478 for spotting. GumGone AP483 deals with chewing gum, which is a regular find in schools. VacAway Red Vanish targets red stains. Work spots before a general clean, test in an inconspicuous area first, and follow the SDS for each product, because spotters often have different handling requirements from your main pre-spray.

Protection

Fibre Shield is the protection product in the range, applied to help carpet resist soiling and spills after cleaning. As with everything else, application rate and method come from the product documentation, so check the SDS and label rather than assuming.

Reading the SDS is the job, not a formality

Citing an AP-code tells you which sheet to open; it does not tell you how to use the product. The SDS carries the hazard information, PPE, handling, storage and first-aid guidance, and the label carries the dilution. In an occupied school or childcare setting this matters even more, because you are working around children and staff. Treat the SDS as the authority on safe use and defer product specifics to it and the technical data sheet.

The takeaway

Build a small, deliberate kit: a pre-spray and extraction product suited to the soil, the right spotters for the stains you actually meet, and a protector if the client wants one. Then let the SDS and label drive concentration, PPE and handling. The product names and AP-codes here are real anchors; the safe way to use them always comes from their current documentation.

lessons.title: Low-disruption method and equipment for classrooms and care rooms
lessons.contentBody:
Cleaning carpet in a classroom or care room is a logistics problem as much as a cleaning problem. The space has to come back into use quickly, and you are often working around or near children and staff. A low-disruption approach built on low-moisture cleaning and careful sequencing lets you service the site without shutting it down.

The encapsulation and orbital option

For occupied rooms, an encapsulation approach using an orbital scrubber such as the Polystar 13" Orbital Encapsulation Scrubber is a strong fit. Because it is a low-moisture method, the carpet returns to service faster than after a full extraction, which is the whole point when a room is needed again soon. The encapsulated soil is removed by vacuuming once the chemistry has done its work, so thorough vacuuming remains part of the process, not an afterthought.

Managing drying

Even low-moisture work leaves the carpet damp for a period. Manage drying actively with airflow and ventilation rather than committing to a fixed dry time, which depends on the carpet, the room and the conditions on the day. While the carpet is damp it is a slip and access risk, so the area needs to be controlled until it is genuinely ready for normal use.

Cords, wet floors and child safety

Trailing cords and damp floors are the two hazards most likely to cause a problem in a child-occupied space. Route and manage cords so they are not a trip hazard, keep equipment and cabling out of areas children can reach, and isolate and clearly sign any wet or recently cleaned floor. The aim is that no child or staff member walks onto a damp surface or trips over your gear. Treat the work zone as off-limits until it is finished and safe.

Sequencing rooms

Because you cannot close the whole site, sequence the work. Clean rooms in an order that lets each one dry and reopen while you move on to the next, coordinating with the site so you are working in spaces that are genuinely free. Periodic deeper work that needs more drying is best scheduled into holiday or deep-clean programs rather than forced into a busy term-time day. Sequencing well is what turns a disruptive job into one the site barely notices.

Working with the site's own rules

Schools and childcare centres have their own safety and access rules, and contractors working around children are subject to state-based screening and the site's child-safe expectations. Treat the site induction and its rules as the baseline you work within, alongside your own WHS duties. Your method and equipment choices should make compliance easier, not harder.

The takeaway

Pair a low-moisture method and the right machine with disciplined hazard control and room sequencing. Manage drying by conditions, not by a fixed figure; keep cords and wet floors away from children; and fit the deeper work into the site's quieter windows.

resources.label: Safe Work Australia — Hazardous chemicals and WHS duties

lessons.title: Knowledge check: carpet cleaning
lessons.contentBody:
Use this short self-check to confirm the key points from this module. Choose one answer (A-D) for each question, then check the answers below.

Question 1

What best describes the status of ANSI/IICRC S100 for carpet cleaning?

  • A. A government regulation that legally mandates how carpet must be cleaned
  • B. A recognised industry standard of care, not law, that supports your judgement
  • C. A SafeWork Australia code of practice
  • D. A state-based licensing requirement for cleaners

Question 2

Compared with encapsulation, hot water extraction (HWE) is generally associated with:

  • A. Deeper soil removal but more moisture and a longer return to service
  • B. The lowest moisture and the fastest return to service
  • C. No need to vacuum beforehand
  • D. A guaranteed fixed dry time

Question 3

Why is encapsulation often preferred for occupied classrooms and care rooms?

  • A. It removes more embedded soil than any other method
  • B. It is a low-moisture method with a faster return to service
  • C. It needs no chemistry at all
  • D. It sets a fixed re-entry time

Question 4

Before using a product such as a pre-spray or spotter from the Actichem range, you should:

  • A. Estimate the dilution from experience to save time
  • B. Read the current SDS and dilute strictly per the product label
  • C. Use the strongest dilution to clean faster
  • D. Rely only on the product name and AP-code

Question 5

When cleaning carpet in a child-occupied room, which is the better practice?

  • A. Quote the client a fixed dry time so they can plan
  • B. Leave cords across walkways to reach the far wall
  • C. Isolate and sign damp floors, manage cords, and sequence rooms so they dry and reopen
  • D. Clean every room at once to finish quickly

Answers

1. B — The IICRC is an industry body; S100 is a standard of care, not law, and it supports rather than replaces your judgement.

2. A — HWE gives deeper soil removal but applies more moisture, so the carpet takes longer to return to service.

3. B — Encapsulation is low-moisture, so the room comes back into use faster, which suits occupied facilities.

4. B — The SDS governs safe handling and the label governs dilution; the AP-code only tells you which sheet to read.

5. C — Control hazards and sequence rooms; drying is conditions-dependent, so avoid promising a fixed dry time.

lessons.title: Hard-floor and grout soil: tools, brushes and chemistry
lessons.contentBody:
Tile and grout look like one surface, but they behave like two. The tile face is usually dense and largely sealed, so soil sits on top and rinses away readily. Grout lines are porous: they soak up greasy and particulate soil, trap it below the surface, and hold the dirt that makes a floor look dingy even after the tiles look clean. Understanding this split is the whole job. You are not just wiping a floor; you are lifting soil out of a porous, absorbent material that resists casual mopping.

Because grout is porous, water and dilute cleaning solution penetrate into it and so does soil. Routine mopping tends to push dirty water across the grout and leave a film behind, which is why grout lines darken over time on an otherwise serviceable floor. Effective cleaning needs three things working together: the right chemistry to loosen and suspend the soil, mechanical agitation to break it free from inside the grout, and proper rinse and recovery to remove the suspended soil rather than redepositing it.

Agitation tools

Agitation is what reaches into the grout line. From the CCW catalogue, the Dirty Grout Demon brushes are designed for this work, scrubbing soil out of the recessed grout that a flat mop simply slides over. For larger areas and a more consistent result, the Hydroforce SX-7 spinner head pairs pressurised cleaning solution with a contained spinning action so you can clean and recover from the tile and grout in one controlled pass. Match the tool to the job: hand brushes for detail, edges and small areas; the spinner head for production cleaning across an open floor. Always confirm the tool suits the tile type and condition before you start.

Alkaline hard-floor chemistry

Greasy and oily soils on hard floors are typically tackled with alkaline chemistry, which helps break down and lift those soils so they can be rinsed away. The Actichem hard-floor range supports this work; an example is Defender SV (SDS AP672). Alkaline products are effective cleaners, but they are also why reading the SDS matters. The Safety Data Sheet tells you the hazards, the personal protective equipment to wear, safe handling and storage, and first-aid measures. Treat the SDS and the product's technical guidance as the authority on dilution, suitability and use; this lesson does not set those figures. If a product you intend to use does not have a current SDS, obtain the current SDS from the supplier before using it.

Remember your broader WHS duties too. A workplace that uses hazardous chemicals must obtain and keep current SDS and keep them accessible to workers, maintain a hazardous chemicals register, and label containers correctly, including any product decanted into a smaller container. An SDS is considered current when it is no more than five years old, and each sheet states its last-reviewed date.

Rinse and recovery basics

Cleaning is only finished when the suspended soil is off the floor. Once chemistry and agitation have loosened the soil, you rinse to flush it out of the grout and recover the dirty solution rather than letting it dry back onto the surface. Good recovery, whether by extraction through a spinner head or by careful pick-up, is what leaves grout genuinely cleaner and the tile residue-free. Poor recovery leaves a film that attracts soil again quickly, so plan your rinse and recovery before you wet the floor.

resources.label: SafeWork Australia — Using and managing safety data sheets

lessons.title: Method, dwell and rinse — and slip resistance after cleaning (AS 4663)
lessons.contentBody:
A reliable tile and grout result comes from a repeatable method, not from working harder. The same sequence applied every time gives a consistent finish and makes problems easy to diagnose. Work to a sound order: pre-wet the floor, apply the cleaning solution, allow it to dwell, agitate, rinse, then recover. Each step has a purpose, and skipping or rushing one undermines the others.

The method, step by step

Pre-wetting the floor stops a dry, porous grout line from drinking up your cleaning solution unevenly and gives a more consistent dwell. Apply the solution evenly across the area you can realistically work before it dries. Let it dwell for the time the product label and SDS/TDS specify; dwell is what lets the chemistry loosen soil so agitation does not have to fight it. Then agitate, using the right tool for the area, the Dirty Grout Demon brushes for detail and edges and the Hydroforce SX-7 spinner head for open floor, to break soil free from inside the grout. Rinse thoroughly to flush the suspended soil out of the grout, and recover the dirty solution so it does not dry back onto the surface.

Residue control

Residue is the most common reason a cleaned floor looks good wet and disappointing dry. Leftover chemical or suspended soil dries as a film that dulls the tile, holds onto new soil, and can affect how the surface feels underfoot. Control it by applying only as much product as you can rinse and recover, rinsing properly, and recovering well. If in doubt, an extra rinse pass is cheaper than a callback. Keep your dwell within the label's guidance so the product does not dry on the floor before you can agitate and rinse it.

Confirming slip resistance after cleaning (AS 4663)

Cleaning, and any change to a floor's surface condition, can affect how slip-resistant it is. For a floor that is already in service, slip resistance is measured under AS 4663, which covers existing, in-service surfaces, as distinct from AS 4586, which classifies new surfaces before installation. The practical point for tile and grout work is that AS 4663 testing is the correct basis to confirm a finished floor still meets the slip-resistance class the site is aiming for. If a contract or site sets a target slip class, AS 4663 measurement is how you verify the cleaned floor against it.

Be careful about what these standards do and do not do. AS 4586 and AS 4663 classify and measure slip resistance only. They do not set cure, dry or re-entry times, and you should not imply that they do. They also do not tell you when it is safe to walk on a floor. While a floor is wet during and after cleaning it is a slip and access hazard, so isolate the area and use signage until it is dry and ready for use. For recommended minimum slip classifications by location type, the standards and supporting guidance such as HB 198 and the NCC are the source to consult directly; this lesson does not quote specific rating numbers.

Tie it together with documentation. A school or childcare contract values evidence as much as outcome, so record your method, the products used with their current SDS, and any slip-resistance verification you carry out. That record is what demonstrates you cleaned the floor safely and to the standard the client expects.

resources.label: Standards Australia — AS 4663 (slip resistance of existing pedestrian surfaces)

lessons.title: Knowledge check: tile & grout
lessons.contentBody:
Use this short self-check to confirm what you have taken from this module. Each question can be answered from the two lessons above. Choose one answer (A–D) per question, then check the answers and rationales at the end.

  • Q1. Why do grout lines tend to look dingy even when the tiles look clean?
    A. Grout is always a darker colour than tile
    B. Grout is porous and traps soil below the surface, while the denser tile face rinses clean
    C. Tiles are never sealed and grout always is
    D. Mopping cleans grout more effectively than tile
  • Q2. Which CCW tool is best suited to production cleaning across an open tiled floor with contained clean-and-recover in one pass?
    A. Dirty Grout Demon hand brush
    B. A flat mop
    C. Hydroforce SX-7 spinner head
    D. A dry dust mop
  • Q3. What does the Safety Data Sheet (SDS) for an alkaline hard-floor product such as Defender SV (AP672) provide?
    A. The required slip-resistance class for the site
    B. Hazards, PPE, safe handling and storage, and first-aid measures
    C. A guaranteed re-entry time for the floor
    D. The contract cleaning schedule
  • Q4. Which statement about AS 4663 is correct?
    A. It classifies new surfaces before installation
    B. It sets the re-entry time after cleaning
    C. It measures slip resistance of existing, in-service surfaces and can confirm a cleaned floor against a target slip class
    D. It mandates a specific cleaning chemical
  • Q5. What is the correct order for a sound tile and grout cleaning method?
    A. Recover, rinse, agitate, dwell, apply, pre-wet
    B. Pre-wet, apply, dwell, agitate, rinse, recover
    C. Apply, recover, pre-wet, rinse, dwell, agitate
    D. Agitate, apply, dwell, pre-wet, recover, rinse

Answers

  • Q1 — B. Grout is porous and absorbs and traps soil below the surface, whereas the denser tile face rinses clean, so grout looks dingy first.
  • Q2 — C. The Hydroforce SX-7 spinner head combines pressurised solution with a contained spinning action to clean and recover in one controlled pass over open floor.
  • Q3 — B. An SDS sets out hazards, PPE, safe handling and storage, and first-aid measures; it does not set the site's slip class or re-entry time.
  • Q4 — C. AS 4663 measures slip resistance of existing surfaces and is the correct basis to confirm a cleaned floor against a target slip class; it does not classify new surfaces or set re-entry times.
  • Q5 — B. The sound sequence is pre-wet, apply, dwell per the label, agitate, rinse, then recover.

modules.title: Vinyl Strip & Seal — the high-risk job

lessons.title: The strip-and-seal process, end to end
lessons.contentBody:
Vinyl and resilient floor strip-and-seal is one of the highest-risk jobs in commercial floor care. It uses caustic chemistry, leaves a wet and slippery surface, and depends on getting a sequence of steps right in the correct order. This lesson walks through the generic process end to end so you understand what each stage is for. The exact details for any step — dilution, coat counts, drying between coats — always come from the product's Safety Data Sheet (SDS) and Technical Data Sheet (TDS), never from memory or habit.

At a high level, the generic process runs: strip the old finish, then rinse and neutralise, then dry the floor, then apply multiple thin coats of sealer and finish with drying between each coat. Each stage prepares the surface for the next, so skipping or rushing a step undermines everything that follows.

Mapping the stages to a real Actichem system

We can map the generic process to a real Australian product line from the CCW / Actichem catalogue. Every product named here has a current SDS you must obtain and read before use.

  • Strip the old finish: Actichem Instastrip (SDS AP400) or Actichem Power Strip (SDS AP404). These strippers remove the existing sealer and finish layers. They are typically alkaline/caustic, which is why the safety lesson that follows matters so much.
  • Rinse and neutralise / prep: Actichem Pre-Seal / Preseal Pro (SDS AP171). After stripping, the floor is rinsed and the surface is prepared and neutralised so the new coats bond properly and no stripper residue is left behind.
  • Apply sealer: Actichem Instaseal (SDS AP420). The sealer goes down first as the base layer or layers.
  • Apply finish: Actichem Instagloss (SDS AP422). The finish / gloss coats build the protective, presentable surface on top of the sealer.

The drying stage between stripping and sealing, and the drying between each sealer and finish coat, is critical. A floor that is not dry, or coats applied before the previous coat is ready, will not perform. How long that takes is product-specific.

Why every step defers to the SDS and TDS

The specifics of every step — how much to dilute the stripper, how many coats to apply, how thin each coat should be, and how long to wait between coats — are set by the manufacturer for that exact product. They live in the SDS and TDS, not in this lesson. Two strippers, or two sealers, can behave very differently, so treating one product's process as a universal rule is a real hazard.

In a school or childcare facility this discipline matters even more. The area must be planned and isolated for the full duration of the job, and the work is usually scheduled into deep-clean or holiday programs rather than squeezed into an occupied day. Read the SDS and TDS first, build the job plan around what they say, and keep a current SDS for every product on site in your hazardous chemicals register. If a product lacks a current SDS, obtain it from the supplier before you start.

resources.label: SafeWork Australia — Using safety data sheets

lessons.title: Caustic stripper safety: PPE, ventilation, isolation and signage
lessons.contentBody:
Strippers used in vinyl strip-and-seal work are typically alkaline/caustic. That single fact drives the whole safety approach for this stage. Caustic chemistry can burn skin and eyes, give off vapours, and react with the floor — and the wet stripped floor itself becomes a serious slip and access hazard. This lesson covers how to control those risks. Always anchor your controls to your work health and safety (WHS) duties and to the specific stripper's Safety Data Sheet (SDS).

Your WHS starting point

Under the model WHS framework, a PCBU that uses, handles or stores hazardous chemicals must obtain and keep a current SDS for each one and keep it accessible to workers at the workplace. A hazardous chemicals register, with a current SDS for each chemical, must be prepared, maintained and accessible. Decanted or transferred containers must still be correctly labelled. Note that Victoria operates under its own OHS Act and Regulations rather than the model WHS framework, so check with your state regulator — SafeWork NSW, WorkSafe Victoria or WHSQ — for your jurisdiction.

The SDS for products such as Actichem Instastrip (SDS AP400) or Actichem Power Strip (SDS AP404) is your primary source for the specific PPE, handling, storage and first-aid measures for that stripper. Read it before the job, not during an incident.

Controls for caustic strippers

  • PPE: Protect skin and eyes. Caustic strippers warrant appropriate gloves, eye protection and clothing as specified in the product SDS. Treat splashes seriously.
  • Ventilation: Provide ventilation as the SDS directs. Stripping is wet, chemical work indoors, so airflow matters.
  • Spill control: Have spill control ready. Confine spills, clean them up promptly, and follow the SDS for the correct method and disposal.
  • Eye and skin contact: Know the first-aid response in the SDS before you start so you can act immediately if contact happens.

The wet floor is a hazard in its own right

A floor that has been stripped or freshly recoated is wet and slippery, and it stays a slip and access hazard while it is wet or curing. Anyone walking onto it risks a fall, and foot traffic also ruins the finish. The control is simple and non-negotiable: isolate the area and sign it clearly. Keep people out for the whole duration the floor is wet or curing.

This is especially important in schools and childcare centres, where children may not read or heed a sign. Physical isolation — barriers, locked-off rooms, scheduling into holiday or out-of-hours periods — protects children far better than a sign alone. Plan the access control before you open the first drum of stripper. Do not state a fixed time for how long to keep people off the floor; how long the floor stays wet or curing is product-specific and comes from the SDS and TDS, not from a rule of thumb.

resources.label: SafeWork Australia — Using safety data sheets

lessons.title: Sealing and finishing — cure & re-entry come from the SDS/TDS, not guesswork
lessons.contentBody:
Once the floor is stripped, rinsed, neutralised and fully dry, you build the new surface back up by applying coats of sealer and then finish. The craft here is in applying thin, even coats and allowing each coat to dry before the next goes down. The single most important safety and quality rule of this lesson is this: do not rely on any fixed dry, cure, re-entry or buffing time. Those times are product-specific, and the only correct source for them is the product's Technical Data Sheet (TDS) and Safety Data Sheet (SDS).

Thin, even coats with drying between them

Sealer and finish are built up in multiple thin coats rather than one thick layer. Thin coats dry and cure more predictably and produce a more durable, even surface. Using a real Actichem system, the sealer base is Actichem Instaseal (SDS AP420) and the finish or gloss coats are Actichem Instagloss (SDS AP422). The earlier prep step — Actichem Pre-Seal / Preseal Pro (SDS AP171) — sets the surface up so these coats bond well.

Between each coat the floor must be allowed to dry. Applying a coat before the one beneath it is ready will trap solvent or water, cloud the finish, and weaken adhesion. How thin each coat should be, how many coats to apply, and how long to wait between them are all product-specific values found in the TDS.

Why you must never state a fixed cure or re-entry time

It is tempting to carry a number in your head — "finishes are walkable after so long." Resist it. Different sealers and finishes cure at different rates, and that rate is affected by temperature, humidity, airflow and coat thickness. The manufacturer publishes the correct dry-between-coats, full-cure and re-entry guidance for that exact product in its TDS. Your job is to read it and plan the work around it.

Practically, that means planning a strip-and-seal job backwards from the TDS: count the coats, add the manufacturer's drying time between each, allow for full cure before the floor returns to service, and only then commit to a return date with the client. In a school or childcare facility this is why such work is scheduled into deep-clean or holiday programs — there has to be enough closed time for the floor to dry and cure properly with the area isolated and signed.

What to do on site

  • Obtain and read the current SDS and TDS for every sealer and finish before you start.
  • Apply thin, even coats and let each dry per the TDS before the next.
  • Keep the area isolated and signed for the whole drying and curing period.
  • Build the schedule and the client's re-entry date from the TDS, not from memory.
  • If a product lacks a current SDS or TDS, obtain it from the supplier rather than guessing.

resources.label: SafeWork Australia — Using safety data sheets

lessons.title: Verifying slip resistance to a target class (AS 4663)
lessons.contentBody:
A freshly sealed and finished floor can have a different slip resistance from the bare or previously finished surface. A glossy new finish may feel and behave differently underfoot, especially when wet. So a strip-and-seal job is not truly finished until you have confirmed the floor still meets the slip-resistance target that the site requires. This lesson explains where AS 4663 fits — and, just as importantly, what it does not do.

Two standards, two jobs

There are two related Australian Standards for slip resistance, and they apply to different stages:

  • AS 4586:2013 classifies the slip resistance of new pedestrian surfaces. This is laboratory or pre-installation testing — it is about products and materials before they go down.
  • AS 4663:2013 measures the slip resistance of existing surfaces in service, on site.

Because a strip-and-seal job produces an existing, in-service floor, AS 4663 is the correct basis to re-verify slip resistance after stripping and sealing. It lets you measure the finished floor as it actually is and check it against the target slip class for that location.

What AS 4663 does and does not do

AS 4663 measures and classifies the slip resistance of the surface. That is its job. It is essential to be clear about its limits:

  • AS 4663 does not set cure or re-entry times. It tells you how slip-resistant the floor is, not when it is safe to reopen. Re-entry timing comes from the product TDS and SDS, as covered earlier in this module.
  • AS 4586 and AS 4663 classify and measure only — they do not impose a particular outcome by themselves.

For guidance on what slip-resistance classification is recommended for a given location type, the recommended minimum classifications are set out in companion guidance such as SA HB 198:2014, which the National Construction Code references for new building work. Quote that guidance and the NCC directly rather than carrying a specific rating value in your head, because the appropriate target depends on the location and the current documents.

Putting it into practice

Decide the target slip class with reference to HB 198 and the NCC and any requirement in the site's contract. After the finish has cured per the TDS, arrange measurement of the finished floor in accordance with AS 4663 to confirm it still meets that target. In a school or childcare setting, where wet entryways and spill-prone areas carry real fall risk for children, this verification step turns "the floor looks great" into documented evidence that it performs to the agreed class. Keep that result with your job records as part of the documentation a contract expects.

resources.label: Standards Australia — AS 4663:2013 (slip resistance of existing surfaces)

lessons.title: Knowledge check: strip & seal
lessons.contentBody:
This short self-check helps you confirm the key safety and process points from this module. Choose one answer (A–D) for each question, then check the answers below.

1. In the generic strip-and-seal process, what is the correct order of stages?

  • A. Apply finish, then strip, then rinse
  • B. Strip old finish, rinse/neutralise, dry, then apply multiple thin sealer/finish coats with drying between coats
  • C. Dry, strip, apply one thick coat, reopen
  • D. Neutralise, apply finish, strip, rinse

2. Where do the correct dry, cure and re-entry times for a sealer or finish come from?

  • A. A fixed industry rule you can memorise
  • B. AS 4663
  • C. The product's SDS and TDS
  • D. Whatever the previous job used

3. Vinyl floor strippers such as Actichem Instastrip (SDS AP400) are typically:

  • A. Acidic and harmless
  • B. Alkaline/caustic, requiring PPE and ventilation
  • C. Food-grade and skin-safe
  • D. Inert and non-hazardous

4. Which standard is the correct basis to verify slip resistance of the finished, in-service floor?

  • A. AS 4586:2013 (new surfaces, lab/pre-installation)
  • B. AS 4663:2013 (existing surfaces, on site)
  • C. Neither — slip resistance cannot be measured
  • D. The SDS sets the slip class

5. While a stripped or freshly recoated floor is wet or curing, the correct control is to:

  • A. Let normal foot traffic continue
  • B. Rely on a single warning sign in an occupied childcare room
  • C. Isolate the area and sign it, keeping people out for the whole wet/curing period
  • D. Open windows and reopen immediately

Answers

  • 1 — B. The generic process is strip, rinse/neutralise, dry, then multiple thin sealer/finish coats with drying between coats.
  • 2 — C. Dry, cure and re-entry times are product-specific and come only from the SDS/TDS, never from a fixed rule.
  • 3 — B. Strippers are typically alkaline/caustic, so PPE and ventilation per the SDS are required.
  • 4 — B. AS 4663:2013 measures existing in-service surfaces, so it is the basis for verifying the finished floor.
  • 5 — C. The wet or curing floor is a slip and access hazard, so isolate and sign it; in childcare, physical isolation beats a sign alone.

lessons.title: Pressure-washing technique, surfaces and pressures
lessons.contentBody:
Pressure washing looks straightforward, but on a school or childcare site it is a job that rewards judgement. The same machine that strips chewing gum off a path can also gouge soft pavers, etch concrete, blow render off a wall, or drive a stream of grit and water into someone's leg. The skill is matching the pressure and the nozzle to the surface in front of you, and setting up the area so nobody gets hurt.

Start by reading the surface. Hard, sound concrete tolerates higher pressure than older or decorative surfaces. Pavers, especially sand-jointed or clay pavers, can be displaced or pitted, and aggressive blasting can wash out jointing sand and leave a loose, uneven path. Soft-fall and rubberised play surfaces, painted line-marking, timber, and anything coated or sealed are easily damaged and usually call for the lowest practical pressure or a different method altogether. If you are unsure how a surface will respond, test a small, out-of-the-way patch first and check the result before committing to the whole area.

Nozzle choice controls how the water hits the surface. A narrow, tight jet concentrates force and is the most likely to cut or scar; a wider fan spreads the same pressure over more area and is gentler. As a rule, work with the widest fan that still does the job, keep the nozzle moving, and hold a consistent distance and angle rather than driving the tip hard into one spot. Backing the nozzle off the surface and increasing the fan angle are both ways to reduce intensity when you see a surface starting to mark.

Treat the equipment as a genuine injury hazard. High-pressure water can break skin and inject contaminants, so never point the lance at yourself or anyone else, never try to clear a blockage with the trigger live, and wear the personal protective equipment the task calls for — eye protection, sturdy footwear and hearing protection where the machine is loud. Hoses, leads and a wet, slippery work zone are all trip and slip risks; keep them tidy and out of walkways.

Working around children's outdoor areas adds a layer that ordinary commercial sites do not have. The expectation under the childcare quality framework is that children are protected from harm and injury, so the work needs to be planned so children are nowhere near it. Schedule pressure washing for times the area is vacated — before children arrive, after they leave, or during holiday and deep-clean programs when outdoor spaces are not in use. Physically cordon off the work zone so there is no casual access, keep equipment, leads and chemicals secured, and make sure the surface is dry, rinsed and safe to walk on before the area is handed back. Confirm timing and access with the site contact rather than assuming a window is clear.

Finally, remember that pressure washing on these sites is never just a cleaning task — it also generates wash water that has to be controlled. How you set up the area, where the water runs, and how you capture it are covered in the next lesson, and they should be planned before you pull the trigger, not after.

lessons.title: “The drain is just for rain”: capturing run-off and trade-waste to sewer
lessons.contentBody:
There are two drainage systems on most Australian sites, and confusing them is one of the easiest ways to cause real environmental harm. Stormwater drains — the grates in the carpark, the pits along the path, the kerbside gully — are built to carry rain. In most areas that water flows untreated, straight to creeks, rivers and the sea. As the regulators put it, the drain is just for rain. The sewer is the other system: it goes to a treatment plant. The whole job of run-off control is making sure your wash water ends up in the right one.

Pressure-washing run-off is not clean water. It carries detergents, grease, grit, paint flakes, gum and whatever else was on the surface, and that mix must not be allowed to enter stormwater drains. The practical answer is to confine the area, collect the wash water, and direct it to sewer. Containment can be done with booms or absorbent socks ringing the work zone, with temporary bunding to pond the water, and with mats or covers over nearby stormwater pits so nothing escapes down them. Recovery is usually by a wet vacuum or vacuum-recovery system that lifts the water for proper disposal rather than letting it run off across the ground.

Getting the water to sewer is not as simple as tipping it down the nearest gully trap. Discharging trade wastewater to the sewer needs prior written approval from the water authority — commonly called a trade-waste agreement. That approval sets out what may be discharged and on what conditions, and it must be in place before you discharge, not arranged afterwards. The water authority is the body that decides whether, where and how your wash water can enter the sewer, so contact them early when you are quoting a recurring contract.

Behind all of this sits a general environmental duty: the broad obligation to take reasonable and practicable steps to prevent or minimise harm to the environment from your activities. In Victoria, for example, the EPA treats contaminated wash water as industrial waste that must be captured and kept out of stormwater, and the general environmental duty applies. That duty does not wait for someone to inspect you — it is on you to plan the job so run-off is controlled from the start.

Letting contaminated water reach waterways is a pollution offence, and polluting-waters offences can carry significant penalties. To give a sense of scale only, you may see figures cited in the tens or hundreds of thousands of dollars for serious water-pollution offences — but treat any such number as illustrative and jurisdiction-specific. The actual offences, defences and penalty amounts differ between states and territories and change over time, so the only reliable figures are the current ones from the relevant Act.

The takeaway for a school or childcare contract is to design run-off control into the method before you start: know where the stormwater pits are and protect them, contain and recover the wash water, arrange the trade-waste approval needed to discharge to sewer, and confirm the specific requirements with the local water authority and EPA. When in doubt, ask the water authority and the EPA in your jurisdiction rather than guessing — the rules are local, and they are the ones who set them.

resources.label: Sydney Water – trade wastewater and the sewer
EPA NSW – stormwater and water pollution

lessons.title: Knowledge check: pressure washing
lessons.contentBody:
Use this short self-check to test the key points from this module. Choose one answer (A–D) for each question, then check yourself against the answers below.

1. You are pressure washing a sand-jointed paver path at a childcare centre. Which approach best avoids surface damage?

  • A. Use the narrowest jet at maximum pressure to finish faster
  • B. Use the widest practical fan, keep the nozzle moving, and test a small area first
  • C. Hold the tight jet on each joint to clear it fully
  • D. Increase pressure until the jointing sand washes out

2. When should outdoor pressure washing at a childcare centre be scheduled?

  • A. During outdoor play so staff can supervise the cleaner
  • B. Whenever the cleaner is available, with children kept a few metres back
  • C. When the area is vacated, with the work zone cordoned off
  • D. It does not matter as long as the equipment is new

3. “The drain is just for rain” means that:

  • A. Stormwater drains generally flow untreated to waterways, so contaminated wash water must not enter them
  • B. Stormwater is treated before release, so any run-off is fine
  • C. Rainwater is the only thing that may legally enter the sewer
  • D. Wash water can go down any drain after it rains

4. Before discharging pressure-washing wash water (trade wastewater) to the sewer, you must:

  • A. Dilute it with clean water until it looks clear
  • B. Have prior written approval – a trade-waste agreement – from the water authority
  • C. Notify the school after the job is done
  • D. Nothing – the sewer accepts any liquid waste

5. A colleague quotes you an exact dollar penalty for a water-pollution offence. How should you treat it?

  • A. As a fixed national figure that applies everywhere
  • B. As illustrative and jurisdiction-specific – verify against the current Act
  • C. As the maximum the EPA can ever impose
  • D. As irrelevant, since penalties are never enforced

Answers

  • 1. B — the widest practical fan, a moving nozzle and a test patch minimise the risk of pitting pavers or washing out jointing sand.
  • 2. C — work when the area is vacated and cordoned off so children are protected from harm.
  • 3. A — stormwater generally flows untreated to waterways, so contaminated wash water must be kept out of stormwater drains.
  • 4. B — discharging trade wastewater to sewer needs prior written approval (a trade-waste agreement) from the water authority.
  • 5. B — penalty figures are illustrative and jurisdiction-specific; always verify against the current Act in your state or territory.

lessons.title: Scheduling around occupancy, term breaks and deep-clean windows
lessons.contentBody:
Schools and childcare centres are occupied facilities, so the schedule is part of the service, not an afterthought. The work splits into two kinds. Daily or routine work is the light, frequent cleaning that keeps a floor presentable and safe between heavier treatments. Periodic work is the heavy, intermittent work: hard-floor strip and seal, and carpet hot water extraction. These periodic jobs are typically scheduled into deep-clean or school-holiday windows rather than squeezed into a daily routine. That is the directional pattern in published programs such as those from School Infrastructure NSW, but the actual frequency and timing always sit in the current contract specification, so verify against the spec rather than assuming.

The reason periodic work goes into holiday or after-hours windows is simple. Stripping and sealing a vinyl floor leaves it wet and curing, which is a slip and access hazard, and the floor is out of action until the product's own drying steps are complete. Carpet extraction adds moisture that needs time to dry before the space is reused. You cannot reliably run either through a corridor full of children. Choosing a low-occupancy window removes that conflict.

When you do have to work in an occupied facility, plan for low disruption with three levers.

  • Sequencing: work room by room or zone by zone so the centre always has usable space. Treat one area, let it return to service, then move to the next, rather than closing everything at once.
  • Drying: build realistic drying time into the plan. For carpet, encapsulation is a low-moisture method that returns a space to use faster than hot water extraction, which removes more soil but leaves more moisture. That is a genuine dry-time versus soil-load trade-off you weigh against the window you have. Do not quote a fixed dry time; it depends on the method, the conditions and the product.
  • Signage and isolation: barrier off and sign wet or curing floors. A freshly stripped or recoated floor is a slip hazard while wet, so isolate it and keep foot traffic out until it is ready.

Term breaks and school holidays are your main periodic-work windows. They give you uninterrupted access, room to ventilate caustic strippers properly, and enough time for multiple thin sealer coats with drying between each coat. They also let you re-verify slip resistance on a finished hard floor before students return. Map your periodic program to these windows early, and confirm dates and access with the site so you are not relying on assumed frequencies.

A practical planning sequence looks like this. First, separate the routine scope from the periodic scope in the contract. Second, place each periodic task in the appropriate deep-clean or holiday window. Third, for any work that must happen while the facility is occupied, write the sequence, the expected return-to-service approach, and the signage and isolation plan. Fourth, confirm the plan against the current contract spec and the site's own rules before you commit dates.

Treating scheduling this way protects the people in the building, keeps the floor safe and serviceable, and shows the client you understand how a child-occupied site actually runs. It is often the difference between a contract that is renewed and one that is not.

lessons.title: Compliance & documentation: SDS register, WWC checks, NQS QA2 and school cleaning standards
lessons.contentBody:
A school or childcare contract assumes a baseline of paperwork before you set foot on site. This lesson maps that baseline at a high level and points you to the authority for each item. None of it replaces the contract's own requirements or the site's own rules; treat these as starting points, not a complete compliance list.

SDS register. If you use, handle or store hazardous chemicals, you are a PCBU with duties under the relevant work health and safety law. You must obtain and keep a current safety data sheet for each hazardous chemical and keep them accessible to workers at the workplace. A safety data sheet is treated as current when it is not more than five years old, and each sheet states its last-reviewed date. You also maintain a hazardous chemicals register with a current SDS for each chemical. Note that VIC operates under its own OHS framework rather than the model WHS laws, so confirm with your state regulator. Direct questions to SafeWork Australia and your state body.

Working With Children checks. Child-related work, which includes contractors at schools and childcare centres, generally requires a Working With Children check. These are legislated and administered separately in each state and territory, and the names differ: NSW has the WWCC, VIC has the Working with Children Check, QLD has the Blue Card, and the ACT uses WWVP registration. They are generally not auto-transferable between jurisdictions, so a check from one state may not be recognised in another. Just as importantly, a WWC check may not be the only screening a site requires; many sites add their own child-safe induction. Do not treat it as sufficient on its own, and check the exact eligibility and process with the relevant state or territory body rather than relying on a general summary.

Childcare NQS Quality Area 2. Childcare operates under the National Quality Framework, overseen by ACECQA. Quality Area 2 covers children's health and safety, including minimising risk and protecting children from harm and infection, across Standard 2.1 (health) and Standard 2.2 (safety). Your floor-care methods, product choices and scheduling should align with that intent. This is the centre's obligation; your job is to support it, not to certify the centre against any standard. Point to ACECQA for the detail.

School cleaning standards. Public schools are cleaned to published cleaning performance standards. In NSW, for example, public schools are cleaned under a whole-of-government cleaning contract with published performance standards from School Infrastructure NSW. Frequencies and tasks in those standards are directional for your planning; the binding detail is the current contract specification, so verify against it.

A simple readiness check before you quote:

  • Is there a current SDS for every chemical you will bring, all within five years and in an accessible register?
  • Does each worker on site hold the correct state or territory WWC check, and have you confirmed any additional site induction?
  • Do your methods and products support, not undermine, NQS Quality Area 2 in a childcare setting?
  • Have you read the actual school cleaning standard or contract spec rather than assuming the frequencies?

Getting this baseline in order signals to a client that you can be trusted on a child-occupied site, which is exactly what the contract is buying.

lessons.title: Capstone: build your child-safe floor-care service offer
lessons.contentBody:
This capstone pulls the whole course into one deliverable: a one-page, child-safe floor-care service offer you can put to a school or childcare client. The aim is not a marketing brochure. It is a clear statement of how you will deliver safe, compliant work in a child-occupied facility, backed by the documentation a contract expects. Build it section by section.

Methods per surface. State your approach for each surface you will service. For carpet, name the method and why: hot water extraction for deeper soil removal, or encapsulation as a low-moisture option that returns a space to use faster in an occupied site. For tile and grout, describe your cleaning approach. For vinyl and resilient floors, set out the strip and seal process at a high level: strip, rinse and neutralise, dry, then multiple thin sealer and finish coats with drying between coats. Make clear that dilution ratios, coat counts and any drying or re-entry steps come from each product's SDS and technical data sheet, not from a fixed rule.

SDS register. List the chemicals you will bring and confirm a current SDS, within five years, for each one, held in an accessible register. Where a product lacks a current SDS, state that you will obtain the current SDS from the supplier before use.

Child-safe product choices. Explain how your product and method choices support NQS Quality Area 2 in childcare settings, including minimising risk and infection. Keep this as alignment of intent, not a certification claim.

Slip-resistance verification. Commit to re-verifying slip resistance on finished hard floors after sealing, on the basis of AS 4663, which measures existing in-service surfaces. Be clear that AS 4663 classifies and measures only; it does not set cure or re-entry times, and you should not quote a specific P-rating requirement without checking HB 198 or the NCC.

Run-off control for pressure washing. State how you will keep wash water out of stormwater, because stormwater flows untreated to waterways. Confine the area, collect the wastewater and direct it to sewer, and note that discharging trade wastewater to sewer needs prior written approval through a trade-waste agreement with the water authority. VIC operators should note EPA Victoria treats contaminated wash water as industrial waste.

Scheduling. Show that periodic strip and seal and carpet extraction sit in deep-clean or holiday windows, with low-disruption sequencing, realistic drying and signage and isolation for any work done while the facility is occupied. Confirm frequencies against the current contract spec.

Documentation. List what you will provide: the SDS register, the correct state or territory WWC checks for your workers plus any site induction, your method statements, and your slip-resistance verification records.

Pull these onto a single page. A short readiness checklist helps:
