  • Methods named per surface, with product steps deferred to SDS and TDS.
  • Current SDS register in place.
  • Child-safe product rationale aligned to NQS QA2.
  • AS 4663 slip verification after sealing.
  • Run-off captured to sewer under a trade-waste agreement.
  • Periodic work scheduled into low-occupancy windows.
  • WWC checks and documentation confirmed.

Then take the single next action this whole course has been driving toward: put a compliant, child-safe floor-care offer to a school or childcare client.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: the contract
lessons.contentBody:
Test yourself on winning and servicing a school or childcare floor-care contract. Choose one answer for each question, then check the answers below.

1. Periodic work such as hard-floor strip and seal or carpet hot water extraction is typically scheduled into:

  • A. The daily routine clean
  • B. Deep-clean or school-holiday windows
  • C. Peak occupancy, to show the client the work
  • D. Whenever a worker has a spare hour

2. For a current safety data sheet, which statement is correct?

  • A. It must be reprinted every week
  • B. It is treated as current if not more than five years old, with a stated last-reviewed date
  • C. It only needs to be kept at head office, not on site
  • D. It is optional if the chemical seems mild

3. Working With Children checks in Australia are:

  • A. A single national check valid everywhere
  • B. Issued only to permanent school staff, never contractors
  • C. State and territory based, generally not auto-transferable, and not necessarily the only screening a site requires
  • D. Replaced entirely by a police check

4. After sealing a hard floor, slip resistance is best re-verified on the basis of:

  • A. AS 4586, which classifies new surfaces before installation
  • B. AS 4663, which measures existing in-service surfaces
  • C. The sealer's gloss level
  • D. The SDS re-entry time

5. Pressure-washing run-off should be:

  • A. Hosed straight into the nearest stormwater drain
  • B. Left to evaporate on the surface
  • C. Confined and collected, then directed to sewer under a trade-waste agreement
  • D. Tipped into a garden bed

Answers

  • 1. B — Strip and seal and extraction leave floors wet or curing, so they go into low-occupancy deep-clean or holiday windows; verify timing against the contract spec.
  • 2. B — A safety data sheet is treated as current when not more than five years old and states its last-reviewed date, and it must be accessible to workers on site.
  • 3. C — WWC checks are administered per state and territory, are generally not auto-transferable, and a site may also require its own child-safe induction.
  • 4. B — AS 4663 measures existing surfaces in service, so it is the correct basis to re-verify a finished sealed floor; it measures only and does not set re-entry times.
  • 5. C — Stormwater flows untreated to waterways, so run-off must be confined, collected and directed to sewer under prior trade-waste approval.

lessons.title: Final knowledge check

courses.title: CARSI Maintenance Company Onboarding — Professional Floor Care & Operational Readiness
courses.description:
Part of CARSI Maintenance Company Onboarding. The internal induction for CARSI Maintenance Company floor-care technicians, supervisors and facilities staff who deliver cleaning, polishing, sealing, maintenance and hygiene services across schools, childcare, education, public buildings, commercial and high-traffic facilities.

Twelve modules take a new starter from induction to independent operator: what professional floor care is and why it matters, health and hygiene in child-sensitive environments, WHS/PPE/SDS and chemical safety, surface identification, methods, machinery, vehicle and equipment readiness, site setup and hazard control, professional conduct, confidentiality and CCTV awareness, job completion and evidence, and a final practical assessment. Each module ends in an auto-marked knowledge check. Written in Australian English; training supports good judgement and does not replace the SDS/TDS, standards, or a site’s own rules.
courses.shortDescription: CARSI Maintenance Company Onboarding — the floor-care induction for technicians, supervisors and facilities staff: safety, hygiene, methods, operational readiness and professional conduct across sensitive sites.

meta.program: Professional Floor Care Onboarding & Operational Readiness Program

pricing.note: Organisation subscription: AUD $1,295/month + GST, unlimited students. Provisioned manually; recurring billing is not yet wired (Stripe subscription build is separate).

modules.title: Module 1: Introduction to Professional Floor Care

lessons.title: What professional floor care is
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • describe what professional floor care covers and its five service types
  • explain why floor care is a safety, hygiene, presentation and asset-protection service
  • use careful, claim-safe language about what the service does

Welcome. This program is for technicians, supervisors and facilities staff who deliver floor surface cleaning, polishing, sealing, maintenance and hygiene services across schools, childcare, education and public buildings, commercial sites and other high-traffic facilities.

Professional floor care is more than cleaning. It is a safety, hygiene, presentation, asset-protection and operational-readiness service. There are five service types you will hear about:

  • Routine cleaning — regular removal of soil and spills.
  • Deep / restorative cleaning — periodic, heavier soil removal.
  • Polishing / burnishing — restoring finish and appearance.
  • Sealing / coating — protecting the surface.
  • Maintenance — keeping the result so the next visit is easier.

We use careful language about what floor care does. It supports hygiene, reduces soil load and helps manage contaminants. We do not make medical claims and we do not claim to sterilise.

lessons.title: Why floors matter in sensitive facilities
lessons.contentBody:
Floors are high-contact, high-risk surfaces. Everyone walks on them, and they carry tracked-in soil, spills and contaminants. In schools, childcare and public buildings, children, staff and vulnerable people use those floors at close range.

How we clean and maintain a floor affects:

  • hygiene and the soil load underfoot;
  • slip, trip and fall risk;
  • the presentation of the facility and the client’s confidence in us;
  • the life of the floor — an expensive asset we protect or damage.

Poor floor care does the opposite: it spreads soil, creates slip hazards, looks bad, and shortens the life of the surface. That is why the standard matters on every job.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: introduction
The contaminants we help manage
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • identify the contaminants floor care helps manage
  • apply clean-to-dirty order and colour-coded equipment to prevent cross-contamination
  • adjust the job for child-occupied and sensitive spaces

Floor care helps manage a range of contaminants that build up or get tracked in:

  • pollen, dust and dirt;
  • body soils, food soils and spills;
  • tracked-in contaminants from outside;
  • conditions that support mould growth when moisture is left behind.

Our job is to remove and reduce that load with the correct process — not to spread it around. Dirty pads, mops and cleaning solution re-deposit soil, so clean equipment and the right method matter as much as the chemical.

Careful language again: we support a facility’s hygiene and help manage contaminants. We do not make disinfection or health-outcome claims.

lessons.title: Cross-contamination control and child-sensitive work
lessons.contentBody:
Two habits prevent cross-contamination:

  • work clean-to-dirty — from cleaner areas toward dirtier ones;
  • keep colour-coded equipment separated, especially toilet and kitchen tools from general-area tools.

In child-occupied and sensitive spaces, adjust the job:

  • choose lower-tox products where suitable and read the SDS;
  • ventilate the room when using chemicals;
  • isolate and sign wet areas, and keep chemicals away from children;
  • time the work around occupancy where you can.

Infection-control awareness means we support the facility’s hygiene practices — we do not claim to disinfect or guarantee an outcome.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: health and hygiene

modules.title: Module 3: WHS, PPE, SDS & Chemical Safety

lessons.title: Reading a Safety Data Sheet (SDS)
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • locate and read the key sections of a Safety Data Sheet
  • select PPE from the SDS and place it correctly in the hierarchy of controls
  • apply the labelling, storage and spill rules, noting the Victorian OHS jurisdiction difference

Our chemicals include strippers and acids/alkalis that can burn skin and eyes and give off fumes. The Safety Data Sheet tells you how to use them safely — it is not optional reading.

Under the model WHS framework, a workplace must keep current SDS (not more than five years old) and a hazardous chemicals register, accessible to workers. The sections you use most on the job are: hazards, PPE, first aid, handling and storage, and spill response.

Jurisdiction flag: most states follow the model WHS framework, but Victoria operates under its own OHS Act and Regulations. Check the rules for the state you are working in.

lessons.title: PPE and the hierarchy of controls
lessons.contentBody:
PPE is the last resort in the hierarchy of controls — used after elimination, substitution, isolation and engineering controls have been considered. PPE supplements those higher controls; it does not replace them.

Select your PPE from the product SDS — it specifies gloves, eye protection and respiratory protection for that chemical. Check PPE condition before use and carry spares.

Ventilation matters too: a workplace must keep airborne contaminants below the relevant exposure standard. The SDS carries that exposure information — read it to judge whether you need extra ventilation, especially in occupied or child-occupied rooms.

lessons.title: Labelling, storage and spills
lessons.contentBody:
Labelling: every container must be labelled, including decanted ones — on the body of the container, clearly visible. The only exception is a chemical used immediately after decanting where the container is then cleaned out. If a decant is left for later or for another worker, it must be labelled.

Storage: segregate incompatible chemicals (acids vs alkalis, oxidisers) per the SDS storage advice — on the vehicle and on site.

Spills: keep a spill kit available; the SDS gives spill and disposal advice. Contain, clean up and dispose per the SDS and the site emergency plan.

The “never” list: no unlabelled bottles; never mix chemicals; never leave a decant for another worker unlabelled.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: WHS, PPE and SDS
Recognising the common surfaces
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • recognise the common floor surfaces
  • determine porosity and whether a surface is sealed
  • choose safe, non-damaging chemistry and test an inconspicuous area first

The fastest way to cause expensive damage is to use the wrong chemical or pad on a surface. Start every job by identifying what you are working on. Common surfaces:

  • vinyl, VCT and linoleum;
  • rubber;
  • concrete (sealed or unsealed);
  • terrazzo;
  • ceramic and porcelain tile;
  • natural stone;
  • timber and engineered timber;
  • epoxy / resin;
  • carpet and carpet tiles.

See the full surface guide in the onboarding pack (standards/floor-surface-identification.md) for how to recognise each one and the safe and unsafe chemistry for it.

lessons.title: Porosity, sealed vs unsealed, and pH
lessons.contentBody:
Two properties decide how you treat a floor:

  • Porosity — porous, semi-porous or non-porous. Porous surfaces absorb, so dwell time and chemical choice change.
  • Sealed vs unsealed — always confirm whether a surface (e.g. vinyl or concrete) is sealed before stripping or applying aggressive chemistry.

Basic chemistry: cleaners are neutral, alkaline or acidic; plus solvents, strippers, sealers and polishes. Each belongs in particular places. Two rules to remember:

  • Never use a strong acidic cleaner on natural stone — it etches.
  • Keep water and strippers off untreated timber — it is water/stripper sensitive.

Always test a small, inconspicuous area first. Cure, dilution and slip-rating figures come from the SDS/TDS and the relevant standard — not from guesswork.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: surface identification

modules.title: Module 5: Cleaning, Polishing, Sealing & Maintenance Methods

lessons.title: Why method discipline matters
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • apply the method steps (dwell, agitation, extraction, rinse/neutralise and finish) with discipline
  • choose between carpet extraction and encapsulation for the situation
  • run a hard-floor strip-and-seal safely with a slip-resistance check

Method discipline is what makes the result consistent. Each step does a job:

  • Dwell time — lets the chemical work;
  • Agitation — breaks the soil free;
  • Extraction — removes soil and solution;
  • Rinsing / neutralising — removes residue so the floor does not re-soil or stay slippery;
  • Polishing / sealing — finishes and protects.

Skipping dwell, rinsing or neutralising is what causes streaking, residue, fast re-soiling and slip problems.

lessons.title: Carpet: extraction vs encapsulation
lessons.contentBody:
Two common carpet methods, and a trade-off:

  • Hot water extraction (HWE) — deeper soil removal, but longer dry time.
  • Encapsulation — low-moisture, faster return to service — useful in occupied rooms.

In an occupied school or care room, the dry-time vs soil-removal trade-off often points to encapsulation so the space comes back into use quickly. Do not state fixed dry times — they depend on the product and conditions.

lessons.title: Hard floors, strip & seal, and slip resistance
lessons.contentBody:
Hard-floor cleaning (including tile and grout) needs the right dwell, agitation and rinse, then a check on slip resistance after cleaning or sealing.

Vinyl strip-and-seal, end to end: strip → rinse/neutralise → dry → thin sealer/finish coats. Strippers are typically alkaline/caustic — ventilation and PPE are required, and a wet or curing floor is a slip/access hazard, so isolate and sign it.

Cure, re-entry and dilution come from the SDS/TDS, not guesswork. Slip-resistance targets come from the relevant standard (AS 4663 / HB 198 / NCC). After sealing, verify the finished floor against the target class.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: methods
The machines and what they do
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • select the right machine, pad and brush for the task
  • complete a machine pre-use check
  • operate safely with RCD protection and managed leads and hoses

Floor-care machines are powerful and run on power or batteries near water and people. Know what each does and when to use it:

  • Rotary machine — scrubbing, stripping, polishing (pad/brush dependent);
  • Auto scrubber — scrub and recover in one pass;
  • Burnisher — high-speed finish/polish;
  • Wet vacuum / extractor — recover water and solution;
  • Pressure system — hard outdoor surfaces (with run-off control).

Run a pre-use check before any machine touches the floor.

lessons.title: Pads, brushes and safe operation
lessons.contentBody:
Pad and brush selection changes the result — an aggressive pad on a soft finish causes damage, the wrong brush on grout wastes effort.

Electrical and trip safety:

  • Portable equipment (floor polishers, extension leads) needs RCD / safety-switch protection; operate a portable RCD’s test button each time you plug in.
  • Check leads for damage on the pre-start; a test-and-tag program (referencing AS/NZS 3760) is how we show equipment is maintained — intervals vary by state.
  • Manage leads and hoses so they are not trip hazards, especially across paths children use.

Any unsafe act in operation is an automatic fail in assessment. When in doubt, stop.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: machinery operation
If it is not checked, it can stop the job
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • complete and sign the daily pre-start before leaving
  • keep equipment ready on daily, weekly and monthly cycles
  • report faults and tag faulty equipment out of service

A job does not fail only because of poor cleaning. It fails because the vehicle is not ready, the TruckMount cannot get to site, a machine will not start, the right tool was left behind, PPE or chemical is missing, a battery is flat, or a hose is damaged.

If it is not checked, it can stop the job. That is why the daily pre-start, completed and signed before you leave, is mandatory. It covers the vehicle, TruckMount (where fitted), machines, batteries, hoses and leads, tools, pads/brushes, PPE, signage and barriers, chemical stock, spill kit, first-aid kit and fire extinguisher.

Carry the standard spares — spare pads, fuses, hose fittings and key chemicals — so a small fault does not stop a whole job.

lessons.title: Daily, weekly and monthly maintenance, and fault reporting
lessons.contentBody:
Keep equipment ready on three cycles:

  • Daily — post-use clean-down, empty/rinse tanks, inspect pads/brushes/hoses/leads, charge batteries.
  • Weekly — deeper clean, filters, wear checks, hose fittings, lead/RCD inspection, lubrication per the manual.
  • Monthly — service items, tool-inventory reconciliation, restock signage/first-aid/spill kit, fire-extinguisher check, review electrical test-and-tag status.

Fault reporting: log the fault, tell the right person, and tag faulty equipment out of service — do not keep using it. A machine that fails its pre-start does not go to site.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: maintenance
Arrival, sign-in and the job briefing
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • run the arrival, sign-in and job-briefing sequence
  • complete an on-the-spot risk assessment
  • set up a controlled work zone with signage before the floor is wet

Most incidents and complaints come from the setup, not the cleaning. Run the arrival sequence every time:

  • park and unload per the site’s rules;
  • complete site sign-in;
  • make contact with the site contact;
  • take the job briefing — scope, access, occupancy, and water and power access.

Confirm you can physically get equipment to the work area before you start unloading everything.

lessons.title: Inspection, risk assessment and work-zone control
lessons.contentBody:
Before you start: inspect the surface and do an on-the-spot risk assessment — hazards, occupancy, children present, and your wet-floor plan.

Then set up a controlled work zone:

  • put up wet-floor signage and barricade / exclude the area before the floor is wet;
  • keep exits and pedestrian paths clear;
  • route and manage leads and hoses so they are not trip hazards;
  • ventilate occupied or child-occupied rooms when using chemicals;
  • place chemicals where they are secure and never within reach of children.

Remember: signage is an administrative control that supplements separating people from wet areas — it is not the primary control.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: site setup
The professionalism standard
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • meet the presentation and conduct standard on a school or childcare site
  • communicate delays, risks and issues early and plainly
  • protect the contract through professional behaviour

On a school or childcare site, how we behave is as visible as how we clean. Professional conduct is what earns the trust that keeps the contract. The standard covers:

  • company uniform, personal hygiene and presentation;
  • behaviour and language on site;
  • respect for children, staff, teachers, visitors, tenants and facility users;
  • vehicle cleanliness and where you park;
  • where you unload, where chemicals go, and how hoses and leads are run.

See the full professionalism standard in the onboarding pack (standards/professionalism-conduct.md).

lessons.title: Presentation, communication and trust
lessons.contentBody:
Control your work zone so it does not disrupt site operations — classes, drop-off, deliveries. If a teacher needs you to move for a class, respond politely and adjust.

Communicate delays, risks and issues early and plainly. A site contact who hears about a delay early stays a happy client; one who finds out late does not.

Professionalism is not extra — it is the difference between a job we did and a contract we keep.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: professional conduct
Confidentiality and the photo rule
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • apply the photo rule and Privacy Act obligations around identifiable people
  • respect restricted areas and child-safe conduct
  • work as though CCTV is always operating

We work inside other people’s facilities, often around children and private information. One photo of a screen or a child, or one careless comment, can breach trust and the law.

Images of an identifiable person are personal information under the Privacy Act 1988, collected only where reasonably necessary. So the default is clear:

  • do not discuss private site information;
  • do not photograph people, children, documents, screens or private information;
  • when you need a before/after photo, frame out people and private information — if you cannot, record why in the notes instead.

lessons.title: Security, restricted areas and assume-CCTV conduct
lessons.contentBody:
Keep work zones clean, controlled and secure. Do not prop or block secure doors. Respect restricted areas. On child-occupied sites, follow child-safe conduct — never be alone with a child. A Working With Children check is a starting point, not the whole obligation; it sits alongside the child-safe code of conduct.

Assume CCTV is operating. Behave as though every action is visible to the client. That single habit protects you and the company’s reputation.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: confidentiality and security
Final inspection, pack-down and evidence
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • complete a final inspection and pack-down
  • capture compliant photo evidence and clear completion notes
  • report defects, escalate and hand over for sign-off

If we cannot show what we did, we cannot defend the work or get paid cleanly. Finish every job properly:

  • final inspection against the job scope;
  • pack-down — equipment, leads, hoses and signage, and leave the area clean;
  • photo evidence within the confidentiality rules;
  • clear, complete completion notes.

If you find pre-existing damage during the final inspection, photograph and record it so it is not wrongly attributed to us.

lessons.title: Defect reporting, escalation and handover
lessons.contentBody:
Report defects and anything outside the standard, and follow the escalation process for anything you cannot resolve on site (unexpected floor condition, equipment failure, access problem, confidentiality conflict).

Then hand over to the client/site contact and get sign-off where required. A clean handover is what turns a finished job into a renewed contract.

Escalation contacts and timeframes are set by the company — know yours before you go solo.

lessons.title: Knowledge check: completion and handover
What the final assessment covers
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • demonstrate a full standard job end to end to the 85%/100%-safety-critical pass bar
  • talk through judgement in scenario walk-throughs, including when to stop and escalate
  • earn independent-operator clearance

This is the gate to working solo. It proves the standard holds when no one is guiding each step. You will run a full standard job, end to end, assessed by a supervisor against the checklists and standards:

  • vehicle and equipment readiness;
  • site arrival, sign-in, briefing and setup;
  • surface identification and the correct method and chemical;
  • safe machine operation and hazard control;
  • professional conduct and confidentiality/security;
  • close-out with evidence and a clear handover.

Pass: at least 85% overall and 100% on every safety-critical and reputation-critical item — no automatic-fail acts.

lessons.title: Scenario walk-throughs
lessons.contentBody:
Be ready to talk through judgement, not just steps:

  • Mid-job you find the surface is not what the scope said. What do you change, and who do you tell? (Stop before damage, document, confirm a revised method or re-scope.)
  • A flat battery stops your scrubber halfway. What is your recovery and escalation? (Switch to a backup if in scope; otherwise notify, complete what is safe, re-book.)
  • You need a photo but private information is in shot. What do you do? (Frame it out or record why no photo was taken.)

Knowing when to stop and escalate is part of passing.

lessons.title: Independent-operator clearance
lessons.contentBody:
When you pass Module 12 and all 12 modules are signed off, your supervisor signs the independent-operator clearance — you are cleared to run standard jobs on your own, with named escalation paths for anything outside the standard.

Independence is the start of the standard, not the end of development. Expect refreshers on the safety-critical modules and ongoing toolbox talks drawn from real jobs.

There is no time pressure to pass. Competence comes before the calendar — the clearance is signed when the work earns it.

courses.title: Australian H5 Bird Flu Awareness for Restoration, IAQ and Facility Professionals
courses.description:
This course helps Australian restoration, cleaning, indoor air quality, facility, strata, insurance-support and professional remediation teams communicate calmly, report correctly, protect workers, stay within scope, document field work and avoid unsupported claims about H5 bird flu.

It is for professionals who may receive questions about sick or dead birds, field documentation, building contamination concerns or client communication during the current Australian H5 bird flu detections in migratory seabirds.

This course is not veterinary training, wildlife handling training, public-health diagnosis, government biosecurity response training or product endorsement training. It follows the official Australian Government and Australian Centre for Disease Control reporting pathway: do not touch sick or dead birds, record what you see and report to the Emergency Animal Disease Hotline on 1800 675 888.
courses.shortDescription: Report correctly, protect workers, document field work and communicate calmly about H5 bird flu — professional-boundary awareness training for Australian restoration, IAQ and facility teams.

meta.officialSourceRecheck: 2026-07-05: DAFF/Australian CDC/Animal Health Australia guidance reconfirmed current — migratory seabird detections only (WA, SA), no poultry, public risk low, hotline 1800 675 888 correct. Recheck again before publish per lms-import-plan.md gate 1.

lessons.title: Official message and reporting pathway — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this course you will be able to:

  • Report a suspected H5 bird flu case through the correct Australian pathway.
  • Apply worker-safety, PPE-awareness and clean/dirty-zone practices on site.
  • Document field work accurately and within your professional scope.
  • Communicate calmly and correctly with clients, media and the public.
  • Recognise the professional boundaries of the role — not diagnosing bird flu, not handling wildlife without authorisation, and not certifying sites as disease-free.

Australia has reported H5 bird flu detections in migratory seabirds. The professional communication position is calm and evidence-led: follow Australian Government and Australian Centre for Disease Control (CDC) advice, avoid speculation and direct the public to the correct reporting pathway.

If a sick or dead bird or animal is found, the public message is simple:

  1. Do not touch it.
  2. Avoid the area.
  3. Keep children and pets away.
  4. Record the location and what is seen.
  5. Take photos or video only if safe.
  6. Report to the Emergency Animal Disease Hotline on 1800 675 888.
Report first. Do not touch. Record the location. Call 1800 675 888.

Professional caution: Do not tell the public that a site is infected or safe. Use neutral language: observed, reported, excluded from scope, referred to authority.

resources.label: Course audio overview — two-voice discussion (6 min)

cards.front: If a sick or dead bird or animal is found, what is the public message?
cards.back: Do not touch it. Avoid the area. Keep children and pets away. Record the location and what is seen, take photos or video only if safe, and report to the Emergency Animal Disease Hotline on 1800 675 888.

cards.front: What is the Emergency Animal Disease Hotline number?
cards.back: 1800 675 888 - the number to report a sick or dead bird or animal.

cards.front: What is the professional communication position on H5 bird flu?
cards.back: Calm and evidence-led: follow Australian Government and Australian Centre for Disease Control advice, avoid speculation, and direct the public to the correct reporting pathway.

cards.front: Why should a professional never tell the public a site is 'infected' or 'safe'?
cards.back: That judgement sits with the relevant authorities. Use neutral language instead: observed, reported, excluded from scope, referred to authority.

cards.front: What has Australia reported in relation to H5 bird flu?
cards.back: H5 bird flu detections in migratory seabirds.

cards.front: What short reminder captures the reporting pathway?
cards.back: Report first. Do not touch. Record the location. Call 1800 675 888.

cards.front: Whose advice should professionals follow on H5 bird flu?
cards.back: Australian Government and Australian Centre for Disease Control (CDC) advice.

resources.label: Course video overview — narrated slides (7 min)

slides.title: The official position
bullets: Australia has reported H5 bird flu detections in migratory seabirds.
The professional communication position is calm and evidence-led.
Follow Australian Government and Australian CDC advice.
Avoid speculation and direct the public to the correct reporting pathway.
slides.speakerNotes: Australia has recorded H5 bird flu detections in migratory seabirds, and our job is to stay calm and evidence-led. That means following Australian Government and Australian Centre for Disease Control advice, avoiding speculation, and pointing people to the correct reporting pathway.

slides.title: If you find a sick or dead bird or animal
bullets: Do not touch it.
Avoid the area.
Keep children and pets away.
Record the location and what is seen.
Take photos or video only if safe.
Report to the Emergency Animal Disease Hotline on 1800 675 888.
slides.speakerNotes: If a sick or dead bird or animal is found, the public message is simple. Do not touch it, avoid the area, and keep children and pets away. Then record the location, take photos or video only if it is safe, and report to the Emergency Animal Disease Hotline on 1800 675 888.

slides.title: Report first
bullets: Report first.
Do not touch.
Record the location.
slides.speakerNotes: The whole pathway compresses into four words: report first, do not touch, record the location, and call 1800 675 888. If you remember nothing else from this module, remember that sequence.

slides.title: Use neutral, professional language
bullets: Do not tell the public that a site is infected.
Do not tell the public that a site is safe.
Use neutral language: observed, reported, excluded from scope, referred to authority.
slides.speakerNotes: A key professional caution: never tell the public a site is infected, and never tell them it is safe. Stick to neutral, defensible language such as observed, reported, excluded from scope, or referred to authority.

slides.title: Support, not authority replacement
bullets: Members may assist property owners, facility and strata managers, insurers or contractors.
The role is support, not authority replacement.
Official disease response sits with the relevant authorities.
slides.speakerNotes: Restoration and IAQ members are often asked to help property owners, facility and strata managers, insurers or contractors. Our role here is support, not replacing anyone in authority, because official disease response sits with the relevant government, veterinary, wildlife and public-health bodies.

slides.title: What professionals can assist with
bullets: Site notes and photo records.
Access control and exclusion-zone records.
Worker-safety planning and PPE record keeping.
Cleaning records and product-label and SDS record checks.
slides.speakerNotes: There is plenty we can legitimately do. That includes site notes and photo records, access control and exclusion-zone records, worker-safety planning, PPE record keeping, cleaning records, and checking product-label and SDS records.

slides.title: Assisting continues
bullets: Client communication notes.
RestoreAssist.app reporting.
Escalation to the correct authority pathway.
slides.speakerNotes: Our support role also covers writing up client communication notes, capturing the job in RestoreAssist.app, and escalating to the correct authority pathway. In every case we are documenting and routing, not deciding disease outcomes.

slides.title: What professionals must not do
bullets: Handle wildlife without authorisation.
Diagnose avian influenza.
Certify a site as disease-free.
Override government, veterinary, wildlife or public-health authorities.
Claim a product or dry-fogging method is required without authority direction and product evidence.
slides.speakerNotes: There are firm boundaries. Do not handle wildlife without authorisation, do not diagnose avian influenza, and do not certify a site as disease-free. Never override the authorities, and never claim a product or dry-fogging method is required unless written authority direction and product evidence support it.

slides.title: Our role in one line
bullets: Document.
Protect workers.
Communicate clearly.
Leave official disease response to the relevant authorities.
slides.speakerNotes: In a single line: our role is to document, protect workers and communicate clearly. Official disease response stays with the relevant authorities, and holding that boundary is what keeps us credible.

decks.module: Worker safety, PPE awareness and clean/dirty zones

slides.title: Start with a WHS risk assessment
bullets: Complete a site-specific WHS risk assessment before any site work.
Determine what has been observed.
Check whether authorities have been contacted and whether the site is under direction.
Confirm the work is within the contractor's scope.
slides.speakerNotes: Before anyone starts work, complete a site-specific WHS risk assessment. Establish what has been observed, whether authorities have already been contacted, whether the site is under direction, and whether the work actually falls within your scope.

slides.title: Use clean and dirty zones
bullets: Use clean and dirty zones on site.
Zones reduce confusion.
Zones reduce cross-contamination risk.
slides.speakerNotes: Clean and dirty zones are a simple but powerful control. They cut down confusion about where you are in the process and reduce the risk of cross-contamination between areas.

slides.title: PPE is matched, not prescribed
bullets: This course does not prescribe a universal PPE set.
PPE must be matched to task, risk, authority guidance, product label and SDS.
Records should show PPE selected, who wore it, and any breaches.
Records should show what waste or equipment movement was excluded from the job.
slides.speakerNotes: There is no universal PPE set in this course, because PPE has to be matched to the task, the risk, authority guidance, and the product label and SDS. What matters is that your records show what PPE was selected, who wore it, whether there were any breaches, and what waste or equipment movement was excluded.

slides.title: What to document
bullets: Who attended and what was observed.
Whether sick or dead birds or animals were present.
Whether the hotline or authority pathway was used.
PPE selected, access restrictions and client instructions.
Work not performed, plus photos and timestamps.
slides.speakerNotes: Your documentation should capture who attended, what was observed, and whether any sick or dead birds or animals were present. Record whether the hotline or authority pathway was used, the PPE selected, access restrictions, client instructions, any work not performed, and photos with timestamps.

slides.title: If it is not documented, it cannot be reviewed
bullets: Records make the work reviewable.
If it is not documented, it cannot be reviewed.
slides.speakerNotes: The guiding principle here is straightforward: if it is not documented, it cannot be reviewed. Good records are what make your safety decisions defensible later.

slides.title: Clean first, disinfect second, document third
bullets: CARSI's training position is simple.
Clean first.
Disinfect second.
Document third.
slides.speakerNotes: CARSI's training position on this is deliberately simple. Clean first, disinfect second, and document third. That order is the backbone of every job.

slides.title: Disinfection claims have limits
bullets: Disinfection claims must never exceed the product label.
They must never exceed the SDS or the regulator position.
They must never exceed the evidence base.
slides.speakerNotes: Disinfection claims are bounded. They must never go beyond the product label, the SDS, the regulator position, or the underlying evidence base. When in doubt, claim less.

slides.title: Dry fogging: an adjunct, never required
bullets: Hydrogen-peroxide dry fogging includes Halosil or HaloFogger-style systems.
It must not be described as required by Australian Government H5 control measures without written authority direction and product evidence.
It may only be an adjunct where legal, label-supported, risk-assessed and unoccupied where required.
It is performed by trained operators after source removal and cleaning.
slides.speakerNotes: Hydrogen-peroxide dry fogging, including Halosil or HaloFogger-style systems, must never be described as required by Australian Government H5 control measures unless written authority direction and product evidence support that. At most it is a possible adjunct method, and only where it is legal, label-supported, risk-assessed, unoccupied where required, and performed by trained operators after source removal and cleaning.

slides.title: Product training stays label- and SDS-led
bullets: NeoSan or any other product training must remain label-led and SDS-led.
This holds until approved H5 or relevant organism claims are verified.
Product training is not product hype.
Use the label, SDS, evidence and authority direction.
slides.speakerNotes: NeoSan, or any other product training, stays label-led and SDS-led until approved H5 or relevant-organism claims are actually verified. Product training is not product hype; we lean on the label, the SDS, the evidence and authority direction.

slides.title: The documentation pathway
bullets: RestoreAssist.app is the documentation pathway for campaign field records.
A professional file lets a client, insurer, supervisor or authority understand the job.
It shows what was observed, reported, done, not done, and what evidence supported the decision.
slides.speakerNotes: RestoreAssist.app is the documentation pathway for campaign field records. A well-built file lets a client, insurer, supervisor or authority quickly understand what was observed, what was reported, what was done and not done, and what evidence backed each decision.

slides.title: Suggested record sections
bullets: Intake notes.
Site address and contact.
Observation notes.
Authority and reporting record.
Photos and captions.
slides.speakerNotes: A good record starts with intake notes, the site address and contact, and your observation notes. It also captures the authority and reporting record, plus photos with clear captions.

slides.title: Suggested record sections continued
bullets: Worker attendance and PPE record.
Access control.
Cleaning record.
Product and SDS record.
Exclusion and limitation notes, client communication, and a completion statement.
slides.speakerNotes: The file then covers worker attendance and PPE, access control, the cleaning record, and the product and SDS record. Finish with exclusion and limitation notes, client communication, and a clear completion statement.

slides.title: From memory to defensible record
bullets: RestoreAssist turns the job from a memory into a defensible record.
slides.speakerNotes: The point of all of this is simple. RestoreAssist turns the job from a memory into a defensible record that stands up to review.

slides.title: A measured tone
bullets: This is a public-interest and professional-readiness campaign.
The tone must be measured.
Do not use crisis, outbreak or emergency unless those are the exact words used by authorities for the current situation.
slides.speakerNotes: This is a public-interest and professional-readiness campaign, so the tone must stay measured. Avoid words like crisis, outbreak or emergency unless those are the exact terms the authorities are using for the current situation.

slides.title: Use approved wording
bullets: We follow Australian Government advice.
Do not touch sick or dead birds.
Report to 1800 675 888.
Our role is documentation, worker safety and professional support.
We do not replace government, veterinary, wildlife or public-health authorities. Product use must follow label, SDS and authority requirements.
slides.speakerNotes: Lean on approved wording. We follow Australian Government advice, do not touch sick or dead birds, and report to 1800 675 888. Our role is documentation, worker safety and professional support; we do not replace the authorities, and product use follows the label, SDS and authority requirements.

slides.title: What to avoid
bullets: Fear-based headlines and unsupported claims.
Fake statistics, fake expert quotes and fake portraits.
Unapproved personal contact details.
Product claims that exceed evidence.
slides.speakerNotes: There is a clear avoid list. Steer away from fear-based headlines and unsupported claims, and never use fake statistics, fake expert quotes or fake portraits. Do not publish unapproved personal contact details, and never make product claims that exceed the evidence.

slides.title: Calm language builds trust
bullets: Calm language builds trust.
Panic language damages credibility.
slides.speakerNotes: The takeaway is short. Calm language builds trust, while panic language damages credibility, so choose your words with that trade-off in mind.

slides.title: Confirm the professional-boundary statement
bullets: Before the knowledge check, confirm you understand the professional-boundary statement.
You will be asked to apply it while answering the quiz questions.
slides.speakerNotes: Before the knowledge check, take a moment to confirm you understand the professional-boundary statement. You will be asked to apply it directly while you answer the quiz questions.

slides.title: The boundary statement
bullets: My role is to report, protect, document and communicate within scope.
I do not diagnose bird flu or handle wildlife without authorisation.
I do not certify sites as disease-free.
I do not make product claims that are not supported by label, SDS, regulator position and evidence.
slides.speakerNotes: The statement is your anchor. Your role is to report, protect, document and communicate within scope. You do not diagnose bird flu, handle wildlife without authorisation, certify sites as disease-free, or make product claims that the label, SDS, regulator position and evidence do not support.

slides.title: What this training is not
bullets: Awareness training only.
Not veterinary or medical training.
Not wildlife-handling training.
Not government biosecurity response training.
slides.speakerNotes: Be clear about the certificate's scope. This is awareness training only. It is not veterinary, medical, wildlife-handling or government biosecurity response training.

slides.title: Apply it in the knowledge check
bullets: Hold the boundary statement in mind as you answer.
Answer each question within scope.
slides.speakerNotes: As you move into the knowledge check, keep the boundary statement in mind. Answer each question from within your professional scope, and you will be applying exactly what this course has taught.

lessons.title: Professional role, scope and boundaries — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Professional restoration and IAQ members may be asked to assist property owners, facility managers, strata managers, insurers or contractors. The role is support, not authority replacement.

Professionals can assist with:

  • site notes and photo records
  • access control and exclusion-zone records
  • worker-safety planning
  • PPE record keeping
  • cleaning records
  • product-label and SDS record checks
  • client communication notes
  • RestoreAssist.app reporting
  • escalation to the correct authority pathway

Professionals must not:

  • handle wildlife without authorisation
  • diagnose avian influenza
  • certify a site as disease-free
  • override government, veterinary, wildlife or public-health authorities
  • claim that a product or dry-fogging method is required unless written authority direction and product evidence support that claim
Our role is to document, protect workers and communicate clearly. Official disease response sits with the relevant authorities.

cards.front: What is the restoration or IAQ professional's role in an H5 situation?
cards.back: Support, not authority replacement. The role is to document, protect workers and communicate clearly; official disease response sits with the relevant authorities.

cards.front: Name tasks a professional can assist with.
cards.back: Site notes and photo records, access control and exclusion-zone records, worker-safety planning, PPE record keeping, cleaning records, product-label and SDS record checks, client communication notes, RestoreAssist.app reporting, and escalation to the correct authority pathway.

cards.front: What must professionals not do?
cards.back: Handle wildlife without authorisation, diagnose avian influenza, certify a site as disease-free, override government/veterinary/wildlife/public-health authorities, or claim a product or dry-fogging method is required without written authority direction and product evidence.

cards.front: Can a professional certify a site as disease-free?
cards.back: No. Certifying a site as disease-free is outside scope; official disease response sits with the relevant authorities.

cards.front: Who might a restoration or IAQ professional be asked to assist?
cards.back: Property owners, facility managers, strata managers, insurers or contractors.

cards.front: Under what condition may a professional claim a dry-fogging method is required?
cards.back: Only where written authority direction and product evidence support that claim - never as a default assumption.

cards.front: What three actions summarise the professional's proper role?
cards.back: Document, protect workers, and communicate clearly.

modules.title: Worker safety, PPE awareness and clean/dirty zones

lessons.title: Worker safety, PPE awareness and clean/dirty zones — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Before any site work, complete a site-specific WHS risk assessment. Determine what has been observed, whether authorities have been contacted, whether the site is under direction and whether the work is within the contractor's scope.

Use clean and dirty zones to reduce confusion and cross-contamination risk. This course does not prescribe a universal PPE set because PPE must be matched to task, risk, authority guidance, product label and SDS. However, records should show what PPE was selected, who wore it, whether there were breaches and what waste or equipment movement was excluded from the job.

Documentation fields:

  • Who attended
  • What was observed
  • Whether sick or dead birds or animals were present
  • Whether the hotline or authority pathway was used
  • PPE selected
  • Access restrictions
  • Client instructions
  • Work not performed
  • Photos and timestamps
If it is not documented, it cannot be reviewed.

cards.front: What must be completed before any site work?
cards.back: A site-specific WHS risk assessment.

cards.front: Why does this course not prescribe a universal PPE set?
cards.back: Because PPE must be matched to the task, risk, authority guidance, product label and SDS.

cards.front: What is the purpose of clean and dirty zones?
cards.back: To reduce confusion and cross-contamination risk.

cards.front: What should PPE records show?
cards.back: What PPE was selected, who wore it, whether there were breaches, and what waste or equipment movement was excluded from the job.

cards.front: What does the site-specific WHS risk assessment help you determine?
cards.back: What has been observed, whether authorities have been contacted, whether the site is under direction, and whether the work is within the contractor's scope.

cards.front: List some documentation fields for a site.
cards.back: Who attended, what was observed, whether sick or dead birds or animals were present, whether the hotline or authority pathway was used, PPE selected, access restrictions, client instructions, work not performed, and photos with timestamps.

cards.front: What principle sums up the importance of records in this module?
cards.back: If it is not documented, it cannot be reviewed.

lessons.title: Cleaning-before-disinfection and product-claim limits — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
CARSI's training position is simple: clean first, disinfect second, document third. Disinfection claims must never exceed the product label, SDS, regulator position or evidence base.

Hydrogen-peroxide dry fogging, including Halosil or HaloFogger-style systems, must not be described as required by Australian Government H5 control measures unless a written authority direction and product evidence support that claim. This course may discuss dry fogging only as a possible adjunct method where legal, label-supported, risk-assessed, unoccupied where required, and performed by trained operators after source removal and cleaning.

NeoSan or any other product training must remain label-led and SDS-led until approved H5 or relevant organism claims are verified.

Product training is not product hype. Use the label, SDS, evidence and authority direction.

cards.front: What is CARSI's training position on the order of work?
cards.back: Clean first, disinfect second, document third.

cards.front: What limits apply to disinfection claims?
cards.back: They must never exceed the product label, SDS, regulator position or evidence base.

cards.front: Can hydrogen-peroxide dry fogging (e.g. Halosil or HaloFogger-style systems) be described as required by Australian Government H5 control measures?
cards.back: No - not unless a written authority direction and product evidence support that claim.

cards.front: Under what conditions may dry fogging be discussed as a possible adjunct method?
cards.back: Only where it is legal, label-supported, risk-assessed, unoccupied where required, and performed by trained operators after source removal and cleaning.

cards.front: How must NeoSan or any other product training be approached?
cards.back: It must remain label-led and SDS-led until approved H5 or relevant organism claims are verified.

cards.front: What separates product training from product hype?
cards.back: Product training is led by the label, SDS, evidence and authority direction; it is not hype.

lessons.title: Client, media and social communication — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
This campaign is a public-interest and professional-readiness campaign. The tone must be measured. Do not use terms such as crisis, outbreak or emergency unless those are the exact words used by authorities for the current situation.

Use approved wording:

  • We follow Australian Government advice.
  • Do not touch sick or dead birds.
  • Report to 1800 675 888.

Avoid:

  • unsupported claims

Calm language builds trust. Panic language damages credibility.

courses.title: Truckmount Operations Course — HydraMaster & Sapphire Scientific (Australian configuration)
courses.description: A specialised operator course for petrol-engine truck-mount systems (HydraMaster and Sapphire Scientific) run in Australian configuration. Covers the manufacturer’s truck-mount safety certificate and the Australian regulatory map (NHVR/Main Roads, WHS, pressure, fuel), then six units: Australian configurations and specifications, safe operating procedures (carbon monoxide as the critical hazard), storage / transport / road safety (load restraint, GVM, fuel and chemical carriage), maintenance and servicing, a reference image catalogue, and an assessment with a trainer pre-flight verification gate and operator competency sign-off. Sourced from manufacturer manuals and Australian authorities (NHVR, NTC, Safe Work Australia, state transport and water authorities). Co-produced by CARSI with Carpet Cleaners Warehouse (CCW).
courses.shortDescription: Specialised HydraMaster & Sapphire Scientific truck-mount operator course — Australian configuration, safe operation, transport and road-safety compliance, maintenance, and a competency sign-off. A CARSI × Carpet Cleaners Warehouse course.

lessons.title: Start here — choose your machine
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Identify the exact machine you operate - its make (HydraMaster or Sapphire Scientific), model, serial number and engine - using the specification/serial plate.
  • Explain why these machines are not 240 V mains-powered: an onboard petrol engine drives the pump and blower and the heat comes from the engine exhaust, with 12 V DC onboard electrics.
  • Locate your model in the spec reference and recognise that model- and engine-specific setpoints must be read against the unit manual.

Start here — choose your machine

This course covers petrol-engine truck-mount systems from HydraMaster and Sapphire Scientific in their Australian configuration. Before you begin, identify the exact machine you operate — the safety-critical setpoints (temperature, working pressure, RPM, service intervals) are model- and engine-specific, so everything that follows must be read against your unit's manual.

The power myth, first: these machines do not plug into 240 V mains. An onboard petrol engine drives the pump and blower and generates the heat from its own exhaust; the onboard electrics are 12 V DC. There is no US-120 V → AU-240 V conversion on the machine — the Australia-vs-US difference is distributor, warranty and fuel, not voltage.

How to identify your machine

  1. Find the specification / serial plate on the unit (usually on the frame or engine shroud).
  2. Note the make (HydraMaster or Sapphire Scientific), the model (e.g. Boxxer 318HP, TITAN 425, Sapphire 370SS, Apex 570), and the serial number.
  3. Note the engine make and model (Briggs & Stratton, Kohler, Kawasaki or Kubota) — service intervals and oil grades follow the engine, not just the truck-mount.
  4. Keep the unit's own operator manual to hand; it is the final authority on every figure in this course.

Your machine — spec reference

Use this to locate your model. Figures are sourced from manufacturer/distributor pages; items marked ⚠ confirm must be checked against the specific unit's manual before you rely on them. For current pricing and availability, contact CCW or your distributor — this course teaches operation and compliance, not purchasing.

HydraMaster Boxxer 423SB&S Vanguard 23 HP petrol1000 psi (69 bar), 15.1 L/min≈113 °CDominator 4005, 300+ CFM265 L / 255 kg
HydraMaster TMTG4000B&S Vanguard 23 HP1000 psi (69 bar), 13.2 L/min≈116 °C ⚠ US-specTuthill Dominator 4005, 350+ CFM⚠ confirm
HydraMaster TITAN 375/425/575HP/625⚠ confirm per model⚠ confirm (575HP adds 2000 psi wash)⚠ confirm⚠ confirm⚠ confirm
Sapphire 370SS / 370EFI23 HP V-Twin (Kohler/Kawasaki — confirm)1500 psi (103 bar), 12.9–13.5 L/min121 °C thermostaticTri-Lobe, 317 CFM340 L / 296 kg
Sapphire Apex 570Kubota WG972 31 HP1500 psi (103 bar), 18.9 L/min⚠ confirmHeliFlow HF408, 455 CFM341 L
