lessons.title: Unit 3.6 — Assessment & Competency Sign-Off — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Complete Gate A trainer pre-flight, confirming every model- and engine-specific setpoint against the actual unit and engine manual and the operator's State before the course is delivered.
  • Assess operator competency under Gate B by demonstrating each competency on the actual machine, marked competent or not-yet-competent.
  • Apply the zero-tolerance carbon monoxide rule - a not-yet-competent mark on any CO-control item fails the whole assessment.

Unit 3.6 — Assessment & Operator Competency Sign-Off

Course: Truck-Mount Operator Course (Module 03) This unit certifies that an operator can run the specific machine safely. It has two gates: a trainer pre-flight (the course cannot be delivered on wrong data) and an operator competency assessment (the operator cannot be signed off unproven).

Why this gate exists. Units 3.1–3.4 are Australianised and sourced, but every safety-critical setpoint (water temperature, working pressure, RPM, service intervals, torque) is model- and engine-specific and several compliance points are flagged ⚠ CONFIRM. This course is training reference, not a substitute for the unit's manual. No operator is signed off, and the course is not taught as fact, until Gate A below is complete for the actual machine in front of them.

Gate A — Trainer pre-flight (complete BEFORE delivering the course)

The trainer confirms the course content against the specific unit + engine manual and the operator's State. Tick every row; attach the source.

#Item to confirmSource to checkConfirmed
A1Exact safe water/solution temperature and any over-temp cutoutUnit manual (the 370 SS manual states only "adjust to desired temperature" — no fixed °C)☐
A2Max working pressure for this unit (TITAN warranty caps at 1,000 psi; some units run to 1,500 psi)Unit manual☐
A3Engine oil interval + grade, pump-oil and blower-oil grades/part numbersEngine + unit manuals☐
A4Water hardness for the operator's supply, and softener/descale requirementLocal water test / authority☐
A5Load-restraint guide applicable to the operator's vehicle (light ≤4.5 t vs heavy) + the exact light-vehicle performance wordingNHVR / NTC guide☐
A6Load-vs-modification status of the install (cargo vs permanent mod) + rego/insurance positionVehicle signatory (see Module 02)☐
A7Per-State petrol "tools of trade" limit and jerrican classState EPA / WorkSafe☐
A8Trade-waste disposal point + permit for the operator's areaLocal water authority☐
A9CO exposure standard current value (30 ppm, reducing to 20 ppm in 2026) and alarm setpoint (35 ppm)Safe Work Australia☐

Trainer: ______________ Unit make/model/serial: ______________ Date: ________ Course must not be delivered as fact until every A-row is confirmed for this unit.

Gate B — Operator competency assessment

The operator demonstrates each competency on the actual machine. Mark C (competent) or NYC (not yet competent). All must be C to sign off.

B1 — Machine knowledge (Unit 3.1)

  • States how the machine is powered (petrol engine + 12 V DC, not 240 V mains)
  • Identifies the model, reads the spec plate (pressure, heat, vacuum)
  • Names CCW as the AU service/warranty channel

B2 — Safe operation (Unit 3.2)

  • Performs the full pre-start walk-around (all fluid levels, fuel, hoses, belts, CO/exhaust)
  • Executes the startup sequence in correct order (water on → engine → pressure; no dry-run)
  • Primes the chemical system correctly
  • Sets pressure/temperature for the tool and fibre
  • Executes the shutdown sequence in correct order (chemical flush → cool-down → depressurise → water off → drain)
  • Demonstrates pressure relief before opening any fitting

B3 — Carbon monoxide (Unit 3.2) — critical, zero-tolerance

  • States CO is odourless/colourless and lethal in minutes
  • Positions the vehicle outdoors with exhaust aimed away from all openings before starting
  • Fits/checks a CO monitor and states the 35 ppm alarm → evacuate + shut down rule
  • States the AU exposure standard (30 → 20 ppm) and the never-remain-in-van rule
  • States the never-enter-to-rescue-without-ventilating rule (A NYC on any B3 item is an automatic fail of the whole assessment.)

B4 — Transport, storage & road safety (Unit 3.3)

  • Secures the unit/tanks to the load-restraint standard (0.8 g forward)
  • States GVM/axle check with full water and the rego/insurance modification trap
  • Stores fuel and chemicals correctly (approved containers, SDS in vehicle, not in cabin)
  • States lawful trade-waste disposal (approved sewer point, never stormwater)

B5 — Maintenance (Unit 3.4)

  • Completes the daily maintenance walk-around
  • States the 5/50/100/500/1,000-hour anchors
  • Tests water hardness and states when a softener + descale is mandatory
  • Diagnoses low heat, low pressure and loss of vacuum from symptoms

B6 — Emergencies (Unit 3.2)

  • Correct response to a CO alarm, HP hose burst, engine shutdown, and petrol spill/fire

Sign-off

I confirm the above operator has been assessed on the specific machine and is competent to operate it unsupervised, and that Gate A was completed for this unit.

  • Operator name / signature: ______________________ Date: ________
  • Assessor name / signature: ______________________ Date: ________
  • Unit make / model / serial: ______________________
  • Re-assessment due: ____________ (recommended annually or on change of machine/model)

Training material — not legal advice and not a substitute for the manufacturer's manual or a licensed practitioner. Sign-off certifies operator competency on this unit, not regulatory approval of the vehicle or its installation (see Module 02).

Key takeaways

  • The unit has two gates: a trainer pre-flight (Gate A) and an operator competency assessment (Gate B); no operator is signed off until Gate A is complete for the actual machine.
  • Every safety-critical setpoint (water temperature, working pressure, RPM, service intervals, torque) is model- and engine-specific, so the course is training reference, not a substitute for the unit's manual.
  • Carbon monoxide control is zero-tolerance - any not-yet-competent on a B3 item is an automatic fail regardless of the total score.
  • Sign-off records the machine make, model and serial number and the assessor's name; re-assessment is recommended annually or on any change of machine or model.

cards.front: What two gates does the operator competency unit require before an operator is signed off?
cards.back: Gate A — a trainer pre-flight completed BEFORE the course is delivered, confirming content against the specific unit + engine manual and the operator's State. Gate B — an operator competency assessment demonstrated on the actual machine. No operator is signed off, and the course is not taught as fact, until Gate A is complete for the actual machine.

cards.front: Why does the Gate A trainer pre-flight exist, and what does it confirm?
cards.back: Because every safety-critical setpoint (water temperature, working pressure, RPM, service intervals, torque) is model- and engine-specific and several compliance points are flagged confirm. The trainer must tick every row — safe water/solution temperature, max working pressure, oil intervals/grades, water hardness, applicable load-restraint guide, load-vs-modification status, per-State petrol limit, trade-waste point/permit, and the current CO exposure standard — and attach the source, before delivering the course as fact.

cards.front: In the operator assessment, how is the carbon monoxide section (B3) scored?
cards.back: B3 is critical and zero-tolerance: a 'not yet competent' (NYC) on ANY B3 item is an automatic fail of the whole assessment. The operator must state CO is odourless/colourless and lethal in minutes, position the vehicle outdoors with exhaust aimed away before starting, fit/check a CO monitor and state the 35 ppm alarm → evacuate + shut down rule, state the AU exposure standard (30 → 20 ppm) and the never-remain-in-van rule, and the never-enter-to-rescue-without-ventilating rule.

cards.front: What does the operator sign-off certify — and what does it NOT certify?
cards.back: It certifies that the operator has been assessed on the specific machine and is competent to operate it unsupervised, and that Gate A was completed for that unit. It does NOT certify regulatory approval of the vehicle or its installation (that sits with Module 02). It is training material, not legal advice and not a substitute for the manufacturer's manual or a licensed practitioner.

cards.front: When is operator re-assessment due?
cards.back: Recommended annually or on any change of machine/model.

cards.front: What CO figures must the trainer confirm at Gate A item A9?
cards.back: The current CO exposure standard value — 30 ppm, reducing to 20 ppm in 2026 — and the alarm setpoint of 35 ppm, confirmed against Safe Work Australia.

lessons.title: Knowledge check
Assessment & competency sign-off — your CARSI record
lessons.contentBody:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Explain how completing the course creates a per-learner competency record stored in CARSI - your enrolment, your progress, your knowledge-check result and the trainer's practical sign-off.
  • Describe the two-gate sign-off (Gate A trainer pre-flight and Gate B operator competency) and the zero-tolerance carbon monoxide criterion.
  • State what is recorded on a pass - the machine make, model and serial number and the assessor's name.

Assessment & competency sign-off — your CARSI record

This course is not download-only. Completing it creates a per-learner competency record stored in CARSI: your enrolment, your progress through each unit, your knowledge-check result, and — the legal heart of it — the trainer's practical sign-off.

How the sign-off works

  • Gate A — trainer pre-flight. Before delivery, the trainer verifies every model- and engine-specific setpoint against your actual unit + engine manual and your State's requirements. The course is not taught as fact until Gate A is complete for your machine.
  • Gate B — operator competency. You demonstrate each competency on the actual machine; the trainer scores you against the rubric.
  • Carbon monoxide is zero-tolerance. Any shortfall on the CO-control criterion is an automatic fail, regardless of your total score.

What is recorded (the legal record)

The trainer's submission records the machine make, model and serial number you were signed off on, and the assessor's name. On a pass, CARSI issues your certificate and the record is retained — so there is a durable, queryable answer to who was assessed, on which machine, by whom, and when.

Open the Practical assessment on this course to view the full two-gate rubric and submit your competency evidence. Re-assessment is recommended annually or on any change of machine or model.

Key takeaways

  • Completing the course is not download-only; it creates a durable per-learner competency record retained in CARSI.
  • The sign-off has two gates - the trainer verifies every model- and engine-specific setpoint against your unit and State (Gate A), then scores your competency on the actual machine (Gate B).
  • Carbon monoxide control is zero-tolerance - any shortfall on the CO-control criterion is an automatic fail regardless of total score.
  • On a pass, CARSI issues your certificate and retains a queryable record of who was assessed, on which machine, by whom and when; re-assessment is recommended annually or on any change of machine or model.

courses.title: CARSI Water Restoration Practitioner
courses.description:
A foundational, Australian-produced course for restoration technicians, supervisors, cleaners and insurance-support staff who respond to water-damaged buildings and contents. It is CEC-accredited training leading to the CARSI Water Restoration Practitioner designation — it aligns to the principles of professional water damage restoration practice and is designed to build sound field judgement, not to replace a technician’s own certification, a manufacturer’s instructions, a Safety Data Sheet, or a site’s own rules.

Six modules take a new technician from first principles to a defensible job file: what water damage restoration is and how to think about it, how water is categorised (Category 1/2/3) and classified by the extent of intrusion, hazard-led safety and PPE selection, moisture assessment with metric instruments and Australian-available equipment, drying principles with realistic 230 V / 10 A power awareness, and documentation, reporting and the Australian insurance context. Each idea is grounded in Australian power (230 V, 10 A GPO, RCD/safety switch), metric units, Australian standards and regulators (AS/NZS, Safe Work Australia, state WHS), and Australian job realities. A ten-question knowledge check closes the course.

CARSI Water Restoration Practitioner is a CARSI Southern Hemisphere Restoration Designation. Completing this CEC-accredited training also earns IICRC Continuing Education Credits (CECs).
courses.shortDescription: CEC-accredited training leading to the CARSI Water Restoration Practitioner designation in the fundamentals of professional water damage restoration — water categories and classes, hazard-led safety, moisture assessment, drying principles and documentation, produced for Australian conditions.

meta.discipline: CARSI Water Restoration Practitioner — CARSI Southern Hemisphere designation (CEC-accredited)
meta.standardsReferenced: Nominative only (ANSI/IICRC S500); no standard text reproduced (IICRC AI Use Policy honoured).

modules.title: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works

lessons.title: What this course covers and who it is for
lessons.contentBody:
Welcome to Water Damage Restoration Fundamentals. This course is for technicians, supervisors, cleaners and insurance-support staff who attend water-damaged buildings and contents in Australia. By the end you will be able to:

  • describe what professional water damage restoration is and why a structured process matters;
  • categorise water by likely contamination (Category 1, 2 and 3) and describe how the extent of intrusion is classified;
  • identify site hazards and select personal protective equipment (PPE) by the hazard present, not by habit;
  • take and interpret moisture readings with metric instruments and set a defensible dry goal;
  • explain the principles of structural drying and size equipment with realistic power awareness at 230 V / 10 A; and
  • produce documentation that stands up in an Australian insurance and dispute context.

This is CEC-accredited training leading to the CARSI Water Restoration Practitioner designation: it aligns to the principles of professional water damage restoration practice. It supports good judgement and does not replace your own certification, a manufacturer’s instructions, a Safety Data Sheet (SDS), a relevant Australian standard, or a site’s own rules.

lessons.title: How the restoration mindset works
lessons.contentBody:
Water damage restoration is a race between drying and damage. Wet materials do not just look bad — over hours and days they support microbial growth, corrosion, swelling and delamination. The professional aim is to return the structure and restorable contents to a dry, safe condition quickly, safely and with evidence, and to know when a material should be restored versus removed.

A reliable job follows a repeatable arc: make it safe, understand the water and the affected materials, control the source and remove bulk water, assess moisture and set a dry goal, apply the right drying strategy, monitor to the goal, then verify and document. Australia adds its own constraints at every step — 230 V power and 10 A circuits, metric measurement, Australian climate and building types, and Australian work-health-and-safety law. We build each of those in as we go. Where standards are named, we reference them nominatively (for example, aligning to ANSI/IICRC S500) and never reproduce their text.

lessons.title: The three water categories
lessons.contentBody:
Water is described by how contaminated it is likely to be. Three categories are used:

  • Category 1 — clean water. Originates from a sanitary source with no substantial contamination, for example a burst mains supply line, an overflowing tap or rainwater ingress that has not passed through contaminated materials.
  • Category 2 — significantly contaminated (“grey water”). Contains contamination that could cause discomfort or illness if contacted or consumed, for example discharge from a dishwasher or washing machine, or a toilet overflow containing urine but no faeces.
  • Category 3 — grossly contaminated (“black water”). May contain harmful agents, for example sewage, water from beyond the toilet trap, or floodwater from rivers or the sea.

Two rules matter on site. First, a category can worsen over time — clean water left in a warm building can degrade toward Category 2 or 3 as it contacts soils and supports growth. Second, when you are unsure, treat water as the worse category and reassess. Category drives the safety, PPE, cleaning and disposal decisions that follow.

lessons.title: Classes: how much water and how hard to dry
lessons.contentBody:
Where category describes contamination, class describes the amount of water and the likely rate of evaporation — how big the drying job is. In broad terms:

  • Class 1 — the least water; only part of a room or materials of low porosity are affected. Fastest to dry.
  • Class 2 — a larger amount of water; an entire room, carpet and cushion (underlay), and wicking up walls to around 300 mm.
  • Class 3 — the greatest amount of water; ceilings, walls, insulation and subfloor are saturated, often from above.
  • Class 4 — specialty drying situations, where water is bound in low-permeability materials such as hardwood, plaster, masonry or concrete that dry very slowly and need specialist methods.

Class shapes how much drying capacity you need and how long the job will take. A Class 3 ceiling collapse needs far more airflow and dehumidification than a Class 1 spill. Getting the class right early prevents both under-drying (a callback and a mould claim) and over-resourcing.

lessons.title: Identify the hazards first
lessons.contentBody:
Safety comes before drying. Under the model Work Health and Safety (WHS) laws administered through Safe Work Australia and your state regulator, hazards are identified and controlled before work begins — often recorded in a Safe Work Method Statement (SWMS) or JSEA, with a site induction where required. Common water-damage hazards include:

  • Electrical — water and 230 V mains do not mix. Do not enter standing water near live electrical fittings; have supply isolated where needed and use a residual current device (safety switch, RCD) on portable equipment. All portable electrical gear should be tested and tagged (AS/NZS 3760).
  • Biological — Category 2 and 3 water carries micro-organisms; sewage is a biohazard.
  • Slips, trips and falls — wet floors, trailing leads and ladders.
  • Structural — saturated ceilings and floors can collapse; a wet plaster ceiling holding water is a serious overhead hazard.
  • Chemical — cleaning and antimicrobial products; always read the SDS.
  • Confined spaces and heat — subfloors, roof voids and hot drying environments.

If a hazard cannot be controlled, the correct decision may be to stop and escalate — for example, an electrician or structural engineer, or the poisons information line 13 11 26 for a chemical exposure.

lessons.title: Select PPE by the hazard present
lessons.contentBody:
PPE is chosen for the hazard in front of you, then stated in the job file. It is the last line of defence, after isolation and engineering controls. A practical guide by category and task:

  • Category 1 (clean water): safety footwear, gloves, eye protection; respiratory protection if disturbing dusty or mouldy materials.
  • Category 2 (grey water): the above plus fluid-resistant gloves, and a properly fitted P2 respirator when generating aerosols or working with contaminated materials.
  • Category 3 (black water / sewage): full skin protection — fluid-resistant coveralls, gloves, boots and eye protection, and P2 (or higher) respiratory protection fit-checked to the wearer. Treat everything contacted as contaminated.

Where mould is present, respiratory and skin protection are selected for the mould hazard as well — covered in CARSI’s Mould Remediation Specialist course. Respirators only protect when they seal, so fit matters, and facial hair breaks the seal. Record the PPE actually used, not just the PPE available.

lessons.title: Instruments and what they tell you
lessons.contentBody:
You cannot dry what you cannot measure. Moisture assessment uses several instruments, each answering a different question, and all read in metric:

  • Non-invasive (capacitance/radio-frequency) meters — scan a surface without pinholes to find where moisture is, quickly, for mapping.
  • Pin (resistance) moisture meters — give a quantitative moisture reading in a specific material such as timber or plaster.
  • Thermo-hygrometers — read air temperature (°C) and relative humidity (%RH), from which you work out dew point and specific humidity (grams of water per kilogram of air).
  • Thermal imaging cameras — show temperature patterns that suggest where evaporative cooling (and therefore moisture) may be; always confirm a thermal indication with a moisture meter.

Instruments sold in Australia in 240 V/battery form — brands such as Protimeter, Tramex, Delmhorst and FLIR are widely distributed here (availability checked 2026-07-09) — are the practical toolkit. Calibrate and check instruments per the manufacturer’s instructions; an uncalibrated meter produces confident but wrong numbers.

lessons.title: Mapping moisture and setting a dry goal
lessons.contentBody:
A defensible drying job starts with a dry standard — a reference reading taken from the same material in an unaffected area of the same building. That is your target: you dry the wet material back to the reference reading, not to an arbitrary number.

Map the affected area on day one: mark the boundary of moisture, record readings at labelled points, note the material and the instrument used, and photograph the readings. Repeat at the same points each visit so the numbers are comparable. A drying job is complete when affected materials reach the dry standard and readings are stable — measured, not assumed. Metric everywhere: metres and millimetres for extent, °C and %RH for air, grams per kilogram for specific humidity. This measured trail is what later proves the structure was returned to a dry, safe condition.

lessons.title: The drying equation: heat, airflow and humidity
lessons.contentBody:
Structural drying is applied psychrometrics — the behaviour of water in air. Four levers control the rate of drying:

  • Evaporation — moving air across a wet surface speeds evaporation (this is what air movers do).
  • Dehumidification — removing the evaporated moisture from the air so drying can continue (refrigerant and desiccant dehumidifiers).
  • Temperature — warmer air holds and releases moisture more readily, within sensible limits.
  • Air exchange or containment — either bringing in drier outside air or sealing the area so equipment can control it.

The balance matters: adding airflow without dehumidification just raises the humidity in the room and stalls drying (and risks secondary damage). You add evaporative energy and remove moisture together, then measure the result. Australian climate matters too — a humid coastal summer needs more dehumidification than a dry inland winter.

lessons.title: Equipment, power budgeting and the mould rule
lessons.contentBody:
The core drying kit is air movers (to drive evaporation), dehumidifiers (to remove moisture), air filtration devices / air scrubbers (to manage airborne particulates), and heat where appropriate. In Australia this equipment runs on 230 V, 50 Hz and plugs into 10 A GPOs, so power is a real constraint, not an afterthought.

Budget the power before you deploy. A standard 10 A GPO circuit delivers roughly 2300 W (230 V × 10 A) in total, and several outlets often share one circuit breaker. Add up each machine’s actual current draw (from its rating plate, in amps at 230 V) and keep the total on any circuit safely below its rating — a low-amperage (LGR) refrigerant dehumidifier plus a few air movers can approach a single circuit’s limit. Overloading trips the breaker (drying stops, unnoticed, overnight) or, worse, overheats wiring. Spread load across separate circuits, use an RCD, and use only tested-and-tagged leads.

Hard rule — mould changes the sequence. If visible mould is present, do not run air movers: high airflow across mouldy surfaces aerosolises spores and spreads contamination through the building. While mould is present, dry using air filtration devices (AFDs/air scrubbers) and dehumidifiers only, under containment. Air movers are introduced only after remediation and a clearance confirm the mould has been addressed. When in doubt, treat suspected mould as mould and hold the air movers.

modules.title: Module 6: Documentation, Reporting & the Australian Insurance Context

lessons.title: Build a defensible job file
lessons.contentBody:
If it is not documented, it did not happen. A professional water-damage job file records, from the first attendance:

  • the loss — cause, category and class, date and time of attendance;
  • photographs of the affected areas, the source and the damage;
  • a moisture map with labelled readings, the dry standard, the instruments used, and repeat readings each visit;
  • the drying plan — equipment placed, where, and why, including power arrangements;
  • daily monitoring — air temperature, %RH, specific humidity and material readings, trending toward the dry goal;
  • safety — hazards identified, controls applied, PPE used, and any specialist called; and
  • authorities and decisions — what was restored, what was removed and why, and who authorised it.

The file tells a complete story: this was the condition, this is what we did, this is the evidence it is dry. That trail protects the customer, the technician and the insurer alike.

lessons.title: The Australian insurance context
lessons.contentBody:
Most water-damage work in Australia is delivered inside an insurance claim, so the job file is also a claim document. A few Australian realities shape the work:

  • Scope and authority. Work is usually carried out to an agreed scope; changes (extra drying days, removing a material, engaging a trade) should be authorised and recorded, not assumed.
  • Evidence expectations. Insurers and their assessors expect measured drying evidence — readings trending to a dry standard — not just “it looks dry”. Your metric readings are the proof.
  • Consumer protection. Australian Consumer Law applies to the services you provide; avoid over-claiming (for example, do not promise a material is “sterilised” or guarantee a fixed dry time).
  • Pricing. Quotes and invoices are in Australian dollars (AUD), noting GST where relevant.
  • Disputes. A clear, dated, measured file is your best protection if a claim is questioned months later.

Restoration is a trusted role: you are drying someone’s home or business and creating the record an insurer relies on. Accuracy, honesty and good documentation are the professional standard.

Key takeaway: If it is not documented, it did not happen — from first attendance record the category and class, a labelled moisture map with the dry standard, the drying plan and power arrangements, and daily psychrometric readings, so the job file is defensible.

lessons.title: Final knowledge check

courses.title: CARSI Mould Remediation Practitioner
courses.description:
A foundational, Australian-produced course for restoration and remediation technicians, supervisors, indoor air quality (IAQ) and facility staff who assess and remediate mould in Australian buildings. It is CEC-accredited training leading to the CARSI Mould Remediation Practitioner designation — it aligns to the principles of professional microbial remediation practice and is designed to build sound, safe field judgement. It supports good judgement and does not replace a technician’s own certification, a manufacturer’s instructions, a Safety Data Sheet, an occupational hygienist’s advice, a relevant Australian standard, or a site’s own rules.

Seven modules take a technician from mould biology to a verified, documented remediation: how mould grows and why it matters to health, how a site is assessed using the Condition 1/2/3 framing, how to design containment and engineering controls, the correct remediation sequence, PPE selected by the condition present, and post-remediation verification and documentation. Australian throughout: 230 V / 10 A power, metric units, Australian-available equipment, and Australian standards and regulators (AS/NZS, Safe Work Australia, state WHS). A ten-question knowledge check closes the course.

CARSI Mould Remediation Practitioner is a CARSI Southern Hemisphere Restoration Designation. Completing this CEC-accredited training also earns IICRC Continuing Education Credits (CECs).
courses.shortDescription: CEC-accredited training leading to the CARSI Mould Remediation Practitioner designation in the fundamentals of professional mould remediation — mould biology and health, Condition 1/2/3 assessment, containment, the correct remediation sequence, PPE by condition and verification, produced for Australian conditions.

meta.standardsReferenced: Nominative only (ANSI/IICRC S520); no standard text reproduced (IICRC AI Use Policy honoured).

modules.title: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works

lessons.title: What this course covers and who it is for
lessons.contentBody:
Welcome to Applied Microbial Remediation: Mould Fundamentals. This course is for restoration and remediation technicians, supervisors, IAQ and facility staff who assess and remediate mould in Australian buildings. By the end you will be able to:

  • explain how mould grows, what it needs, and why it matters to health and to buildings;
  • assess a mould situation using the Condition 1/2/3 framing used in Australian practice;
  • design containment and engineering controls appropriate to the size and severity of the job;
  • carry out remediation in the correct sequence, including the rule that air movers are not used while mould is present;
  • select PPE by the condition present; and
  • verify the remediation and document it defensibly.

This is CEC-accredited training leading to the CARSI Mould Remediation Practitioner designation: it aligns to the principles of professional microbial remediation practice. It supports good judgement and does not replace your own certification, a Safety Data Sheet, a manufacturer’s instructions, an occupational hygienist’s advice, a relevant Australian standard, or a site’s own rules. Where standards are named, we reference them nominatively (for example, aligning to ANSI/IICRC S520) and never reproduce their text.

lessons.title: The remediation mindset
lessons.contentBody:
Mould remediation has one job: remove the mould and the moisture that feeds it, without spreading contamination and without harming people. Two ideas sit underneath everything that follows. First, mould is a moisture problem — you cannot successfully remediate mould while the water source that caused it remains, so finding and fixing the moisture is part of the work, not separate from it. Second, disturbing mould releases spores, so the whole method is built to contain and capture what you disturb rather than blow it around the building.

A reliable remediation follows an arc: make it safe and identify the moisture source, assess the extent and condition, contain the area and set up engineering controls, remediate in the correct sequence, then verify and document that the area is returned to a normal (Condition 1) state. Australia frames all of this within AS/NZS standards, Safe Work Australia and state WHS law, metric measurement and 230 V power.

lessons.title: How mould grows and what it needs
lessons.contentBody:
Mould is a fungus. It is a normal part of the outdoor environment, and its microscopic spores are present in ordinary indoor air. It becomes a problem indoors when it finds what it needs to grow:

  • Moisture — the controlling factor. Leaks, flooding, condensation, or high humidity keeping materials damp.
  • A food source — organic materials such as plasterboard paper, timber, dust, carpet and soft furnishings.
  • Suitable temperature — ordinary indoor temperatures suit most moulds.
  • Time — growth can begin within one to two days of a material staying wet.

Because temperature and food are almost always available indoors, moisture is the factor we control. Remove the moisture and keep materials dry, and mould cannot establish. This is why every remediation includes finding and fixing the water source, and why Australia’s humid coastal climates make moisture control an ongoing task in many buildings.

lessons.title: Why mould matters to health and buildings
lessons.contentBody:
Mould matters for two reasons: people and property.

Health. Exposure to damp and mould is associated with respiratory symptoms, allergy and asthma aggravation, and irritation of the eyes, nose and throat; some people — infants, the elderly, and those with respiratory conditions or reduced immunity — are more sensitive. We describe these as health considerations and follow Australian public-health guidance; we do not diagnose individuals or make medical claims, and we refer health questions to a medical professional.

Buildings. Mould degrades the materials it grows on — staining and weakening plasterboard, timber and finishes — and signals a moisture problem that will keep causing damage until it is fixed. Musty odour is often the first sign of hidden growth. Our role is to control the exposure, remove the growth safely, and remove the moisture that caused it — not to alarm occupants. Calm, evidence-led communication is part of the professional standard.

lessons.title: The three conditions
lessons.contentBody:
Australian remediation practice describes an indoor environment using three conditions, which set the goal of the work:

  • Condition 1 — normal fungal ecology. An indoor environment that may have settled spores and normal fungal traces but no actual growth. This is the target end state of remediation.
  • Condition 2 — settled spores. An indoor environment primarily contaminated with settled spores that were dispersed directly or indirectly from a Condition 3 area — for example, a room downwind of active growth.
  • Condition 3 — actual growth. An indoor environment contaminated with the presence of actual mould growth and associated spores — visible or hidden active growth on materials.

The purpose of remediation is to return a Condition 2 or 3 area to Condition 1. Naming the condition for each affected area drives the containment, PPE and verification decisions that follow.

lessons.title: Inspection and finding the moisture
lessons.contentBody:
Assessment combines what you can see, smell and measure. On site you:

  • Inspect visually — look for growth, staining, damage and the tell-tale musty odour, including hidden areas such as behind skirting, inside wall cavities, under flooring and in roof voids.
  • Find the moisture — use metric moisture meters, thermo-hygrometers (°C, %RH) and thermal imaging to locate damp materials and the water source. Mould marks where water has been.
  • Define the extent — establish the boundary of affected material and assign a condition to each area, in metric (m² of affected surface).
  • Decide on sampling — where independent confirmation is needed (for example, sensitive occupants, disputes, or clearance), sampling and interpretation are best carried out by a suitably qualified occupational hygienist.

Assessment produces a plan: which areas are which condition, where the moisture is coming from, how big the job is, and what containment and controls it needs. Large or complex jobs may warrant an independent assessment before and after works.

lessons.title: Containment: stop the spread
lessons.contentBody:
Because disturbing mould releases spores, containment keeps that contamination inside the work area and out of the rest of the building. The level of containment scales with the size and severity of the job:

  • Source/limited containment — for small areas, isolate the immediate work zone, often with a single layer of sheeting, and minimise disturbance.
  • Full containment — for larger or higher-risk jobs, seal the work area with sheeting, close off vents and openings, and control entry and exit, often through a decontamination arrangement so people and waste leave without carrying contamination.

The point is the same at any scale: what you disturb stays in the contained zone and is captured and removed, rather than migrating to clean parts of the building through the air or on people and equipment.

lessons.title: Engineering controls: negative pressure and air filtration
lessons.contentBody:
Containment is made effective by engineering controls that manage the air:

  • Air filtration devices (AFDs / air scrubbers) with HEPA filtration — capture airborne particulates, including spores, from the air inside the containment.
  • Negative pressure — by extracting air from the contained area (ideally exhausting outside), the containment is held at a slightly lower pressure than the surrounding building. Air then flows into the work area through any gaps, not out of it, so contamination cannot drift into clean spaces.
  • HEPA vacuuming — for capturing settled particulates from surfaces during cleaning.

In Australia this equipment runs on 230 V and plugs into 10 A GPOs, so plan the power: add up each machine’s current draw (amps at 230 V), keep the total safely under each circuit’s rating, spread load across circuits, use an RCD, and run only tested-and-tagged leads. An AFD that trips a breaker overnight leaves the containment unmanaged.

lessons.title: The correct order of work
lessons.contentBody:
Remediation follows a deliberate sequence so that contamination is captured, not spread:

  1. Fix the moisture source — remediation will fail if the water that caused the mould is still active.
  2. Establish containment and engineering controls — set up containment, negative pressure and HEPA air filtration before disturbing anything.
  3. Remove contaminated porous materials — porous materials with actual growth (for example mould-affected plasterboard, insulation, carpet and underlay) generally cannot be reliably cleaned and are removed and bagged for disposal within the containment.
  4. Clean recoverable surfaces and structure — HEPA-vacuum and damp-wipe salvageable non-porous and semi-porous surfaces to remove settled spores and residue.
  5. Dry the structure — bring affected materials back to a dry standard so mould cannot return (see the airflow rule below).
  6. Verify — confirm the area is visibly clean, dry and returned to Condition 1 before removing containment.

Work clean-as-you-go: HEPA-vacuum and wipe down within the containment so you leave a clean zone, not a cloud of disturbed spores.

lessons.title: The airflow rule: AFDs and dehumidifiers only while mould is present
lessons.contentBody:
This is the most important operational rule in mould drying, and it is a hard rule:

While mould is present, do not run air movers. Air movers drive high-velocity air across surfaces — exactly what is needed for ordinary structural drying, but exactly what you must avoid around mould, because that airflow aerosolises spores and blasts contamination through the containment and the building.

While mould is present, dry using air filtration devices (AFDs / air scrubbers) and dehumidifiers only. AFDs capture airborne particulates and dehumidifiers pull moisture out of the air and materials, lowering humidity without creating the aggressive surface airflow that spreads spores.

Air movers are introduced only after remediation and a clearance confirm the mould has been removed and the area is verified clean. Once the area is Condition 1 and cleared, normal drying with air movers is appropriate to finish drying the structure. When you are unsure whether mould is still present, treat it as present and keep the air movers off. Getting this sequence wrong can convert a contained, localised job into a building-wide contamination event.

lessons.title: Selecting PPE for the mould condition
lessons.contentBody:
As with all restoration work, PPE is selected for the hazard — here, the mould condition and the task — and is the last line of defence after containment and engineering controls. A practical guide:

  • Minor / limited work in a low-condition area: a properly fitted P2 respirator, gloves and eye protection at a minimum.
  • Moderate to large remediation, or Condition 3 growth: increased protection — a higher level of respiratory protection (for example a P2/P3 or, for heavy work, a powered respirator fit-checked to the wearer), disposable coveralls, gloves and eye protection, so spores are kept off skin, clothing and the airway.
  • Wet or contaminated water combined with mould: add fluid-resistant protection as for the relevant water category.

Respiratory protection only works when it seals to the face, so fit matters and facial hair breaks the seal. Don PPE before entering containment and remove it as part of leaving the containment so you do not carry contamination out. Record the PPE actually used in the job file.

lessons.title: Verifying the area is returned to Condition 1
lessons.contentBody:
Remediation is not finished when the mould is gone from view — it is finished when the area is verified as returned to Condition 1. Verification checks that the work achieved its goal before containment comes down:

  • Visual — the area is visibly clean, with no remaining growth, dust or debris, and materials are dry.
  • Dryness — affected materials have reached the dry standard (metric moisture readings back to the unaffected reference), so mould cannot re-establish.
  • Independent clearance where warranted — for larger, higher-risk or disputed jobs, or where sensitive occupants are involved, an independent occupational hygienist assesses the area (which may include sampling) and issues a clearance. Keeping assessment and clearance independent of the party doing the works avoids a conflict of interest.

Only once the area passes verification are containment and engineering controls removed and normal drying (including air movers, if still needed) resumed.

lessons.title: Documenting the remediation
lessons.contentBody:
The job file tells the complete story and protects everyone involved. A professional mould-remediation file records:

  • the assessment — conditions assigned to each area, the moisture source, and the extent in metric (m²);
  • photographs before, during and after;
  • the moisture source and how it was fixed;
  • containment and engineering controls used, and the drying approach (AFDs and dehumidifiers while mould present; air movers only after clearance);
  • materials removed and disposed of, and surfaces cleaned;
  • safety — hazards, controls and the PPE actually used;
  • verification — final readings, visual check and any independent clearance; and
  • authorities and decisions — who authorised the scope and any variations.

Work in Australia sits within AS/NZS standards, Safe Work Australia and state WHS law, Australian Consumer Law (avoid over-claiming — do not promise a building is “mould-proof” or “sterilised”), and AUD pricing noting GST where relevant. A clear, dated, measured, verified file is the professional standard and the best protection if the work is ever questioned.

Key takeaway: Mould remediation is finished only when the area is verified back to Condition 1 — visibly clean, materials dried to the unaffected reference standard, and independently cleared where warranted — not when the mould is simply out of sight.

lessons.title: Final knowledge check

courses.title: CARSI Structural Drying Practitioner
courses.description:
A foundational, Australian-produced course for restoration technicians and supervisors who dry water-damaged buildings and contents, and for cleaners and insurance-support staff who need to understand how a drying job is planned, run and proven. It is CEC-accredited training leading to the CARSI Structural Drying Practitioner designation — it aligns to the principles of professional applied structural drying practice and is designed to build sound field judgement, not to replace a technician’s own certification, a manufacturer’s instructions, a Safety Data Sheet, or a site’s own rules.

Seven modules take a technician from the drying goal to a defensible drying file: what applied structural drying is and how to set a dry standard, metric psychrometry (temperature, RH, dew point and specific humidity in g/kg) and how to use it to prove drying is working, airflow and air-mover placement, dehumidification (refrigerant, LGR and desiccant) and how to size it for an Australian job, drying-system design with realistic 230 V / 10 A power budgets and RCD/test-and-tag electrical safety, and daily monitoring, documentation and knowing when to change the plan. Every figure is metric, every power calculation assumes Australian mains, and the mould-remediation sequence is respected throughout. A ten-question knowledge check closes the course.

CARSI Structural Drying Practitioner is a CARSI Southern Hemisphere Restoration Designation. Completing this CEC-accredited training also earns IICRC Continuing Education Credits (CECs).

modules.title: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works

lessons.title: What this course covers and who it is for
lessons.contentBody:
Welcome to Applied Structural Drying Fundamentals. This course is for restoration technicians and supervisors in Australia who dry water-damaged buildings and contents, and for cleaners and insurance-support staff who want to understand how a drying job is planned, run and proven. By the end you will be able to:

  • explain what applied structural drying is and set a defensible dry goal from a reference material;
  • read the air with metric psychrometry — temperature, relative humidity, dew point and specific humidity (g/kg) — and use it to prove drying is working;
  • place air movers to drive evaporation without spreading contamination or causing secondary damage;
  • choose between refrigerant, low-grain refrigerant (LGR) and desiccant dehumidification and size it for an Australian job in metric units;
  • build a drying system that stays inside a realistic 230 V / 10 A power budget with a working safety switch (RCD) and test-and-tagged equipment; and
  • monitor daily, keep a drying record, and produce documentation that stands up in an Australian insurance and dispute context.

This is CEC-accredited training leading to the CARSI Structural Drying Practitioner designation: it aligns to the principles of professional applied structural drying practice. It builds field judgement and does not replace your own certification, a manufacturer’s instructions, a Safety Data Sheet (SDS), a relevant Australian standard, or a site’s own rules.

lessons.title: How structural drying fits with water damage restoration
lessons.contentBody:
Water damage restoration first makes a building safe, stops the water, removes what cannot be saved and extracts standing water. Applied structural drying is the stage that follows: returning the remaining structure and salvageable contents to a dry, stable condition so that mould does not grow and materials do not continue to degrade. Extraction removes the bulk of the water; drying removes what has soaked into materials and moved through the building as vapour.

Good drying is deliberate, not "put out some fans and come back in a week". You set a target, create the conditions for water to leave the materials, measure whether it is leaving, and adjust. A drying job that is not measured cannot be defended, and in the Australian insurance context an undefended job is a disputed job. Throughout this course, every number is metric and every power figure assumes Australian mains (230 V nominal, 50 Hz).

Safety note that governs the whole course: if visible mould or a confirmed microbial contamination is present, do not run air movers across affected surfaces. Follow the mould remediation sequence — containment, then air filtration devices (AFDs/negative air) and dehumidifiers to control moisture — and only introduce air movers after the area has been cleaned and cleared. Blowing air across mouldy material spreads spores through the building.

modules.title: Module 2: What Applied Structural Drying Is & Setting the Drying Goal

lessons.title: The dry standard and the drying goal
lessons.contentBody:
You cannot know when a material is "dry" without a reference. The dry standard is the moisture level of the same or similar material in an unaffected part of the building — for example, the moisture reading of skirting or a wall cavity in a room the water never reached. The drying goal is to bring the affected material back to at or near that reference reading and keep it there.

Set the dry standard at the start, in writing, before you place a single machine. Record it in the same units and with the same instrument you will use each day, so your daily readings are comparable. A drying goal expressed as "return the affected timber sub-floor to within a few points of the 12 % reading taken in the adjoining dry room" is defensible; "dry it out" is not.

Australian building stock matters here: brick veneer, suspended timber sub-floors, lightweight framing, and slab-on-ground all hold and release moisture differently, and our climate zones (from tropical far north to cool temperate south) change how quickly a building will give up water. The dry standard anchors your goal to this building in this location, not a textbook average.

lessons.title: The factors you control
lessons.contentBody:
Drying is the management of four things:

  • Temperature — warmer air holds and moves more moisture, and warming a material helps water leave it. In Australian conditions you are often managing existing heat rather than adding it, but temperature is still a lever.
  • Humidity — the drier the surrounding air, the faster water evaporates from wet materials. Lowering the humidity of the drying space is the dehumidifier’s job.
  • Airflow — moving air across a wet surface carries evaporated water away and replaces it with drier air. This is the air mover’s job.
  • The materials themselves — how porous they are and how much water they hold decides how hard the job is. A saturated carpet and underlay dry very differently from a dense hardwood floor.

These four interact: raising airflow does little if the surrounding air is already saturated, and dehumidification does little if no air is moving moisture off the materials. Applied structural drying is getting the balance right for the class of water intrusion in front of you, then proving with measurements that the balance is working.

lessons.title: Reading the air — temperature, RH, dew point and specific humidity
lessons.contentBody:
Psychrometry is the study of the properties of moist air. Four readings drive drying decisions:

  • Temperature (°C) — how warm the air is.
  • Relative humidity (RH, %) — how full the air is relative to what it could hold at that temperature. Because it depends on temperature, RH alone can mislead: the same amount of water shows a higher RH in cool air and a lower RH in warm air.
  • Dew point (°C) — the temperature at which the air would become saturated and condensation would form. It is a direct measure of how much water the air is carrying.
  • Specific humidity — the actual mass of water in the air, expressed in Australian practice as grams of water per kilogram of dry air (g/kg). It is the most reliable figure for drying because, unlike RH, it does not change as the air warms or cools.

For drying, specific humidity and dew point are more useful than RH because they tell you the real quantity of water in the air, independent of temperature. A hygrometer that reports temperature and RH lets you derive the others from a psychrometric chart or calculator.

lessons.title: Using readings to prove drying is working
lessons.contentBody:
Take a consistent set of readings every visit and compare them. The useful comparisons are:

  • Inside the drying space vs outside — your dehumidification should make the affected area drier (lower specific humidity / lower dew point) than unaffected or outside air. If the drying space is not drier than ambient, your dehumidification is not keeping up.
  • Affected area vs an unaffected control area inside the building — the gap between them should shrink day by day as materials give up water.
  • Material moisture readings vs the dry standard — the direct proof that the structure is approaching goal.

Record all four air readings plus material readings each day, at roughly the same time and location, with the same instruments. A drying record that shows specific humidity falling and material readings approaching the dry standard is your evidence that the job is on track — and, if a claim is disputed, your evidence that it was done properly. Readings that flatline tell you to change the plan (Module 7).

lessons.title: How evaporation works and where to place air movers
lessons.contentBody:
Evaporation happens at the surface of a wet material. As water leaves the surface, a thin layer of moist air forms right against it; if that layer is not moved away, evaporation slows. Air movers break up that boundary layer and replace it with drier air, so evaporation continues.

Place air movers to sweep air along wet surfaces at a shallow angle rather than blasting straight at them — you want a moving sheet of air travelling across the floor or up a wall and around the room. Space them so the airflow from one continues past the next, keeping air circulating around the whole affected area. On a wet timber or hard floor, low, angled placement moves air across the surface; for walls, direct airflow up the affected face.

More air movers is not automatically better: once the surfaces have enough airflow, extra machines mainly add heat, noise and electrical load without drying faster. Match the number to the wet surface area and the room, then let the dehumidifier remove the moisture the moving air has released.

lessons.title: The drying chamber, balance and avoiding secondary damage
lessons.contentBody:
Drying is faster and cheaper when you dry the smallest sensible space — a drying chamber. Close doors, or use containment sheeting, to isolate the wet area so your dehumidification is not fighting the whole building. A smaller, contained space reaches a low humidity quickly and stays there.

Balance airflow against dehumidification: moving air releases moisture into the space, and the dehumidifier must remove at least that much or the air becomes saturated and drying stalls. If windows and surfaces start to sweat, or the space feels warm and clammy, you have more evaporation than dehumidification — add dehumidification or reduce airflow.

Avoid secondary damage: do not aim high-velocity air at fragile finishes, artwork or loose materials; do not drive moist air into unaffected cavities or adjoining rooms; and, as covered in Module 1, never run air movers where mould is present — contain, filter and dehumidify first, and only introduce airflow after the area is cleaned and cleared. The goal is to move water out of the building, not to redistribute it.

lessons.title: Refrigerant, LGR and desiccant — how each works and when to use it
lessons.contentBody:
A dehumidifier removes water from the air so that moving air can keep picking up more from the materials. Three types are used in Australian restoration:

  • Conventional refrigerant — cools air below its dew point so water condenses out, then reheats it. Effective in warm, humid conditions common across much of Australia; less effective as the air gets cold or already quite dry.
  • Low-grain refrigerant (LGR) — a refrigerant unit that pre-cools the incoming air so it can pull the air down to a much lower specific humidity. Better for reaching a dry finish and for cooler conditions.
  • Desiccant — passes air over a moisture-absorbing wheel rather than cooling it, so it keeps working in cool and already-dry air where refrigerant units fade. Useful for dense materials, cooler southern conditions, and jobs needing very low humidity.

Choose by the conditions and the target: refrigerant/LGR for typical warm-to-mild jobs, desiccant when it is cool or you need to push the air very dry. All units are mains-powered and must be part of your power budget (Module 6).

lessons.title: Sizing dehumidification for an Australian job
lessons.contentBody:
Size dehumidification to the volume of the drying space and the amount of water to be removed, which depends on the class of water intrusion — how much material is wet and how porous it is. A small area of a single low-porosity surface needs far less than a room where carpet, underlay, plaster and framing are all saturated.

Manufacturers rate water-removal capacity in litres per day under stated conditions; some imported units quote US pints per day (1 US pint ≈ 0.47 L) — convert to litres as your working figure, and note the rating conditions, because a unit’s real output on site is usually lower than its headline figure. Work out the space volume in cubic metres, estimate the water load from the class and wet materials, then select equipment (often more than one unit, or a mix of types) to remove it within your target timeframe.

Always confirm the units you specify are sold in Australia in 240 V / 50 Hz configuration through an Australian distributor, and record the date of your availability check — models and distributors change. Do not size a job around a US-voltage unit that cannot be run here. (Availability of AU-configured refrigerant, LGR and desiccant units confirmed generally available through Australian restoration suppliers as at July 2026; confirm the specific model before quoting.)

modules.title: Module 6: Drying System Design, Power & Electrical Safety

lessons.title: Building the drying system
lessons.contentBody:
A drying system is the deliberate combination of airflow and dehumidification for the space in front of you: enough air movement to release water from every wet surface, and enough dehumidification to remove that water from the air, all inside a contained drying chamber. Design it before you plug anything in.

Work through it in order: define the drying chamber and the wet surfaces; decide the airflow needed to move air across those surfaces; choose the dehumidification type and capacity to remove the released moisture; then check the total electrical load fits the power available (next lesson). Write the design down — machine types, numbers and placement — so the daily monitoring can tell you whether the design is working or needs adjustment.

Revisit the design as the job progresses. Early on, wet materials release a lot of water and you need full airflow and dehumidification; as materials approach the dry standard you can often reduce equipment. Drying is a plan you adjust from evidence, not a fixed rig you leave running.

lessons.title: Power and load at 230 V / 10 A — amp budgets, RCDs and test-and-tag
lessons.contentBody:
Australian mains are 230 V nominal, 50 Hz. A standard general power outlet (GPO) is rated 10 A, giving roughly 2,300 W of usable capacity on that circuit — and several GPOs often share one circuit, so you cannot assume each outlet is independent. Overloading a circuit trips the breaker (best case) or creates a fire risk (worst case), and on a water-damaged site electrical safety is already compromised.

Budget your load in amps at 230 V. Add up the running current of every machine — each air mover and each dehumidifier draws its own amps — and keep the total on any one circuit comfortably below its rating, allowing for start-up surges. Because current falls as voltage rises for the same wattage, overseas equipment guides that quote current at a lower mains voltage will overstate the amps drawn on Australian 230 V mains; always redo the sum at 230 V. Spread machines across separate circuits, and never run drying equipment through daisy-chained power boards.

Every portable lead and appliance on site must have a working safety switch (RCD) in the circuit and be tested and tagged in line with AS/NZS 3760, using AS/NZS 3112 plugs and leads. This is a Safe Work Australia / state WHS duty, not an optional extra, and it is the single most important control protecting your crew on a wet site. Prepare a SWMS for the electrical and drying setup and induct everyone to it.

modules.title: Module 7: Monitoring, Documentation & Knowing When to Adjust

lessons.title: Daily monitoring and the drying record
lessons.contentBody:
Once the system is running, the job is monitoring and proof. Each visit, take the same readings at the same places with the same instruments: air temperature, RH, dew point and specific humidity in the drying space, in an unaffected control area, and (where practical) outside; plus material moisture readings at marked points against the dry standard set in Module 2.

Record it all in a drying record — a dated log per visit, ideally with photos of the meter readings and machine placement. A good record shows a clear trend: specific humidity in the drying space staying below ambient, and material readings falling toward the dry standard. This log is both your management tool and, in an Australian insurance or dispute context, your evidence that the structure actually reached a dry, stable condition.

lessons.title: When to change the plan, and closing the job
lessons.contentBody:
Readings tell you when to act. If material readings stop falling for more than a day, something is limiting the job: the drying chamber is leaking to the rest of the building, dehumidification is not keeping the air dry enough, airflow is not reaching a wet area, or there is a hidden reservoir of water (a wall cavity, under a cabinet, beneath a floor) you have not reached. Investigate and change the design — add or reposition equipment, tighten containment, or open up a trapped area — rather than simply waiting longer.

Close the job only when the affected materials have reached the dry standard and held it, confirmed by readings on the drying record, not by how a surface looks or feels. Then remove equipment, take final readings and photos, and complete the documentation: the dry standard set at the start, the daily record, the final readings proving goal was met, and any variations. Remember the mould rule: if microbial growth was present or found during the job, it is a remediation matter to be contained, cleaned and cleared — drying alone does not resolve mould. A defensible drying file is a proven drying file.

courses.title: CARSI Contaminated-Water Practitioner
courses.description:
A foundational, Australian-produced course for restoration technicians and supervisors who respond to grossly contaminated water losses — sewage overflows, failed septic systems and flood water — and for supervisors and insurance-support staff who need to understand how these high-risk jobs are controlled. It is CEC-accredited training leading to the CARSI Contaminated-Water Practitioner designation — it aligns to the principles of professional water damage restoration for Category 3 losses and is designed to build sound field judgement, not to replace a technician’s own certification, a manufacturer’s instructions, a Safety Data Sheet, or a site’s own rules.

Seven modules treat Category 3 as the health-and-safety job it is: what black water is and why porous materials usually cannot be saved, the health hazards and a documented risk assessment/SWMS, PPE selection and decontamination for a biohazard, containment/removal/cleaning with Australian-registered (APVMA/TGA) products, lawful disposal under the state EPA and water authority (trade waste, AS/NZS 3500), and drying, verification and a defensible file. Australian throughout: Safe Work Australia and state WHS duties, Poisons Information 13 11 26, AU building stock, and the mould-remediation sequence (AFDs + dehumidifiers, air movers only after clearance). A ten-question knowledge check closes the course.

CARSI Contaminated-Water Practitioner is a CARSI Southern Hemisphere Restoration Designation. Completing this CEC-accredited training also earns IICRC Continuing Education Credits (CECs).
courses.shortDescription: CEC-accredited training leading to the CARSI Contaminated-Water Practitioner designation in Category 3 (sewage & black-water) remediation — hazard-led risk assessment and SWMS, biohazard PPE and decontamination, containment/removal/cleaning with Australian-registered products, lawful disposal under state EPA/water authority, and drying and verification, produced for Australian conditions.

meta.discipline: CARSI Contaminated-Water Practitioner — CARSI Southern Hemisphere designation (CEC-accredited)
meta.standardsReferenced: Nominative only (ANSI/IICRC S500 Category 3; AS/NZS 3500 sanitary plumbing). No standard text reproduced (IICRC AI Use Policy honoured).

modules.title: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works

lessons.title: What this course covers and who it is for
lessons.contentBody:
Welcome to Category 3 — Sewage & Black-Water Remediation. This course is for restoration technicians and supervisors in Australia who attend jobs involving grossly contaminated water — sewage overflows, rising flood water and similar — and for supervisors and insurance-support staff who need to understand how these high-risk jobs are assessed, controlled and documented. By the end you will be able to:

  • identify Category 3 (black) water and explain why it is treated as a biological hazard, not just "dirty water";
  • carry out a health-and-safety risk assessment before work starts, including the people in and around the building;
  • select and use the correct personal protective equipment (PPE) for a biohazard, and decontaminate properly;
  • set up containment, decide what must be discarded, and clean and sanitise salvageable materials with Australian-registered products;
  • dispose of contaminated water and waste lawfully under the relevant state regulator and Australian rules; and
  • dry, verify and document the job to a defensible standard.

This is CEC-accredited training leading to the CARSI Contaminated-Water Practitioner designation: it aligns to the principles of professional water damage restoration for Category 3 losses. It builds field judgement and does not replace your own certification, a manufacturer’s instructions, a Safety Data Sheet (SDS), a relevant Australian standard, or a site’s own rules. Category 3 work can make people seriously ill — when in doubt, stop and escalate.

lessons.title: How this fits with water damage restoration and where the line is
lessons.contentBody:
Water damage restoration categorises water by how contaminated it is likely to be. Category 1 is clean water from a sanitary source; Category 2 is significantly contaminated ("grey") water that can cause illness; Category 3 is grossly contaminated ("black") water that contains harmful agents — sewage, water from beyond the toilet trap, rising ground water and flood water from rivers or the sea, and any water that has been sitting long enough or in conditions that let microbial growth take hold.

Category is not fixed. Category 1 or 2 water that is left, that passes through contaminated materials, or that supports microbial growth deteriorates to Category 3 over time and conditions. Treat the water in front of you by what it is now, not by where it started.

This is fundamentally a health-and-safety job that happens to involve water. In Australia it sits under Safe Work Australia and state WHS duties for biological hazards, and the disposal side sits under state environment and water authorities. If mould is also present, the mould-remediation sequence applies (Module 7) — do not simply blow air across contaminated surfaces.

modules.title: Module 2: What Category 3 Water Is & Why It Is Different

lessons.title: Sources and behaviour of black water
lessons.contentBody:
Category 3 water carries harmful agents — bacteria, viruses, protozoa, and often chemicals and silt. Common Australian sources include:

  • Sewage — toilet overflows involving anything beyond the trap, blocked or surcharging sewer mains, failed septic systems (common in regional and rural properties on-site wastewater).
  • Flood water — river and stormwater flooding, which in Australia can carry agricultural chemicals, animal waste, fuel, silt and debris from a large catchment.
  • Rising ground water and sea water intruding into a building.
  • Deteriorated Category 1/2 water that has supported microbial growth.

Because the hazard is biological and chemical, the response is decontamination and safe disposal, not simply extraction and drying. Assume the water, and everything it has touched, is contaminated until your assessment says otherwise.

lessons.title: Why porous materials usually cannot be saved
lessons.contentBody:
The more porous a material, the deeper contamination penetrates and the harder — usually impossible — it is to clean and verify. As a general rule for Category 3:

  • Discard saturated porous materials that cannot be effectively cleaned and verified: carpet and underlay, most insulation, contaminated plasterboard/gyprock, particleboard and chipboard, and soft furnishings that were wetted.
  • Assess and often salvage non-porous and semi-porous materials — sealed concrete, glass, metal, glazed tile, solid timber — if they can be thoroughly cleaned, sanitised and verified.

The decision is driven by health risk and whether cleaning can be verified, not by replacement cost. Removing contaminated porous material is often the single most important step in making the building safe, and it must be bagged and disposed of as contaminated waste (Module 6). Record what was removed and why — it is central to a defensible file and to the insurance discussion.

lessons.title: The health hazards of Category 3 work
lessons.contentBody:
Category 3 water can cause gastrointestinal illness, skin and wound infections, respiratory illness and, less commonly, serious disease. Routes of exposure are ingestion (hand-to-mouth), contact with broken skin, and inhalation of aerosols and, later, mould spores. Risk is higher for the very young, the elderly, pregnant people and anyone immune-compromised — who must be kept away from the affected area.

Two hazards often travel with sewage and flood jobs and must be considered in the assessment: mould, which grows quickly on wet organic material and is handled by the remediation sequence in Module 7; and, in older Australian buildings, asbestos-containing materials in wet sheeting, vinyl and eaves — if asbestos is suspected, stop and treat it under the separate asbestos process, do not disturb it.

lessons.title: Risk assessment before work starts
lessons.contentBody:
Before anyone enters, complete a documented risk assessment and a Safe Work Method Statement (SWMS). Cover: the source and extent of the water; who is in and around the building (and getting vulnerable occupants out); electrical safety on a wet site (isolate power, use a safety switch/RCD); structural and slip hazards; the presence of mould or suspected asbestos; PPE required (Module 4); containment and access; and the disposal plan (Module 6).

Keep the Australian emergency and information numbers to hand: 000 for emergencies and the Poisons Information Centre on 13 11 26 for exposure advice. Induct every worker to the SWMS before they start, and record it. On a Category 3 site the risk assessment is not paperwork for its own sake — it is what keeps your crew and the occupants from getting sick.

lessons.title: Selecting PPE for a biohazard
lessons.contentBody:
PPE for Category 3 is selected for the biological (and any chemical) hazard, not by habit. A typical minimum for hands-on sewage/black-water work includes:

  • Body: a fluid-resistant coverall (disposable where practical);
  • Hands: chemical/biological-resistant gloves, often over an inner glove;
  • Feet: waterproof boots that can be cleaned or are disposable;
  • Eyes/face: goggles or a face shield to stop splashes;
  • Respiratory: at least a properly fitted P2 respirator; a higher level where aerosols, strong odours or mould are present.

Respiratory protection only works if it fits and seals — fit-test, and no facial hair under the seal. Match the PPE to the hazards found in the risk assessment, and step it up (for example, to fuller respiratory protection) whenever the assessment says so. All PPE and its selection should be recorded in the SWMS.

lessons.title: Hygiene, decontamination and avoiding cross-contamination
lessons.contentBody:
The point of PPE is to keep contamination on the outside and off the worker — so how you remove it matters as much as wearing it. Set up a decontamination routine: a clean/dirty boundary at the exit of the work area; remove PPE in the right order so contaminated surfaces do not touch skin; bag disposable PPE as contaminated waste; and wash hands and any exposed skin thoroughly.

Do not eat, drink, smoke or touch your face in the work area. Keep contaminated tools, materials and PPE from travelling into clean parts of the building or into vehicles — cross-contamination undoes the whole job and can spread illness. Anyone who suffers a cut, splash to the eyes or mouth, or feels unwell should stop, decontaminate, and seek advice (Poisons Information 13 11 26, or medical care for injuries or illness). Record any incident.

lessons.title: Containment and removal
lessons.contentBody:
Contain the affected area before disturbing anything, so contamination and aerosols do not spread through the building: isolate the space (close it off or use containment sheeting), control access through the decontamination boundary, and use air filtration (negative air / AFDs with HEPA filtration) where aerosols or mould are a concern.

Then remove what cannot be saved (Module 2): extract the contaminated water, and cut out and bag saturated porous materials — carpet and underlay, affected plasterboard, insulation, particleboard. Double-bag or wrap contaminated waste at the point of removal and take it out through the controlled route, not through clean areas. Removing the contaminated material is what actually reduces the hazard; cleaning comes after removal, not instead of it.

lessons.title: Cleaning and sanitising with Australian-registered products
lessons.contentBody:
Once contaminated porous materials are out, clean all salvageable surfaces: remove visible soiling first (you cannot sanitise over grime), then apply an appropriate antimicrobial and allow the required contact ("dwell") time. Cleaning is mechanical; sanitising only works on an already-clean surface.

Use products that are legal to use for this purpose in Australia. Antimicrobial and disinfectant products are regulated here — agricultural/veterinary and many surface antimicrobials fall under the APVMA, and products making therapeutic claims fall under the TGA; do not rely on a US EPA registration as if it applied here. Follow the Australian label and the SDS exactly: dilution, contact time, ventilation and PPE. Never mix products (for example, bleach with acids or ammonia) — it can create dangerous gases. Record the products used, their registration, and dwell times in the job file.

modules.title: Module 6: Safe Disposal & the Australian Regulatory Context

lessons.title: Disposing of contaminated water and waste lawfully
lessons.contentBody:
Category 3 water and the materials it soaked are contaminated waste and must be disposed of lawfully — you cannot simply pour sewage water down a stormwater drain or send saturated sewage-soaked material to general landfill without checking the rules.

  • Contaminated water generally goes to the sewer under the water authority’s trade-waste rules, or is removed by a licensed liquid-waste contractor — not to stormwater, which runs to the environment.
  • Solid contaminated waste (bagged porous materials, PPE) is disposed of per the state environment authority (EPA) and the receiving facility’s requirements for that waste type.
  • Plumbing and sanitary work interfaces with AS/NZS 3500 and must be done by appropriately licensed trades.

Rules vary by state and local council, so confirm the requirements for the job’s location before you dispose of anything, and keep disposal records (dockets, contractor details). Lawful disposal is both a legal duty and part of a defensible file.

lessons.title: Working within Australian regulation
lessons.contentBody:
Category 3 restoration sits inside several Australian regulatory systems at once, and knowing which body governs what keeps the job lawful:

  • Safe Work Australia and the state WHS regulator — worker health and safety, biological hazards, PPE, SWMS.
  • State environment authority (EPA) and the water authority — disposal of contaminated water and waste, trade-waste approvals.
  • APVMA / TGA — the antimicrobial and disinfectant products you are allowed to use and the claims that can be made.
  • Australian Consumer Law — honest description of the work and outcomes to the customer and insurer.

You do not need to be a lawyer, but you do need to know that these systems apply, work to Australian labels and standards rather than imported US ones, and bring in licensed trades (plumbing, asbestos, liquid-waste) when the job calls for them.

lessons.title: Drying after decontamination — and the mould rule
lessons.contentBody:
Structural drying comes after the contamination is removed and surfaces are cleaned and sanitised — you dry a clean structure, not a contaminated one. Once decontamination is complete, apply the applied-structural-drying approach: set a dry standard, use dehumidification and controlled airflow, and monitor with metric psychrometry until materials reach goal.

The mould rule governs the airflow decision. If mould is present or found, do not run air movers across affected surfaces — contain, run air filtration devices (AFDs) and dehumidifiers to control moisture, remediate (clean and remove) the mould, and only introduce air movers once the area is cleaned and cleared. Blowing air across mouldy or still-contaminated material spreads spores and pathogens through the building and undoes the decontamination.

lessons.title: Verification, clearance and the defensible file
lessons.contentBody:
A Category 3 job is finished when the area is verified clean, dry and safe — not when it merely looks tidy. Verify by the standard set for the job: contamination removed, surfaces cleaned and sanitised, materials dried to the dry standard confirmed by readings, and, where mould was involved, the area cleaned and cleared before it was closed.

The documentation is what protects everyone afterwards, in an Australian insurance or health context.
