A defensible file records: the initial risk assessment and SWMS; the category determination and why; what was removed and why; the products used with their Australian registration and dwell times; the disposal records; the drying record and final readings; and any incidents. If you cannot show it was done safely and completely, you cannot prove the building is safe — and on a sewage or flood job, that proof is the whole point.

Key takeaway: In Category 3 work, decontamination comes before drying — remove and clean the contamination first, then dry a clean structure, and dispose of waste lawfully under your state EPA or water-authority requirements.

courses.title: CARSI Asbestos Awareness for Restoration
courses.description:
A foundational, Australian-produced awareness course for restoration and cleaning technicians, supervisors and support staff who attend water-, fire- and storm-damaged buildings, where asbestos-containing materials (ACM) may be disturbed. It builds the single most important safety behaviour on older buildings: recognise likely asbestos, do not disturb it, and escalate to the right licensed professionals.

Six modules cover what asbestos is (friable vs non-friable), where ACM is found in Australian buildings, the health risk and the 2003 Australian ban, your legal duties at an awareness level under the model WHS Regulations Part 8.7 and the Safe Work Australia codes (with AS 4964 and AS 2601 referenced nominatively), how to recognise likely ACM using building age and the asbestos register, and the correct stop–do-not-disturb–isolate–escalate response. Australian throughout: state WHS regulators, ASEA, licensing classes, and Australian building stock. A ten-question knowledge check closes the course.

Important: this is an awareness course only. It does not licence or qualify anyone to sample, remove or work on asbestos — that is work for licensed asbestos assessors and licensed removalists.
courses.shortDescription: Australian-produced asbestos AWARENESS training for restoration technicians — recognise likely asbestos-containing materials, understand the health risk and WHS duties, and respond correctly (stop, do not disturb, isolate, escalate to licensed professionals). Not a removal or assessment qualification.

meta.audience: restoration technicians, supervisors and support staff
meta.standardsReferenced: Nominative only (model WHS Regulations Part 8.7; Safe Work Australia asbestos codes; AS 4964; AS 2601). No standard text reproduced.

modules.title: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works

lessons.title: What this course covers and who it is for
lessons.contentBody:
Welcome to Asbestos Awareness for Restoration Technicians. This course is for restoration and cleaning technicians, supervisors and support staff in Australia who attend water-, fire- and storm-damaged buildings, where asbestos-containing materials (ACM) may be disturbed. By the end you will be able to:

  • explain what asbestos is, the difference between friable and non-friable (bonded) ACM, and why any exposure matters;
  • recognise where ACM is likely to be found in Australian buildings and on restoration jobs;
  • understand the health risk and the Australian ban, and why disturbing ACM is the danger;
  • know your legal duties under Australian WHS law at an awareness level; and
  • respond correctly when you suspect asbestos — stop, isolate, do not disturb, and escalate to the right licensed professionals.

This is an awareness course, not a removal or assessment qualification. It does not licence or authorise you to sample, remove, or work on asbestos. Its purpose is to keep you and others safe by helping you recognise likely ACM and take the correct step: stop and get the right people in. It supports, and does not replace, your legal duties, your employer’s procedures, a licensed asbestos assessor, or a licensed removalist.

lessons.title: Why awareness matters in restoration work
lessons.contentBody:
Restoration is exactly the kind of work that disturbs asbestos. Water, fire and storm damage break, wet, cut and pull apart the very materials that in older Australian buildings often contain asbestos — eaves and soffits, wall and wet-area sheeting ("fibro"), vinyl floor tiles and their backing, roofing, and some insulation. Cutting, drilling, sanding, water-blasting, demolishing or even handling damaged ACM can release fibres into the air.

You cannot tell whether a material contains asbestos just by looking at it — only laboratory testing can confirm it. That is why the safe default on any older building is to presume suspect materials contain asbestos until a competent person confirms otherwise, and to avoid disturbing them. Awareness is the control that stops a routine restoration task from becoming an exposure that harms you, your workmates, and the building’s occupants.

lessons.title: Friable and non-friable asbestos
lessons.contentBody:
Asbestos is a group of naturally occurring fibrous minerals that were used widely in Australian building products until they were banned. Because the fibres are strong, heat-resistant and cheap, they were mixed into many materials. Asbestos-containing materials are grouped by how easily they release fibres:

  • Non-friable (bonded) ACM — asbestos bound firmly in a matrix such as cement sheeting ("fibro"), vinyl floor tiles, and some adhesives. It is lower risk while intact, but becomes dangerous when it is broken, cut, drilled, sanded or weathered — which is common on damaged buildings.
  • Friable ACM — asbestos that can be crumbled to powder by hand pressure when dry, such as some pipe lagging, sprayed insulation and old heat shields. It releases fibres very easily and is the higher-risk form; work on friable asbestos is tightly restricted to specially licensed removalists.

Damage changes the picture: fire, water and impact can turn intact bonded material into friable debris. Treat damaged suspect material with extra caution.

lessons.title: Where asbestos is found in Australian buildings
lessons.contentBody:
Asbestos was used extensively in Australian construction, so it can be almost anywhere in older buildings. As a guide, buildings built or renovated before the 2003 ban (and especially before the late 1980s) should be presumed to contain ACM unless shown otherwise. Common locations relevant to restoration include:

  • eaves, soffits and gable sheeting;
  • internal wall and ceiling sheeting, and wet-area (bathroom, laundry) linings;
  • vinyl floor tiles and sheet-vinyl backing, and the adhesives under them;
  • corrugated cement roofing and fencing;
  • pipe lagging, flue and heater components, and some textured coatings.

Restoration work regularly touches these exact materials. Because you cannot confirm asbestos by sight, use the building’s age and an asbestos register (workplaces built before 2004 are generally required to have one) as your first indicators, and presume suspect materials are ACM until a competent person confirms otherwise.

modules.title: Module 3: The Health Risk & the Australian Ban

lessons.title: How asbestos harms health
lessons.contentBody:
Asbestos harms health when its fibres are airborne and inhaled. The fibres are tiny and sharp; once breathed deep into the lungs the body cannot clear them, and over years they can cause serious, often fatal, disease — asbestosis (scarring of the lungs), lung cancer, and mesothelioma (a cancer of the lining of the lungs or abdomen). These diseases typically appear decades after exposure.

There is no known safe level of exposure. The risk is driven by disturbing ACM and releasing fibres, so intact, undisturbed, well-maintained material is far less of a hazard than the same material once it is cut, broken or weathered. This is why the whole approach is built around not disturbing suspect material rather than around a "safe amount" of handling. Smoking greatly multiplies the lung-cancer risk from asbestos exposure.

lessons.title: The Australian ban and what it means on site
lessons.contentBody:
Australia banned the manufacture, import, supply and use of all forms of asbestos from 31 December 2003. That ban is about new asbestos — it does not remove the enormous quantity already in existing buildings, which is exactly what restoration technicians encounter. The national body coordinating asbestos (and silica) safety and awareness is the Asbestos and Silica Safety and Eradication Agency (ASEA).

On site, the ban means two things for you: any building from before the ban may contain legacy ACM, and there is never a legitimate reason to install or reuse asbestos material. Your job is not to deal with the asbestos yourself, but to recognise it may be present, avoid disturbing it, and bring in the licensed professionals the law requires. The next module covers those legal duties at an awareness level.

lessons.title: The Australian WHS framework for asbestos
lessons.contentBody:
Asbestos work in Australia is governed by the model Work Health and Safety (WHS) Regulations, Part 8.7 (adopted by most states and territories; Victoria has equivalent OHS provisions), supported by Safe Work Australia codes of practice: How to Manage and Control Asbestos in the Workplace and How to Safely Remove Asbestos. Related standards include AS 4964 (method for identifying asbestos in samples) and AS 2601 (demolition).

Key duties this framework creates (which your employer and the site controller must meet): identifying or presuming asbestos and keeping an asbestos register and management plan; ensuring anyone who may disturb asbestos has had asbestos-awareness training; and ensuring removal work is done by appropriately licensed removalists with air monitoring and clearance where required. You do not need to memorise the regulation numbers; you do need to know this framework exists and that it puts hard limits on who may disturb asbestos and how.

lessons.title: Licensing and the limits on who may disturb asbestos
lessons.contentBody:
Australian WHS law restricts asbestos removal by licence class. In broad terms: a Class A licence is required to remove friable asbestos (and any non-friable); a Class B licence covers non-friable asbestos removal. A very limited amount of non-friable ACM (up to 10 m²) may be removed without a licence under the regulations — but only by a competent person following the code, with the right controls, and never where the material is friable or the situation is uncertain.

For a restoration technician the safe and lawful position is simple: treat asbestos removal as work for licensed removalists, and treat identification as work for a licensed or competent asbestos assessor. This awareness course does not make you either. Even where a small non-friable removal might be technically allowed unlicensed, the presence of water or fire damage, the difficulty of confirming the material, and the risk to occupants mean the right call on a restoration job is almost always to stop and bring in the licensed professionals. When in doubt, do not disturb it.

modules.title: Module 5: Recognising & Responding to Suspected Asbestos

lessons.title: Recognising likely ACM on a restoration job
lessons.contentBody:
You will not confirm asbestos by eye, but you can recognise when it is likely and raise the alarm. Treat material as suspect when: the building predates the 2003 ban (especially pre-1990); you are looking at the common ACM locations from Module 2 (eaves, fibro sheeting, wet-area linings, vinyl tiles and backing, cement roofing, pipe lagging); or the damage has produced broken sheeting, dust or debris of unknown origin.

Before starting work, check for an asbestos register and management plan for the site and read it — a workplace built before 2004 should generally have one, and it tells you where known ACM is. If there is no register, or the damaged material is not listed, presume suspect materials are ACM. Never sand, cut, drill, water-blast or sweep up suspect material to "check" it — those are the actions that release fibres.

lessons.title: Stop, isolate, escalate — the correct response
lessons.contentBody:
When you suspect asbestos, follow a simple, disciplined response:

  • Stop work in the area immediately — do not continue the task that would disturb the material.
  • Do not disturb it — no cutting, drilling, sanding, breaking, sweeping or water-blasting. Do not try to remove it yourself.
  • Isolate the area — keep yourself, other workers and occupants away; restrict access; and, where it is safe to do so, avoid tracking any dust or debris elsewhere.
  • Report and escalate — tell your supervisor and the site controller, and have the material identified by a licensed/competent asbestos assessor and, if it must be removed, by a licensed removalist. Follow the site’s asbestos management plan.

If you believe you or others have already disturbed suspect material and may have been exposed, stop, keep people out of the area, and report it straight away so it can be assessed and any exposure managed. The correct response is never to "push on and finish the job".

modules.title: Module 6: Course Summary & Where to Get Help

lessons.title: The awareness technician’s takeaways
lessons.contentBody:
The whole course reduces to a few reliable rules for a restoration technician:

  • Older Australian buildings (pre-2003, especially pre-1990) are presumed to contain asbestos until a competent person confirms otherwise.
  • You cannot confirm asbestos by sight — only testing can.
  • The danger is disturbing ACM and releasing fibres; there is no safe exposure level.
  • You are not licensed by this course to sample, remove or work on asbestos.
  • When in doubt: stop, do not disturb, isolate, and escalate to a licensed assessor and licensed removalist, following the site’s asbestos management plan.

Getting this right protects you, your workmates, the occupants and future occupants from a disease that can take decades to appear. It also protects your business: disturbing asbestos unlawfully is a serious WHS breach.

lessons.title: Where to get help and stay current
lessons.contentBody:
For authoritative Australian guidance, go to the source rather than overseas material:

  • Your state or territory WHS regulator (for example SafeWork NSW, WorkSafe Victoria, Workplace Health and Safety Queensland) — the legal duties, licensing, and how to report concerns.
  • Safe Work Australia — the model WHS Regulations and the asbestos codes of practice.
  • The Asbestos and Silica Safety and Eradication Agency (ASEA) — national coordination, awareness resources and the national strategic plan.

Because rules, licence conditions and codes are updated over time, confirm the current requirements with your state regulator for each job and location rather than relying on memory. If you regularly work in older buildings, keep your asbestos-awareness training current and hold the general construction induction (White Card) your work requires. This awareness training is a starting point for safe recognition and response — it is not, and does not replace, a licence to work on asbestos.

courses.title: CARSI Psychrometry & Building Science for Drying
courses.description:
A foundational, Australian-produced course for restoration technicians, supervisors and insurance-support staff who need to understand the science behind every drying decision. It is psychrometry & building-science CEC training — it aligns to the underlying science that professional drying practice is built on, and is designed to build sound field judgement, not to replace a technician’s own certification, a manufacturer’s instructions, a Safety Data Sheet, or a site’s own rules.

Seven modules take a technician from the four core measures of moist air — temperature, relative humidity, dew point and specific humidity in grams of water per kilogram of dry air (g/kg) — through reading a hygrometer and a psychrometric chart or calculator, vapour pressure and how water actually leaves a wet material, the dry standard and equilibrium moisture content (EMC), and daily monitoring across the drying space, an unaffected control area and outside the building. The final module applies it to Australian conditions: Bureau of Meteorology humidity and dew-point climatology and EMC expectations by National Construction Code (NCC) climate zone. Every figure is metric, every power reference assumes Australian mains, and the mould-remediation airflow sequence is respected throughout. A ten-question knowledge check closes the course.
courses.shortDescription: Psychrometry & building-science CEC training covering temperature, RH, dew point and specific humidity (g/kg), reading a hygrometer and psychrometric chart, vapour pressure, the dry standard and equilibrium moisture content (EMC), daily drying-space/control/outside readings, and Australian BoM/NCC climate-zone context, produced for Australian conditions.

meta.audience: restoration technicians, supervisors and insurance-support staff
meta.standardsReferenced: Nominative only (ANSI/IICRC S500). No standard text reproduced (IICRC AI Use Policy honoured).

modules.title: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works

lessons.title: What this course covers and who it is for
lessons.contentBody:
Welcome to Psychrometry & Building Science for Drying. This course is for restoration technicians, supervisors and insurance-support staff in Australia who need to understand the science behind every drying decision — not just which buttons to press on a dehumidifier, but why a drying plan works or fails. By the end of this course you will be able to:

  • define and measure temperature, relative humidity (RH), dew point and specific humidity in grams of water per kilogram of dry air (g/kg);
  • read a hygrometer correctly and use a psychrometric chart or digital psychrometric calculator to relate those four measures to one another;
  • explain vapour pressure and how it drives water out of a wet material and into the surrounding air;
  • explain the dry standard and equilibrium moisture content (EMC), and use them to set a defensible drying goal;
  • take and interpret daily readings from the drying space, an unaffected control area and outside the building to prove drying is actually working; and
  • apply Bureau of Meteorology (BoM) humidity and dew-point climatology and National Construction Code (NCC) climate-zone EMC targets to Australian drying jobs.

This is psychrometry & building-science CEC training: it aligns to the underlying science that professional drying practice is built on. It builds field judgement and does not replace your own certification, a manufacturer’s instructions, a Safety Data Sheet (SDS), a relevant Australian standard, or a site’s own rules.

lessons.title: Why building science sits underneath every drying decision
lessons.contentBody:
Every drying decision a technician makes — how many air movers to run, which dehumidifier to choose, when to change the plan, when to call the job dry — is really a decision about moving water vapour from a wet material into the air, and then removing that vapour from the building. You cannot make good decisions about that process by feel. You make them by measuring the air and the materials, understanding what the numbers mean, and comparing today’s readings with yesterday’s and with a reliable reference.

This course does not repeat equipment placement or dehumidifier-sizing content covered elsewhere — it builds the underlying science: psychrometry (the study of moist air) and the building-science concepts of vapour pressure and equilibrium moisture content (EMC) that explain why materials dry the way they do. Every figure in this course is metric, and every power reference assumes Australian mains (230 V nominal, 50 Hz, standard 10 A general power outlet).

Safety note that applies throughout: where mould or confirmed microbial contamination is present, air filtration devices (AFDs) and dehumidifiers are used to control moisture and airborne spores — air movers are not introduced across affected surfaces until the area has been cleaned and cleared. Blowing air across mouldy material spreads spores through the building; this rule governs every airflow decision discussed later in the course.

modules.title: Module 2: Temperature, Relative Humidity, Dew Point & Specific Humidity

lessons.title: The four numbers that describe moist air
lessons.contentBody:
Temperature (°C) is the easiest of the four to picture, but on its own it tells you nothing about how much water the air is carrying. Relative humidity (RH) is the percentage of moisture the air is holding compared with the maximum it could hold at that temperature. Dew point is the temperature to which air must be cooled, at constant pressure and moisture content, for it to become saturated (100 % RH) and begin condensing water — it is the number you see appear as condensation on a cold window or a chilled drink can. Specific humidity is the actual mass of water vapour carried by the air, expressed in Australian practice as grams of water per kilogram of dry air (g/kg).

These four numbers are related, not independent. If you know any two of temperature, RH, dew point and specific humidity for a given air sample, the other two can be found — either by calculation or by reading them off a psychrometric chart or calculator, which the next module covers. Understanding this relationship is the difference between reacting to a single number and genuinely reading the air.

lessons.title: Why RH alone misleads, and the case for specific humidity in g/kg
lessons.contentBody:
Relative humidity is temperature-dependent: the same mass of water in the air will show a higher RH reading in cool air and a lower RH reading once that air warms up, even though nothing about the actual water content has changed. This is why a room can feel “dry” on a cool morning and “humid” by afternoon with no water added or removed — the RH percentage moved because the temperature moved, not because the water content did.

  • RH tells you how close the air is to saturation at its current temperature.
  • Specific humidity (g/kg) tells you the actual mass of water the air is carrying, independent of temperature.
  • Dew point tells you, in a temperature figure, exactly how much water is present — a higher dew point always means more water in the air, regardless of the current temperature or RH reading.

For this reason, experienced technicians track dew point and specific humidity in g/kg alongside RH, rather than RH in isolation. Two rooms can show the same RH percentage while one is carrying twice the water vapour of the other, simply because they are at different temperatures — specific humidity in g/kg is what exposes that difference.

modules.title: Module 3: Reading the Air — Hygrometer, Psychrometric Chart & Calculator

lessons.title: Using a hygrometer correctly
lessons.contentBody:
A hygrometer measures temperature and RH at a point in space. To get readings you can trust and compare day to day:

  • take readings at the same height and in the same general location in each area every visit (for example, mid-room, away from a running air mover or a window);
  • allow the instrument time to stabilise after moving it into a new area — a reading taken the instant you walk in can lag the true air condition;
  • keep the instrument calibrated and out of direct sun, direct airflow from equipment, and outdoor draughts, all of which skew the reading; and
  • record temperature and RH together, every time — RH without its matching temperature cannot be converted to dew point or specific humidity later.

Many drying hygrometers display dew point and grains or grams-per-kilogram specific humidity directly, calculated internally from the temperature and RH sensors. Even where the instrument does the arithmetic for you, understanding what is being calculated is what lets you sanity-check a reading that looks wrong — a fogged sensor, a low battery, or a probe left in direct airflow can all produce numbers that look plausible but are not.

lessons.title: The psychrometric chart and digital calculators
lessons.contentBody:
A psychrometric chart is a graph that plots the relationships between temperature, RH, dew point, specific humidity (g/kg) and a handful of other properties of moist air, all on one diagram. Enter with any two known values — commonly temperature and RH — and you can read off the others without doing the underlying mathematics by hand. Digital psychrometric calculators (apps and built-in instrument functions) do the same job faster and are now the more common tool in the field, but they are performing exactly the same conversions the paper chart represents.

  • Plot temperature on one axis and specific humidity (g/kg) on the other; curved lines across the chart represent constant RH.
  • Following a horizontal line (constant specific humidity) left to where it meets the saturation curve gives the dew point for that air sample.
  • A chart or calculator lets you quickly answer practical questions: “if I cool this air to the surface temperature of that cold slab, will moisture condense on it?” is really asking whether the slab surface is below the air’s dew point.

Whether you use a paper chart, a phone app or an instrument’s built-in calculation, the value is the same: it turns two field-measurable numbers (temperature and RH) into the complete picture of what the air is doing, which is what daily monitoring in later modules relies on.

modules.title: Module 4: Vapour Pressure & How Water Moves from Materials to Air

lessons.title: Vapour pressure and the drive toward equilibrium
lessons.contentBody:
Water molecules at the surface of any wet material are constantly escaping into the air as vapour, and water vapour molecules in the air are constantly re-depositing onto surfaces. The balance between these two movements is described by vapour pressure — the pressure exerted by water vapour at a given surface or in a given volume of air. Where the vapour pressure at the surface of a wet material is higher than the vapour pressure of the surrounding air, net movement is from the material into the air: the material dries. Where the two are equal, there is no net movement either way — the system has reached equilibrium.

This is the physical mechanism behind every drying job: raising the temperature of a wet surface increases its vapour pressure; removing moisture from the surrounding air (dehumidification) lowers the air’s vapour pressure; and moving that moist air away from the surface (airflow) stops a locally saturated boundary layer from forming and stalling the process. All three actions work by widening the vapour-pressure gap between the wet material and the air around it.

lessons.title: From material to air — evaporation, airflow, and the mould rule
lessons.contentBody:
As water evaporates from a wet material, a thin layer of air immediately at the surface becomes locally more humid than the room air — this is the boundary layer. If that layer is not disturbed, its vapour pressure rises until it matches the material’s surface vapour pressure and evaporation slows or stops, even though the material is still wet and the room air is still comparatively dry. Air movement across the surface breaks up this boundary layer, replacing saturated air with drier room air and re-establishing the vapour-pressure gradient that keeps evaporation going.

This is the science reason airflow matters to drying — but it is not applied everywhere without qualification. Where visible mould or confirmed microbial contamination is present, air movers are not run across the affected surfaces. Disturbing the boundary layer over mouldy material also disturbs and disperses spores through the building. The correct sequence is containment, then air filtration devices (AFDs) and dehumidification to control moisture and airborne contamination, with air movers introduced only after the area has been cleaned and cleared. This mould rule sits alongside — not in place of — the vapour-pressure principles in this lesson, and it overrides normal airflow practice whenever mould is present.

modules.title: Module 5: The Dry Standard & Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC)

lessons.title: What equilibrium moisture content (EMC) is
lessons.contentBody:
Equilibrium moisture content (EMC) is the moisture content a hygroscopic material (timber, plasterboard, many contents) naturally settles at when it has been exposed to a given temperature and RH for long enough to stop gaining or losing moisture. A timber skirting board sitting in a room at 20 °C and 55 % RH will, given time, settle at a particular moisture-content percentage — not zero, and not a fixed number that applies everywhere, but the level at which that material and that air are in balance.

  • EMC changes with temperature and RH: warmer, drier air pulls a material’s EMC down; cooler, more humid air lets it settle higher.
  • Different materials have different EMC curves at the same temperature and RH — timber, plasterboard and masonry do not equilibrate at the same percentage.
  • A building’s unaffected areas are already sitting at their local EMC for the conditions that building normally experiences — which is exactly why they make a useful reference.

EMC is the building-science concept underneath the practical drying goal covered next: rather than chasing an arbitrary fixed number, drying aims to bring an affected material back to the EMC it would naturally settle at in that building’s normal conditions.

lessons.title: Setting a defensible dry standard using EMC
lessons.contentBody:
The dry standard is the moisture level of the same or a similar material in an unaffected part of the building — in effect, a real-world measurement of that material’s current EMC for that building. Reading the moisture of skirting or a wall cavity in a room the water never reached gives you a number the affected material can realistically be expected to return to, because it is already sitting at equilibrium with the same building, the same climate and the same season.

  • Record the dry standard at the start of the job, in writing, using the same instrument you will use for every subsequent reading.
  • Choose the reference material and location carefully — comparing affected timber framing against unaffected timber framing (not against plasterboard or masonry) keeps the comparison valid.
  • Remember EMC shifts with the season and with indoor conditions, so a dry standard taken in summer is not automatically valid if the job runs into a different season.

A drying goal expressed as “return the affected timber to within a few points of the reading taken in the adjoining unaffected room” is defensible because it is anchored to a real, local EMC reference. “Dry it out” or a single fixed percentage applied to every building and every material is not, and will not stand up in an Australian insurance or dispute context.

modules.title: Module 6: Proving Drying Is Working — Daily Readings

lessons.title: The three readings: drying space, unaffected control, and outside
lessons.contentBody:
A single reading taken once tells you the current state of the air. A drying job is proved by a trend, and a trend needs at least three comparison points logged at the same time each day:

  • the drying space — temperature, RH, dew point and specific humidity (g/kg) inside the affected area where equipment is running;
  • an unaffected control area — the same measurements in a comparable, dry part of the building, which represents the target condition (and doubles as your EMC reference material location); and
  • outside the building — the same measurements in the outdoor air, which shows you what the weather is doing to the building and to your equipment’s performance.

Logging all three, every day, at the same instrument height and location, turns a snapshot into a comparison. A drying space converging toward the control reading, even as outside conditions vary, is strong evidence the plan is working; a drying space that tracks outside conditions instead of moving toward the control reading suggests the building envelope or the equipment plan needs attention.

lessons.title: Interpreting trends and knowing when to change the plan
lessons.contentBody:
Specific humidity in g/kg is the most useful of the daily figures for tracking real progress, because — unlike RH — it is not distorted by day-to-day temperature swings. A falling specific-humidity trend in the drying space, moving toward the control reading, shows water is genuinely leaving the building rather than simply relocating as the temperature changes.

  • If the drying space stays warm and humid with little change over several readings, evaporation is outpacing dehumidification — add dehumidification capacity or reduce excess airflow.
  • If material moisture readings have stopped falling for more than a day, investigate before assuming the job is complete: a leaking chamber, a hidden reservoir, dehumidification not keeping pace, or airflow not reaching a wet area are common causes.
  • Compare each day’s outside dew point against the drying space — a building that ventilates humid outside air into an actively drying space on a wet day can undo a day’s progress.

Only call a drying job complete when the drying-space readings have converged on the unaffected control reading and held there, with the trend and the reasoning recorded in the drying file — not on a fixed number of days or a single good reading.

modules.title: Module 7: Australian Conditions — BoM Climatology & EMC by NCC Climate Zone

lessons.title: Bureau of Meteorology humidity and dew-point climatology
lessons.contentBody:
The Bureau of Meteorology (BoM) publishes long-term humidity and dew-point climatology for locations across Australia, and current observations for any given day. This matters to a drying job for two reasons. First, it sets realistic expectations: a job running in tropical far-north Queensland during the wet season starts from a much higher outside dew point and specific humidity than the same job in a cool, dry inland town, and will need more dehumidification capacity and more time to reach the same drying goal. Second, day-to-day BoM observations let you explain a stalled reading — a run of unusually humid regional weather showing in the BoM record supports a decision to hold the plan rather than assume something has failed.

  • Check BoM humidity and dew-point observations for the job’s location at the start of the job, to set expectations against the season and region.
  • Re-check during the job if progress stalls, to see whether a regional humidity event (not equipment or plan failure) explains the trend.
  • Record the relevant BoM data alongside your own readings in the drying file — it supports the reasoning behind timeframes and any plan changes.

lessons.title: EMC targets by NCC climate zone
lessons.contentBody:
The National Construction Code (NCC) defines a set of Australian climate zones — from hot humid tropical zones in the north, through hot dry and warm temperate zones, to cool and cold temperate zones in the south and at altitude — used across Australian building design and regulation. Because equilibrium moisture content (EMC) depends on the temperature and RH a material normally experiences, the EMC a building’s materials naturally settle at differs by climate zone: materials in a hot humid coastal zone typically equilibrate higher than the same materials in a hot dry inland zone.

  • Use the NCC climate zone for the job’s location as context for how demanding the drying task will be, and as a sanity check on the dry standard reading you take on site.
  • Treat the on-site unaffected-area reading as the primary dry standard for that specific job — NCC climate zone and BoM climatology explain and support that reading, they do not replace taking it.
  • Where a job sits near a climate-zone boundary, or conditions on the day are unusual for the zone, weight the actual on-site control reading and current BoM observations over the general zone expectation.

Together, BoM climatology and NCC climate-zone context turn a single site reading into a properly Australian, regionally realistic drying plan — consistent with the psychrometry and EMC principles covered earlier in this course.

courses.title: CARSI Fire & Smoke Restoration Practitioner
courses.description:
A foundational, Australian-produced course for restoration technicians and supervisors who respond to fire and smoke damage events, and for cleaners and insurance-support staff who need to understand how a fire restoration job is assessed, run and documented. It is CEC-accredited training leading to the CARSI Fire & Smoke Restoration Practitioner designation — it aligns to the principles of professional fire and smoke restoration practice and is designed to build sound field judgement, not to replace a technician’s own certification, a manufacturer’s instructions, a Safety Data Sheet, or a site’s own rules.

Seven modules take a technician from how fire, smoke and soot behave and migrate through a building, through identifying the four smoke residue types (dry, wet, protein and fuel-oil) and matching cleaning method to residue, the restoration process from assessment and source removal through surface cleaning, contents and structural cleaning, HVAC impact and contents assessment, why corrosion urgency demands time-critical action, structural deodorisation principles nominatively aligned to ANSI/IICRC S700, and safety and PPE — including two hazards specific to Australia and not covered by United States wildfire documentation: bushfire ash from burnt treated timber, and asbestos-in-debris. Every figure is metric, every power reference assumes Australian 230 V/50 Hz mains, and the mould-remediation sequence is respected wherever drying is discussed. A ten-question knowledge check closes the course.

CARSI Fire & Smoke Restoration Practitioner is a CARSI Southern Hemisphere Restoration Designation. Completing this CEC-accredited training also earns IICRC Continuing Education Credits (CECs).
courses.shortDescription: CEC-accredited training leading to the CARSI Fire & Smoke Restoration Practitioner designation in fire & smoke damage restoration — smoke and soot behaviour, the four residue types and cleaning-method selection, the restoration process, HVAC and contents impact, corrosion urgency, structural deodorisation, and Australian-specific hazards including bushfire ash and asbestos-in-debris, produced for Australian conditions.

meta.discipline: CARSI Fire & Smoke Restoration Practitioner — CARSI Southern Hemisphere designation (CEC-accredited)
meta.standardsReferenced: Nominative only (ANSI/IICRC S700). No standard text reproduced (IICRC AI Use Policy honoured).

modules.title: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works

lessons.title: What this course covers and who it is for
lessons.contentBody:
Welcome to Fire & Smoke Damage Restoration Fundamentals. This course is for restoration technicians and supervisors in Australia who respond to fire and smoke damage events, and for cleaners and insurance-support staff who want to understand how a fire restoration job is assessed, run and documented. By the end you will be able to:

  • explain how fire, smoke and soot behave, and how combustion by-products migrate through a building;
  • identify the four smoke residue types — dry, wet, protein and fuel-oil smoke — and select the right cleaning method for each;
  • describe the fire & smoke restoration process from assessment and source removal through surface cleaning, contents handling and structural cleaning;
  • assess the impact of a fire event on HVAC systems and building contents;
  • recognise why corrosion urgency demands time-critical action on metals and electronics; apply structural deodorisation principles nominatively aligned to ANSI/IICRC S700; and
  • work safely with the correct PPE, and recognise two Australian-specific hazards — bushfire ash and asbestos-in-debris — that United States wildfire restoration guidance does not cover.

This is CEC-accredited training leading to the CARSI Fire & Smoke Restoration Practitioner designation: it aligns to the principles of professional fire and smoke restoration practice. It builds field judgement and does not replace your own certification, a manufacturer’s instructions, a Safety Data Sheet (SDS), a relevant Australian standard, or a site’s own rules.

lessons.title: How fire & smoke restoration fits with the wider building recovery
lessons.contentBody:
A fire event rarely damages a building through flame alone. Heat, smoke and soot travel far beyond the point of origin, firefighting operations often introduce significant water, and the building must first be made physically safe before any restoration work begins. Fire & smoke damage restoration is the discipline that assesses this combined damage, removes what cannot be saved, cleans what can, and returns the structure and its contents to a safe, clean, odour-free condition.

Because firefighting water is common, fire restoration frequently runs alongside water damage restoration — the same drying and monitoring principles apply wherever a structure has been wetted, and the same mould-prevention sequence must be respected if drying follows a fire event. Every figure in this course is metric, every power reference assumes Australian mains, and Australian regulators and standards are used throughout.

Safety note that governs the whole course: fire and smoke jobs frequently involve hazards that go beyond soot and odour — structural instability, damaged electrics, hazardous combustion by-products, and, in Australia, ash from burnt treated timber and asbestos-containing materials broken open by fire. These are covered in Module 7 and must be checked for on every job before cleaning starts.

modules.title: Module 2: Understanding Fire, Smoke & Soot Behaviour

lessons.title: How fire and combustion produce smoke and soot by-products
lessons.contentBody:
Fire is a chemical reaction that rarely burns fuel completely. Incomplete combustion produces a mix of fine particulates (soot), unburnt hydrocarbons, and gases — and the mix produced depends heavily on how the fire burned:

  • Flaming, high-heat combustion of natural materials (timber, paper, cotton) tends to burn cleanly and produces a fine, dry, powdery residue.
  • Smouldering, low-heat, oxygen-starved combustion — common with synthetic materials (foams, plastics, some furnishings) — burns incompletely and produces a thicker, wetter, more chemically complex residue with a stronger odour.

Soot particles are extremely small — often only a few micrometres across — which is why they stay airborne for long periods, penetrate fine gaps, and settle on every horizontal and many vertical surfaces in a building, not just the room of origin. Understanding how the fire burned (fast and hot, or slow and smouldering) is the first clue to which residue type you are dealing with, covered in Module 3.

lessons.title: How smoke and soot migrate through a building
lessons.contentBody:
Smoke moves through a building far beyond the room where the fire started, driven by several forces working together:

  • Heat and buoyancy — hot smoke rises and spreads across ceilings, then cools and migrates outward and downward through the building.
  • The stack effect — warm smoke-laden air rises through stairwells, service risers and wall cavities, drawing smoke into upper floors and roof spaces even when the fire was on a lower level.
  • Pressure differentials and air movement — open doors, broken windows, and the building’s own HVAC system (Module 5) actively carry smoke into rooms with no visible fire damage.
  • Porous materials — soft furnishings, carpet, plasterboard and timber absorb the volatile compounds carried in smoke, which is why odour often persists in rooms with little visible soot.

Because of this migration, a proper fire restoration assessment checks the whole building — roof space, wall cavities, ductwork and adjoining rooms — not just the visibly burnt area. Missing a migration pathway is one of the most common causes of a job that looks clean on handover but still smells of smoke weeks later.

modules.title: Module 3: Smoke Residue Types & Cleaning-Method Selection

lessons.title: The four residue types — dry, wet, protein and fuel-oil smoke
lessons.contentBody:
Correctly identifying the residue type on a surface is the single most important assessment decision in a fire restoration job, because it determines the cleaning chemistry and method used in Module 4. Four residue types are recognised in professional practice:

  • Dry smoke residue — from a fast, high-heat fire of natural materials. Appears powdery and dry, smears very little, and is generally the easiest residue to remove.
  • Wet smoke residue — from a slower, smouldering, lower-heat fire, often involving synthetic materials. Appears smeary and sticky, has a strong, pungent odour, and is harder to remove — wiping it can push it deeper into porous surfaces if the wrong method is used.
  • Protein smoke residue — typically from a cooking incident or scorched food with little visible flame damage. Often produces a near-invisible, off-white or yellowed film that discolours paint and varnish, paired with an extremely strong, penetrating odour out of proportion to the visible residue.
  • Fuel-oil smoke residue — from an oil-fuelled heater, furnace puff-back, or similar equipment fault rather than a structure fire. Leaves an oily, distinct-smelling residue that travels efficiently through ductwork.

A single job can present more than one residue type in different rooms, depending on what burned and how. Assess and record the residue type room by room before choosing a cleaning method.

lessons.title: Matching cleaning method to residue type
lessons.contentBody:
The residue type identified drives both the chemistry and the sequence of cleaning:

  • Dry smoke — usually starts with dry chemical sponges to lift the powdery residue before any liquid is introduced; a wet cleaner used first can smear dry soot into the surface.
  • Wet smoke — generally needs a degreasing, solvent-based cleaning approach suited to the smeary, sticky residue, applied with appropriate ventilation and PPE (Module 7).
  • Protein smoke — needs a specialised protein-residue cleaner capable of breaking down the film, applied with thorough ventilation, because the odour load is often severe relative to the visible mess.
  • Fuel-oil smoke — generally needs a solvent-based degreaser suited to oily residues, and particular attention to HVAC and duct surfaces where fuel-oil smoke travels furthest.

Always test a cleaning method on a small, inconspicuous area of the affected surface first, working from the least aggressive method appropriate to the residue type toward a more aggressive one only if needed — the goal is to remove residue without damaging the substrate underneath.

modules.title: Module 4: The Fire & Smoke Restoration Process

lessons.title: Assessment and source removal
lessons.contentBody:
Before any cleaning starts, confirm the structure is physically safe to enter — this is a Safe Work Australia and state WHS duty, not an optional step, and it comes before any restoration activity. Once safe, the assessment records:

  • the extent and residue type of smoke and soot damage, room by room, including migration pathways (Module 2);
  • whether firefighting water was used, and if so, the moisture condition of affected materials;
  • which materials and contents are unsalvageable and must be removed as source material — charred timber, collapsed linings, ruined soft furnishings; and
  • any suspected hazardous materials in the debris (Module 7) that must be checked before removal proceeds.

Source removal takes out unsalvageable, heavily charred material so that cleaning is not fighting an ongoing source of soot and odour. Stabilise the site — board-up, tarping, temporary power isolation — before structural cleaning begins.

lessons.title: Surface cleaning, contents and structural cleaning
lessons.contentBody:
Surface cleaning proceeds from the least aggressive method appropriate to the residue type (Module 3) toward more aggressive methods only where needed, working from ceilings down to floors so residue is not redeposited onto already-cleaned surfaces.

Contents are triaged separately from the structure: items cleanable on site, items requiring specialist off-site restoration, and total losses (Module 5). Structural cleaning extends to framing, wall and roof cavities, and any surface a migration pathway reached — not just visibly sooted rooms.

Mould rule: where firefighting water has left the structure wet, the same drying rules apply as on any water-damage job. If mould is present or develops during the dry-out that follows a fire, use air filtration devices (AFDs) and dehumidifiers only — do not run air movers across affected surfaces until the area has been cleaned and cleared. Blowing air across mouldy material spreads spores through the building regardless of what caused the moisture.

lessons.title: Assessing the impact of a fire event on HVAC systems
lessons.contentBody:
A building’s heating, ventilation and air-conditioning (HVAC) system is one of the most efficient pathways for spreading smoke and soot into rooms with no visible fire damage (Module 2). Assessment should check:

  • supply and return air registers for visible soot deposit;
  • filters, which typically show contamination even in rooms distant from the fire;
  • coils and the interior of ductwork runs, particularly after fuel-oil or wet smoke events; and
  • whether the system was running during or after the fire, which increases the likelihood of building-wide contamination.

Do not restart or continue running a contaminated HVAC system before it has been professionally cleaned and its filters replaced — doing so actively re-contaminates rooms you have already cleaned. Document the HVAC assessment and any professional duct-cleaning recommendation as part of the job file.

lessons.title: Contents assessment and triage
lessons.contentBody:
Contents affected by a fire event are assessed and triaged into three categories:

  • Restorable on site — items with light residue that can be cleaned in place using the method matched to their residue type and material.
  • Restorable off site — items needing specialist cleaning, deodorisation or repair at a dedicated contents restoration facility, away from an active job site.
  • Total loss — items too damaged, too porous, or too heavily contaminated (particularly by protein or fuel-oil residue) to restore economically or safely.

Photograph and inventory contents before they are moved or cleaned — this is essential documentation for an Australian insurance claim. Handle triaged contents in a controlled area away from cleaned structural surfaces to avoid re-introducing residue, and store restorable items in a way that does not transfer odour between items.

lessons.title: Corrosion urgency — why the first hours matter
lessons.contentBody:
Soot residue is frequently acidic — carrying chloride and sulphur compounds released during combustion — and acidic residue left on bare metal actively corrodes it. Appliances, wiring, chrome fittings, tools and electronics can show measurable, worsening corrosion within hours to a few days if the residue is not removed or neutralised.

This is why fire restoration practice treats corrosion control as time-critical emergency mitigation, carried out in the first 24–48 hours, ahead of the full cleaning and deodorisation scope that follows once the site is stabilised. Prioritise neutralising and cleaning exposed metal surfaces — particularly electronics, HVAC components and bare metal fixtures — over less time-sensitive cosmetic cleaning, and document the corrosion-control actions taken and when.

lessons.title: Structural deodorisation principles
lessons.contentBody:
Smoke odour comes from residual particulate and volatile compounds absorbed into porous materials. Deodorisation only works after source removal and cleaning are complete — masking an odour source that has not been physically removed simply hides the problem temporarily.

Structural deodorisation, nominatively aligned to the principles in ANSI/IICRC S700, generally follows this sequence:

  • remove the source and clean all reachable surfaces first;
  • seal or encapsulate porous substrates that cannot be fully cleaned (some framing, subfloor material) so residual odour compounds are trapped rather than continuing to off-gas;
  • apply an appropriate deodorisation technique — thermal fogging, ozone treatment (only in unoccupied spaces, for a controlled period), or hydroxyl generation (safe for occupied spaces) — matched to the space and occupancy; and
  • confirm the structure is dry (Module 4’s mould rule still applies) before closing up wall or ceiling cavities.

No standard text is reproduced here; consult ANSI/IICRC S700 and manufacturer instructions for full technique detail.

lessons.title: PPE and safety for fire & smoke restoration work
lessons.contentBody:
Fire and smoke restoration exposes workers to fine particulate, residual combustion gases, and chemical cleaning agents, on top of a physically compromised structure. Select PPE based on a hazard assessment of the specific job, generally including:

  • a properly fitted P2 or higher-rated respirator for fine soot particulate, stepped up where hazard assessment indicates (Module 7, Lesson 2);
  • chemical-resistant gloves and disposable coveralls suited to the cleaning chemistry in use;
  • eye protection against airborne particulate and cleaning agents; and
  • appropriate footwear and care with manual handling around unstable, charred debris.

Consult the Safety Data Sheet (SDS) for every cleaning agent used, and follow Safe Work Australia and your state WHS regulator’s requirements. Before power is restored to any part of a fire-affected structure, have a qualified electrician check the circuit, and ensure all portable equipment brought onto site runs through a working safety switch (RCD) and is test-and-tagged to AS/NZS 3760, on Australian 230 V/50 Hz mains through a standard 10 A GPO. Fire damage frequently compromises wiring in ways that are not visible.

lessons.title: Australian-specific hazards — bushfire ash and asbestos-in-debris
lessons.contentBody:
Two hazards are specific to the Australian context and are not covered by United States wildfire and fire-restoration documentation. Treat both as a standing part of every fire assessment.

  • Bushfire ash — ash from a bushfire or structure fire that has burnt treated timber (fencing, decking, structural members treated with copper chrome arsenate, commonly known as CCA-treated timber) can carry arsenic, copper and chromium compounds. This ash must be handled as contaminated waste, not swept dry (which raises hazardous dust), and disposed of in line with your state EPA’s requirements for contaminated waste — it is materially different from clean wood ash and from what US wildfire ash guidance describes.
  • Asbestos-in-debris — many older Australian buildings contain asbestos-containing materials (fibro sheeting, older eaves linings, some roofing and flue materials). Fire damage frequently breaks these materials open, releasing friable asbestos fibres into the debris. If asbestos is suspected in any fire debris, stop work immediately, do not disturb the material, and hand the area over to the separate, licensed asbestos removal process. Fire and smoke restoration technicians do not clean, move, or treat suspected asbestos-containing debris as part of the general restoration process.

Both hazards must be checked for during the initial assessment (Module 4) and documented, before any source removal or surface cleaning proceeds in the affected area.

Key takeaway: Identify the soot residue type before you choose a cleaning method, act early because corrosive residues keep attacking surfaces until they are removed, and treat bushfire ash and asbestos-in-debris as job-specific hazards worked under a properly fitted P2-or-higher respirator.

courses.title: CARSI Trauma & Biohazard Decontamination Practitioner
courses.description:
A foundational, Australian-produced awareness course for restoration technicians, supervisors and support staff who may be called on to assist with, or manage, the decontamination of a site affected by blood or other potentially infectious material following a trauma, sudden death or crime scene event. It is CEC-accredited training leading to the CARSI Trauma & Biohazard Decontamination Practitioner designation — it aligns to the awareness principles of professional trauma and crime scene decontamination practice, and is designed to build sound field judgement, discretion and safety awareness. It does not replace a technician’s own competency-based training, licensing where required, a Safety Data Sheet, or a supervisor’s direction.

Seven modules take a technician from understanding the professional and ethical duty of care this work demands, through the bloodborne pathogen and biohazard risks involved (including hepatitis B, hepatitis C and HIV), to risk assessment and Safe Work Method Statements, PPE selection and safe decontamination, containment and removal of contaminated porous materials, cleaning and sanitising with APVMA/TGA-registered products and safe sharps handling, and lawful biohazard and clinical waste disposal within the Australian regulatory and coronial context. The course closes with a dedicated focus on the psychological safety of workers performing this high-risk work. A ten-question knowledge check closes the course.

CARSI Trauma & Biohazard Decontamination Practitioner is a CARSI Southern Hemisphere Restoration Designation. Completing this CEC-accredited training also earns IICRC Continuing Education Credits (CECs).
courses.shortDescription: CEC-accredited CEC awareness training in trauma and crime scene decontamination — biohazard risk and bloodborne pathogens, risk assessment and SWMS, PPE and safe decontamination, lawful clinical waste disposal, and worker psychological safety, produced for Australian conditions.

meta.audience: restoration technicians, supervisors and biohazard remediation support staff
meta.discipline: CARSI Trauma & Biohazard Decontamination Practitioner — CARSI Southern Hemisphere designation (CEC-accredited)
meta.standardsReferenced: Nominative only (ANSI/IICRC S540). No standard text reproduced (IICRC AI Use Policy honoured).

modules.title: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works

lessons.title: What this course covers and who it is for
lessons.contentBody:
Welcome to Trauma & Crime Scene Decontamination Fundamentals. This course is an awareness-level introduction for restoration technicians, supervisors and support staff in Australia who may be asked to assist with, or manage, the decontamination of a site affected by blood or other potentially infectious materials (OPIM) following a trauma, sudden death or crime scene event. It builds foundational awareness and professional judgement only — it is not a substitute for site-specific competency training, a company’s own biohazard procedures, a Safety Data Sheet (SDS), or the direction of a supervisor experienced in this work. By the end of this course you will be able to:

  • describe what trauma and crime scene decontamination work involves and the professional and ethical duty of care and discretion it demands;
  • explain the biohazard risk posed by bloodborne pathogens such as hepatitis B, hepatitis C and HIV, and how exposure occurs;
  • outline the risk assessment and Safe Work Method Statement (SWMS) steps required before any biohazard work begins;
  • select appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) for a biohazard risk profile and describe safe donning and doffing;
  • describe containment, removal of contaminated porous materials, and cleaning and sanitising with APVMA/TGA-registered products, including safe sharps handling;
  • explain lawful disposal of biohazard and clinical waste under the relevant state Environment Protection Authority (EPA) and clinical waste requirements, and the Australian coronial context; and
  • recognise the psychological toll this work can carry and know where to seek support.

This is CEC-accredited training leading to the CARSI Trauma & Biohazard Decontamination Practitioner designation: it aligns to the awareness principles of professional trauma and crime scene decontamination practice. This is high-risk work performed only by trained, appropriately equipped operators. If in doubt at any point, stop and escalate to a supervisor.

lessons.title: How this training fits with restoration and biohazard remediation work
lessons.contentBody:
Trauma and crime scene decontamination (often described in the industry as biohazard remediation) sits alongside water, fire and mould restoration as a specialised discipline within the broader restoration and remediation sector. It shares some skills with general restoration — site assessment, containment, and structured cleaning — but it carries a materially higher biohazard risk and a heavier duty of care toward the people affected by the incident, including grieving family members, witnesses and first responders.

This awareness-level course does not qualify a technician to perform trauma and crime scene decontamination unsupervised. Organisations undertaking this work maintain their own competency-based training, licensing arrangements where a state or territory requires them, and supervision structures. Treat this course as the starting point for recognising the hazards and the standard of conduct expected, not as a stand-alone competency.

modules.title: Module 2: Understanding the Work, Duty of Care & Discretion

lessons.title: What the work involves and the professional and ethical duty of care
lessons.contentBody:
Trauma and crime scene decontamination is the remediation of a site where blood or other potentially infectious material is present following an accident, sudden death, suicide, homicide, or similar traumatic event. The technical task is biohazard cleaning, disinfection and safe disposal; the human context is a family, a property owner, a workplace or a community coping with a traumatic loss. Professional conduct in this field means far more than technical competence — it means dignity, discretion and restraint at every stage.

A worker in this space carries an ethical duty of care that extends beyond standard workplace health and safety obligations. That includes never discussing case details outside the work team, following the organisation’s policy on photography and documentation, using unmarked vehicles and discreet packaging where the organisation’s practice requires it, and communicating with affected people, police and other authorities calmly, briefly and respectfully. This course references relevant industry guidance, including the ANSI/IICRC S540 reference standard for trauma and crime scene decontamination, in name only; no text from that standard is reproduced here.

lessons.title: Discretion, sensitivity and the standard of conduct expected
lessons.contentBody:
Confidentiality is a professional obligation, not a courtesy. Details of a job — the nature of the incident, the people involved, images, or the address — must never be shared on social media, with friends or family, or with anyone outside the authorised work team. Breaching this trust causes real harm to grieving families and can end a career in this field.

On site, technicians should keep interactions with family members, neighbours and onlookers brief, calm and respectful, and should defer all questions about the incident itself to police or the property owner’s representative. Work should proceed quietly and without drawing unnecessary attention. Where a family member wishes to retrieve a personal item, follow the organisation’s procedure for doing so safely, and never guide anyone into an area that has not yet been assessed and controlled.

lessons.title: Bloodborne pathogens: hepatitis B, hepatitis C and HIV
lessons.contentBody:
Blood and other potentially infectious materials can carry bloodborne pathogens. The pathogens of greatest occupational concern in this work are hepatitis B (HBV), hepatitis C (HCV) and human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). Each can cause serious, long-term illness, and none can be reliably identified by looking at a stain or a surface. Every job must be treated as though blood or OPIM present a genuine infection risk, regardless of how the scene appears.

Standard (universal) precautions apply on every job: treat all blood and OPIM as potentially infectious, use appropriate PPE at all times, and never rely on visual judgement alone to decide a surface is safe. Vaccination against hepatitis B is available in Australia and is strongly recommended for anyone regularly performing this work; discuss vaccination and any post-exposure protocols with your organisation and a treating doctor, and note that Poisons Information (13 11 26) can advise in the event of an incidental chemical exposure during decontamination.

lessons.title: Routes of exposure: broken skin, mucous membranes and sharps
lessons.contentBody:
Bloodborne pathogens generally enter the body through broken skin (cuts, abrasions, dermatitis), through mucous membranes (eyes, nose, mouth), or through a penetrating injury from a sharp object such as a needle, glass or metal fragment contaminated with blood or OPIM. Intact, healthy skin is an effective barrier, which is why correctly fitted PPE, good skin integrity, and safe handling practices are the primary controls. Recognised routes of exposure include:

  • direct skin contact where skin is broken, cracked or affected by dermatitis;
  • splashes or aerosols contacting the eyes, nose or mouth;
  • a sharps injury from an object that penetrates the skin;
  • indirect contact from touching a contaminated surface and then the face before hand hygiene.

Any suspected exposure incident must be reported immediately under the organisation’s exposure protocol, followed by first aid (wash the area; do not scrub broken skin) and prompt medical assessment — do not wait until the end of the shift.

modules.title: Module 4: Risk Assessment & Safe Work Method Statements

lessons.title: Conducting a site-specific risk assessment before entry
lessons.contentBody:
Before any equipment is unloaded, a supervisor or trained technician must complete a site-specific risk assessment. This considers the nature and extent of the biohazard, the layout and construction of the affected area, access and egress, the presence of sharps or other physical hazards, ventilation, and any information available from police or the property representative about what occurred and where.

Under Safe Work Australia’s model WHS framework and the equivalent state and territory WHS regulators, a person conducting a business or undertaking (PCBU) must eliminate or minimise risks so far as is reasonably practicable. For biohazard remediation this means the risk assessment directly drives PPE selection, containment method, and the number and competency of workers assigned — it is not a paperwork formality completed after the fact.

lessons.title: Developing and following a Safe Work Method Statement (SWMS)
lessons.contentBody:
A Safe Work Method Statement (SWMS) sets out the specific hazards of the job and the control measures and safe work steps that will be followed, in sequence, by everyone on site. For biohazard work, a SWMS typically covers PPE requirements and change-out points, containment and access control, safe handling and disposal of sharps, the products to be used for cleaning and sanitising and their safe use, waste segregation and removal, and the process for an exposure incident.

The SWMS must be read, understood and followed by every worker on the job, not just the supervisor who wrote it. If conditions on site do not match what the SWMS assumed — for example, unexpected sharps, structural damage, or a larger contaminated area than anticipated — work must stop and the SWMS must be reviewed before continuing. When in doubt, stop and escalate.

lessons.title: Selecting PPE for a biohazard risk profile
lessons.contentBody:
PPE for trauma and crime scene decontamination is selected against the risk assessment, not a single fixed kit. At minimum, work involving blood or OPIM typically requires fluid-resistant or fluid-impermeable coveralls, nitrile gloves (often double-gloved), eye and face protection, and respiratory protection appropriate to the hazard, which may range from a well-fitted P2 particulate respirator to a higher level of respiratory protection where aerosols, strong odours or chemical vapours are present. Boot covers or dedicated washable footwear and head covering complete the ensemble on most jobs.

PPE selection must also account for the cleaning and disinfection products being used — check the Safety Data Sheet (SDS) for each product for compatible glove material and any additional respiratory or eye protection it requires. PPE that is torn, punctured or otherwise compromised must be replaced immediately, and no one should enter a contaminated area without the full PPE set specified for that job.

lessons.title: Safe doffing and decontamination of PPE and self
lessons.contentBody:
Removing (doffing) contaminated PPE incorrectly is one of the most common ways workers contaminate themselves. Doffing should follow a deliberate, practised sequence: outer gloves and grossly contaminated items removed first, without letting bare skin touch the outer contaminated surface; coveralls rolled inward as they are removed; inner gloves removed last, followed immediately by thorough hand hygiene. Respiratory protection is removed only once face and hands are clean, and only after leaving the contaminated area.

Set up a designated doffing station at the edge of the containment area, with a waste receptacle for disposable PPE and a means of hand hygiene close at hand. Reusable PPE (such as boots or respirator facepieces) must be decontaminated according to the manufacturer’s instructions before reuse. Never take contaminated PPE, tools or clothing home or into a vehicle cabin.

lessons.title: Containment and safe removal of porous contaminated materials
lessons.contentBody:
Establish containment before disturbing the affected area — physical barriers, signage and controlled access limit the spread of contamination and protect anyone not directly involved in the work. Within containment, porous materials that have absorbed blood or OPIM (carpet, underlay, some timber, plasterboard, soft furnishings) generally cannot be reliably decontaminated and are treated as non-restorable; they are bagged in appropriately rated biohazard bags, sealed and removed as contaminated waste rather than cleaned in place.

Non-porous surfaces (hard flooring, tiles, sealed benchtops) can often be cleaned and disinfected in place once bulk material and porous items have been removed. Work methodically from least to most contaminated, and from higher to lower surfaces, to avoid re-contaminating areas already cleaned.

lessons.title: Cleaning, sanitising with registered products, and safe sharps handling
lessons.contentBody:
Once bulk contamination and non-restorable porous material are removed, remaining surfaces are cleaned and then sanitised or disinfected using products registered for the purpose with the Australian Pesticides and Veterinary Medicines Authority (APVMA) or, where relevant, the Therapeutic Goods Administration (TGA). Always follow the label directions for dilution, contact time and application — a disinfectant left on a surface for less than its stated contact time will not achieve the claimed result.

  • never recap a used needle or sharp by hand;
  • use a puncture-resistant, approved sharps container for all sharps encountered, and never overfill it;
  • use tools such as tongs or forceps, rather than fingers, to retrieve any sharp or glass fragment;
  • seal and label the sharps container before it leaves the site for disposal.

modules.title: Module 7: Lawful Disposal, Regulatory Context & Psychological Safety

lessons.title: Lawful biohazard disposal, and the Australian regulatory and coronial context
lessons.contentBody:
Biohazard waste, including contaminated porous materials and sharps containers, is classified as clinical or related waste in Australia and must be transported and disposed of through a licensed clinical waste contractor in accordance with the relevant state or territory Environment Protection Authority (EPA) and clinical waste regulations. Waste must never be placed in general kerbside bins, and chain-of-custody documentation (consignment notes, waste tracking) should be kept as part of the job file.

Where a death has occurred, police and, in many cases, the coroner have jurisdiction over the scene until they formally release it. No decontamination work may begin until that release has been confirmed by the supervisor, in line with instructions from police or the property representative — disturbing a scene before release can compromise an investigation and is unlawful. Safe Work Australia’s guidance on biological hazards, together with the relevant state or territory WHS regulator, underpins the workplace health and safety obligations that apply throughout this process.

lessons.title: Psychological safety: the toll of this work and getting support
lessons.contentBody:
Trauma and crime scene decontamination is confronting work, even for experienced technicians. Exposure to the aftermath of a traumatic event, sometimes repeatedly, carries a genuine mental-health toll, and no amount of professional composure removes that risk. Organisations undertaking this work have a duty of care to support the psychological wellbeing of their people, not only their physical safety.

  • normalise talking about the impact of a job within the team, without breaching client confidentiality;
  • make use of an Employee Assistance Program (EAP) or equivalent counselling support where one is available, and know how to access it before you need it;
  • watch for signs of cumulative stress in yourself and colleagues — sleep disruption, withdrawal, irritability, or intrusive thoughts about a job — and encourage early support rather than waiting for a crisis;
  • debrief after difficult jobs, and rotate or rest workers who have had a heavy run of difficult sites.

This is high-risk work, and it should only ever be carried out by trained, appropriately equipped operators working within their organisation’s procedures. If a task, a site condition, or your own wellbeing raises doubt at any point, the correct response is always to stop and escalate — to a supervisor for a work issue, or to appropriate support for a personal one.

courses.title: CARSI Carpet Cleaning Practitioner
courses.description:
A foundational, Australian-produced course for carpet cleaning technicians, cleaning supervisors and business owners who clean, maintain and restore carpeted floors in Australian homes, offices, schools, childcare centres, aged care facilities and strata common areas. It is CEC-accredited training leading to the CARSI Carpet Cleaning Practitioner designation — it aligns to the principles of professional carpet cleaning technician practice and is designed to build sound field judgement, not to replace a technician’s own certification, a manufacturer’s instructions, a Safety Data Sheet, or a site’s own rules.

Seven modules take a technician from carpet basics to a finished, dry, undamaged floor: carpet fibre types (nylon, polyester, wool and olefin) and construction, and how each affects method choice; soil types and cleaning chemistry — pH, surfactants and safe chemical use per the SDS, never mixing products; the main cleaning methods — hot water extraction (steam), low-moisture encapsulation, bonnet and dry compound cleaning — and how to select between them by fibre, soil and situation; pre-inspection, fibre identification and pre-cleaning testing; the cleaning process itself, from vacuuming through pre-spray, agitation, extraction and grooming, to spot and stain treatment; and drying and airflow management to avoid over-wetting and the secondary damage — including mould — that follows it. Every figure is metric, every machine is powered from Australian 230 V / 10 A mains, and the mould-remediation sequence is respected throughout. A ten-question knowledge check closes the course.

CARSI Carpet Cleaning Practitioner is a CARSI Southern Hemisphere Restoration Designation. Completing this CEC-accredited training also earns IICRC Continuing Education Credits (CECs).
courses.shortDescription: CEC-accredited training leading to the CARSI Carpet Cleaning Practitioner designation in professional carpet cleaning — fibre and construction, soil and cleaning chemistry, hot water extraction, encapsulation, bonnet and dry compound methods, pre-inspection and fibre testing, the cleaning process, spot and stain treatment, and drying/airflow to avoid over-wetting, produced for Australian conditions.

meta.audience: carpet cleaning technicians and cleaning supervisors
meta.discipline: CARSI Carpet Cleaning Practitioner — CARSI Southern Hemisphere designation (CEC-accredited)
meta.standardsReferenced: Nominative only (the current ANSI/IICRC S100 edition; AS/NZS 3733:2018 for textile floor coverings — cleaning maintenance, as the Australian peer standard). No standard text reproduced (IICRC AI Use Policy honoured).

modules.title: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works

lessons.title: What this course covers and who it is for
lessons.contentBody:
Welcome to Carpet Cleaning Technician Fundamentals. This course is for carpet cleaning technicians, cleaning supervisors and business owners in Australia who clean, maintain and restore carpeted floors in homes, offices, schools, childcare centres, aged care facilities and strata common areas. By the end you will be able to:

  • identify the main carpet fibre types — nylon, polyester, wool and olefin — and their construction, and explain how each affects your choice of cleaning method;
  • recognise common soil types and apply sound cleaning chemistry — pH, surfactants and safe product use per the Safety Data Sheet (SDS) — without ever mixing chemical products;
  • select between the main cleaning methods — hot water extraction (steam), low-moisture encapsulation, bonnet cleaning and dry compound cleaning — based on fibre, soil and the job situation;
  • carry out a proper pre-inspection, identify fibre type, and run simple pre-cleaning tests before any chemical touches the carpet;
  • run the cleaning process in the right order — vacuum, pre-spray and agitate, extract, groom and dry — and treat spots and stains safely; and
  • manage drying and airflow after cleaning to avoid over-wetting and the secondary damage, including mould, that follows it.

This is CEC-accredited training leading to the CARSI Carpet Cleaning Practitioner designation: it aligns to the principles of professional carpet cleaning technician practice. It builds field judgement and does not replace your own certification, a manufacturer’s instructions, a Safety Data Sheet (SDS), a relevant Australian standard, or a site’s own rules.

lessons.title: How carpet cleaning fits with professional cleaning and restoration work
