lessons.contentBody:
Carpet cleaning sits alongside general cleaning, end-of-lease cleaning and water damage restoration as a distinct, skilled trade. A technician is not just running a machine over a floor — they are reading the fibre and construction in front of them, choosing chemistry and a method that suit that carpet and its soiling, and leaving the carpet clean, undamaged and properly dry. Getting any one of those wrong can shrink a carpet, dull its colour, leave a wicking stain, or set up conditions for mould.

In restoration work, carpet cleaning technique also matters after water damage: the same principles of fibre identification, safe chemistry and controlled drying apply, with the added discipline of the mould-remediation sequence when contamination is present. Throughout this course, every measurement is metric and every machine is powered from Australian mains (230 V nominal, 50 Hz) through a standard 10 A general power outlet (GPO).

Safety note that governs the whole course: if visible mould or a confirmed microbial contamination is present in or under a carpet, do not run air movers across the affected area. Follow the mould remediation sequence — containment, then air filtration devices (AFDs) and dehumidifiers to control moisture — and only introduce air movers once the area has been cleaned and cleared. This rule is repeated in Module 7, where drying is covered in full.

Key takeaways

  • This course prepares carpet cleaning technicians, supervisors and business owners in Australia to identify fibre types, apply sound cleaning chemistry, select a cleaning method, pre-inspect and test, run the clean in the right order, and manage drying.
  • Carpet cleaning is a distinct skilled trade: reading the fibre and construction in front of you, choosing chemistry and a method that suit the carpet and its soiling, and leaving it clean, undamaged and properly dry.
  • This is CEC-accredited training leading to the CARSI Carpet Cleaning Practitioner designation; it builds field judgement and does not replace your own certification, a manufacturer's instructions, a Safety Data Sheet, a relevant Australian standard, or a site's own rules.
  • Every measurement is metric and machines run on Australian mains (230 V nominal, 50 Hz) through a 10 A general power outlet; the governing safety rule is that where visible mould or confirmed microbial contamination is present, you do not run air movers but follow the mould remediation sequence instead.

modules.title: Module 2: Carpet Fibre Types & Construction and How They Affect Method

lessons.title: Fibre types — nylon, polyester, wool and olefin
lessons.contentBody:
The fibre a carpet is made from decides how it responds to moisture, heat, agitation and chemistry. Four fibres cover most Australian carpets:

  • Nylon — strong, resilient and the most common fibre in mid-to-premium residential and commercial carpet. It recovers well from foot traffic and tolerates hot water extraction and most cleaning chemistry well, but many nylon carpets carry a stain-resist treatment that can be stripped by the wrong product or excessive alkalinity, so check and use compatible chemistry.
  • Polyester — soft, colourfast and naturally resistant to water-soluble staining, but it can hold oily and greasy soil more stubbornly and is more prone to crushing and re-soiling in heavy traffic areas. It responds well to hot water extraction with a pre-spray suited to oily soil.
  • Wool — a natural protein fibre, prized for appearance and resilience, but sensitive to high alkalinity, high heat and over-wetting. Wool can be damaged (felting, shrinkage, colour bleed) by aggressive chemistry or excess moisture, so use pH-controlled, wool-safe products and keep moisture and dry times tightly managed.
  • Olefin (polypropylene) — inexpensive, colourfast and resistant to water-soluble stains and most acids, but it has poor resilience (crushes and does not spring back) and poor resistance to oily and greasy soils, which bond strongly to it. It also has a lower melt point than other fibres, so keep heat and friction moderate.

Always confirm fibre type before choosing chemistry or method — see Module 5 for identification and testing.

lessons.title: Carpet construction and how it changes your method
lessons.contentBody:
Construction is how the fibre is built into a carpet, and it changes how much moisture and agitation the carpet can safely take:

  • Tufted carpet — fibre is pushed through a primary backing and held with a latex adhesive, usually with a secondary backing. This is the majority of Australian carpet. Over-wetting can break down the latex bond over time, so moisture control matters even on robust tufted goods.
  • Woven carpet (Axminster and Wilton) — fibre is woven through the backing during manufacture, often over a jute or natural-fibre backing in older or premium goods. Jute backing browns and can wick moisture badly if over-wet, so these carpets need lower-moisture methods or very tight moisture control with hot water extraction.
  • Pile type — cut pile (upright tufts) shows vacuum and grooming marks and traffic patterns more readily; loop pile (including Berber) resists crushing but can snag with aggressive agitation tools; cut-and-loop combines both behaviours. Match agitation tools and pressure to the pile so you clean without distorting the surface.

Between fibre and construction, you now have the two inputs that drive every method decision in Module 4: what the fibre can chemically and thermally tolerate, and how much moisture and agitation the construction can safely absorb.

Key takeaways

  • Four fibres cover most Australian carpets: nylon (strong and resilient, but many carry a stain-resist treatment that the wrong product or excess alkalinity can strip), polyester (resists water-soluble staining but holds oily soil and is prone to crushing), wool (a natural protein fibre sensitive to high alkalinity, heat and over-wetting, needing pH-controlled wool-safe products), and olefin (colourfast but low in resilience, poor against oily soil, and with a lower melt point, so keep heat and friction moderate).
  • Always confirm fibre type before choosing chemistry or method.
  • Construction changes how much moisture and agitation a carpet can safely take: tufted goods can have their latex bond broken down by over-wetting, while woven Axminster and Wilton carpets with jute backing brown and wick badly if over-wet, so they need lower-moisture methods or tight moisture control.
  • Match agitation tools and pressure to the pile type (cut pile shows marks readily, loop pile can snag with aggressive tools) so you clean without distorting the surface.

lessons.title: Soil types and how they respond to cleaning
lessons.contentBody:
Carpet soil generally falls into four groups, and each needs a different chemical approach:

  • Particulate (dry) soil — dust, sand, grit and skin cells. This is the bulk of everyday soiling and is removed mechanically by thorough vacuuming before any liquid is applied; wetting particulate soil first turns it into mud that is harder to remove.
  • Water-soluble soil — sugary spills, some food and drink residues, salts. Responds well to water and a mild detergent, and is the easiest category to fully remove.
  • Oil and grease-based soil — cooking oils, body oils, traffic-lane grime. Needs a surfactant-based pre-spray to break the oil’s bond to the fibre before extraction; water alone will not shift it.
  • Protein-based soil — blood, milk, egg and other bodily or food proteins. Reacts badly to heat, which can set the stain, so cooler water and an appropriate protein-specific approach generally work better than a hot, aggressive first pass.

Identifying which soil type (or combination) you are dealing with before you select a pre-spray is the difference between a clean carpet and a re-soiled or set-in stain.

lessons.title: pH, surfactants and safe chemical use
lessons.contentBody:
Cleaning chemistry is measured on the pH scale, from 0 (strongly acidic) to 14 (strongly alkaline), with 7 neutral. Most carpet pre-sprays are mildly to moderately alkaline because alkalinity helps break down oily and acidic soils; many rinse or neutralising products are mildly acidic, used after extraction to bring the fibre back toward neutral, remove alkaline residue, and help restore feel and colour. Wool needs chemistry kept close to neutral throughout, as covered in Module 2.

Surfactants are the active cleaning agents in a pre-spray: they reduce the surface tension of water and surround soil particles so they can be suspended and rinsed away rather than reattaching to the fibre. Different surfactant blends suit water-soluble, oily or protein soils, which is why matching product to soil type (Lesson 1) improves results.

  • Always read the current label and Safety Data Sheet (SDS) for every product before use, and follow the manufacturer’s dilution rates — concentration is not a shortcut to a better result and can damage fibre or leave residue.
  • Never mix cleaning chemical products, including different brands or types (for example, an acid-based spotter with an alkaline pre-spray, or any two products not designed to be combined). Mixing can produce hazardous reactions or fumes and is not covered by either product’s SDS.
  • Store, label and dispose of chemicals as directed by the SDS and applicable Safe Work Australia and state EPA requirements, and keep the Poisons Information Centre number (13 11 26) accessible on every job.
  • Wear the personal protective equipment (PPE) specified on the SDS for each product, and ensure adequate ventilation when working with any cleaning chemical indoors.

Key takeaways

  • Carpet soil falls into four groups that each need a different approach: particulate (dry) soil removed mechanically by vacuuming before any liquid, water-soluble soil that responds to water and a mild detergent, oil and grease soil that needs a surfactant-based pre-spray, and protein soil that reacts badly to heat and generally suits cooler water.
  • Cleaning chemistry is measured on the pH scale from 0 to 14 (7 is neutral): most pre-sprays are mildly to moderately alkaline to break down oily and acidic soils, many rinse products are mildly acidic to bring the fibre back toward neutral, and wool needs chemistry kept close to neutral throughout.
  • Surfactants are the active cleaning agents that reduce water's surface tension and surround soil so it can be suspended and rinsed away rather than reattaching, which is why matching product to soil type improves results.
  • Always read the current label and SDS, follow the manufacturer's dilution rates, and never mix cleaning chemical products; store, label and dispose per the SDS and Safe Work Australia and state EPA requirements, keep the Poisons Information Centre number (13 11 26) accessible, wear the specified PPE, and ensure adequate ventilation.

modules.title: Module 4: Cleaning Methods & Selecting the Right One

lessons.title: Hot water extraction and low-moisture encapsulation
lessons.contentBody:
Hot water extraction (HWE), often called steam cleaning, sprays a heated cleaning solution into the carpet under pressure and immediately extracts it (with the dissolved soil) using a vacuum. It is the most thorough deep-cleaning method for most fibres and soil levels, giving the best overall soil removal, but it introduces the most moisture into the carpet and pad, which makes drying management (Module 7) essential to avoid over-wetting, wicking and mould risk.

Low-moisture encapsulation applies a polymer-based cleaning solution that surrounds (encapsulates) soil in a crystalline residue as it dries; the dried residue is then removed by dry vacuuming on a later visit or the same day once dry. It uses far less water than HWE, so drying times are short (often under an hour), which suits routine commercial maintenance, olefin and other low-resilience fibres, and situations where fast turnaround matters more than the deepest possible soil removal.

Choose HWE where the job needs a deep clean and moisture can be properly managed and dried; choose encapsulation for lighter, more frequent maintenance cleans, fast-turnaround commercial sites, or fibres and constructions that are sensitive to moisture.

lessons.title: Bonnet and dry compound cleaning, and choosing a method
lessons.contentBody:
Bonnet cleaning uses a rotating floor machine fitted with an absorbent bonnet pad, lightly dampened with a cleaning solution, to buff and lift surface soil from the top of the pile. It is fast and produces a quick visual result with minimal downtime, which suits hotels, offices and other commercial sites needing a same-day appearance clean, but it only cleans the top of the pile and does not deep-clean soil lower in the fibre, so it is a maintenance method rather than a full restorative clean.

Dry compound cleaning spreads an absorbent compound (carrying a small amount of cleaning solution) into the carpet, works it in with a machine or brush, lets it dwell to absorb soil, then removes it entirely by vacuuming. Because almost no free moisture is introduced, the carpet is walkable immediately, which makes this method well suited to moisture-sensitive constructions (Module 2), busy commercial sites that cannot be closed for drying, and situations where an HWE dry time is not practical.

Selecting a method comes down to three questions: what can the fibre and construction (Module 2) tolerate; what soil type and how heavy is it (Module 3, Lesson 1); and what does the situation allow for drying time and site downtime? As a general guide — heavy or deep soiling with time to dry properly favours hot water extraction; light, frequent maintenance or fast turnaround favours encapsulation or dry compound; a same-day appearance refresh on a low pile commercial carpet favours bonnet cleaning. Always confirm the choice against the fibre and construction limits from Module 2 before committing.

Key takeaways

  • Hot water extraction sprays heated solution under pressure and immediately extracts it with the dissolved soil, giving the most thorough deep clean but introducing the most moisture, so drying management is essential to avoid over-wetting, wicking and mould risk.
  • Low-moisture encapsulation surrounds soil in a crystalline residue that is later dry-vacuumed, using far less water and giving short dry times that suit routine commercial maintenance and moisture-sensitive fibres.
  • Bonnet cleaning buffs surface soil from the top of the pile for a fast same-day appearance clean but is a maintenance method rather than a full restorative clean, while dry compound cleaning introduces almost no free moisture so the carpet is walkable immediately, suiting moisture-sensitive constructions and sites that cannot close for drying.
  • Selecting a method comes down to three questions: what the fibre and construction can tolerate, what soil type and how heavy it is, and what the situation allows for drying time and downtime; always confirm the choice against the Module 2 fibre and construction limits.

lessons.title: The pre-inspection walkthrough
lessons.contentBody:
Before any equipment comes off the van, walk the whole area with the client (or note it thoroughly if working unaccompanied) and record:

  • fibre type and construction (Module 2), and any manufacturer or supplier labelling still present;
  • existing wear, traffic-lane matting, pile distortion, seams, patches and any pre-existing staining or discolouration — ideally photographed and dated before work begins;
  • soil type and level, and any obvious problem areas such as pet stains, furniture indentations or previous cleaning residue;
  • site conditions that affect method choice: available power (confirm 230 V / 10 A GPOs and their circuit layout), site access for hoses and machines, and any areas that must stay in use during the job; and
  • anything that changes the drying plan — ventilation, sub-floor type, and whether the area has a history of moisture or mould problems.

Setting and recording client expectations at this stage — what will and will not come out, and roughly how long the carpet will take to dry — prevents disputes after the job and is good professional practice.

lessons.title: Fibre identification and pre-cleaning testing
lessons.contentBody:
Confirm fibre type before choosing chemistry, using whatever combination of these is available: manufacturer labelling or carpet samples if supplied; the feel and appearance of the fibre (wool has a distinct natural feel and slight lustre; olefin looks and feels waxier; nylon and polyester can be harder to tell apart by eye alone); and, where trained and appropriate, a small, industry-standard fibre burn test on a loose or hidden tuft, comparing the smell, ash and melt behaviour against known references for nylon, polyester, wool and olefin.

Before applying any chemical product across a visible area, always run these checks on a small, inconspicuous section (such as inside a wardrobe or under a couch):

  • Colourfastness — apply a small amount of the intended product to a white cloth, press it against the test area, and check for any colour transfer.
  • pH suitability — particularly important on wool, to confirm the product will not push the fibre into damaging alkalinity.
  • Fibre reaction — check for any bleeding, browning, texture change or damage after a short dwell time, before proceeding to the full area.

If any test raises a concern, stop, select a gentler product or method, or seek further advice before continuing — testing takes minutes; a damaged carpet is far more costly.

Key takeaways

  • Before any equipment comes off the van, walk the whole area with the client and record fibre type and construction, any labelling, existing wear and pre-existing staining (ideally photographed and dated), soil type and level, site conditions such as available 230 V / 10 A power and access, and anything that affects the drying plan.
  • Setting and recording client expectations at this stage about what will and will not come out, and roughly how long drying will take, prevents disputes and is good professional practice.
  • Confirm fibre type using manufacturer labelling, the feel and appearance of the fibre, and where trained and appropriate a small industry-standard burn test on a loose or hidden tuft.
  • Before applying any chemical across a visible area, always test on a small inconspicuous section for colourfastness (using a white cloth), pH suitability (particularly on wool), and fibre reaction; if any test raises a concern, stop and select a gentler product or seek advice.

modules.title: Module 6: The Cleaning Process & Spot and Stain Treatment

lessons.title: The cleaning process — vacuum, pre-spray, agitate, extract, groom
lessons.contentBody:
A professional clean follows a consistent sequence, regardless of which method from Module 4 you have chosen:

  • Vacuum thoroughly first, in multiple directions if needed, to remove as much dry particulate soil as possible (Module 3) — this single step accounts for a large share of total soil removal and protects the fibre from being ground in by later steps.
  • Pre-spray a chemistry matched to the soil type and fibre (Modules 2 and 3), applied evenly at the manufacturer’s recommended dilution and dwell time so it has time to break the bond between soil and fibre before extraction.
  • Agitate with a brush or grooming tool suited to the pile type, working the pre-spray into the fibre to lift soil, without over-working delicate or wool fibres.
  • Extract (for hot water extraction) using slow, overlapping passes with adequate vacuum recovery, finishing each area with dry (water-only) strokes to pull out as much moisture as possible before moving on.
  • Groom the pile with a carpet rake or groomer in the direction that restores the natural lay of the fibre, improving appearance and helping even drying.

Working in a planned sequence around the room, from the far corner back toward the exit, avoids walking on freshly cleaned, still-damp carpet and keeps the process efficient.

lessons.title: Spot and stain treatment
lessons.contentBody:
Treat spots and stains after the general clean, once you can see what remains, rather than guessing in advance. For each spot or stain:

  • identify the likely soil type (Module 3, Lesson 1) — water-soluble, oily, or protein-based — as this decides which spotting product is appropriate;
  • always test the intended spotter on an inconspicuous area first (Module 5, Lesson 2) for colourfastness and fibre reaction;
  • work from the outside of the stain toward the centre to avoid spreading it further, and blot rather than scrub wherever possible to avoid distorting the pile or forcing the stain deeper;
  • never mix spotting chemicals with each other or with the main pre-spray — if one product does not fully resolve a stain, rinse and dry the area before trying a different, compatible product; and
  • rinse treated spots with clean water and blot or extract to remove chemical residue, which otherwise attracts new soil (re-soiling) faster than the surrounding carpet.

Some stains — old, set-in, or from an unknown source — may not fully release. Set that expectation with the client during the pre-inspection (Module 5, Lesson 1) rather than promising a result the chemistry cannot deliver.

Key takeaways

  • A professional clean follows a consistent sequence regardless of method: vacuum thoroughly first (this single step accounts for a large share of total soil removal), pre-spray chemistry matched to soil and fibre at the recommended dilution and dwell, agitate to suit the pile without over-working wool, extract with slow overlapping passes finishing dry, then groom to restore the natural lay.
  • Work in a planned sequence from the far corner back toward the exit to avoid walking on freshly cleaned, still-damp carpet.
  • Treat spots and stains after the general clean: identify the likely soil type, test the spotter on an inconspicuous area first, work from the outside of the stain toward the centre, blot rather than scrub, never mix spotting chemicals, and rinse treated spots to remove residue that would otherwise speed re-soiling.
  • Some old, set-in or unknown-source stains may not fully release, so set that expectation with the client during the pre-inspection rather than promising a result the chemistry cannot deliver.

modules.title: Module 7: Drying, Airflow & Avoiding Secondary Damage

lessons.title: Managing moisture and airflow after extraction
lessons.contentBody:
Drying is part of the clean, not an afterthought. The goal is to return the carpet and underlay to a dry, stable condition as quickly as practical after cleaning, because a wet carpet left too long can wick, brown at seams and skirtings, delaminate at the latex bond (Module 2), or become a mould risk.

To speed drying after hot water extraction in particular:

  • finish extraction passes dry (water-only, no fresh solution) to pull out as much moisture as the machine can recover before you leave the area;
  • increase airflow across the carpet surface with air movers, positioned to sweep air along the pile and keep it circulating around the room, breaking up the moist air layer that slows evaporation;
  • run dehumidification where practical, especially in enclosed rooms or humid conditions, to remove the moisture the airflow releases rather than letting it recirculate as damp air; and
  • improve natural ventilation — open windows and doors where weather and security allow — to assist mechanical drying.

All electrical equipment used for drying must run from Australian 230 V / 50 Hz mains through a 10 A general power outlet (GPO) protected by a safety switch (RCD), with portable leads and appliances tested and tagged in line with AS/NZS 3760 and using AS/NZS 3112 plugs. Add up the running current of every air mover and dehumidifier at 230 V, keep the total on any one circuit below its rating, and never run drying equipment through daisy-chained power boards.

lessons.title: Drying failures, secondary damage and the mould rule
lessons.contentBody:
Watch for signs that drying is not keeping pace: a persistent damp or musty smell, cool or clammy carpet more than a day after cleaning, visible dampness at the underlay when checked, or condensation on nearby windows. Any of these means airflow or dehumidification is not sufficient for the amount of moisture introduced, and the setup should be adjusted — add air movers or dehumidification, improve ventilation, or in a slow-drying room, reduce the moisture used on the next visit.

The mould rule, which governs every drying decision in this course: if visible mould or a confirmed microbial contamination is present in a carpet, underlay or the sub-floor beneath it, do not run air movers across the affected area. Follow the mould remediation sequence — contain the area, then use air filtration devices (AFDs) and dehumidifiers to control airborne moisture and spores — and only introduce air movers once the area has been professionally cleaned and cleared. Running air movers across mould spreads spores through the building rather than drying it safely.

Where cleaning is being carried out as part of, or after, a water damage restoration job, this drying discipline sits within the broader applied structural drying principles referenced nominatively in the current ANSI/IICRC S100 edition (professional cleaning) and the current ANSI/IICRC S500 edition (water damage restoration), and within the Australian peer standard AS/NZS 3733:2018 (textile floor coverings — guide to cleaning maintenance). This course references those standards by name only and does not reproduce their text, tables or procedures — always work to your organisation’s current copy of the relevant standard, a manufacturer’s instructions, and the site’s own rules.

Key takeaways

  • Drying is part of the clean: the goal is to return the carpet and underlay to a dry, stable condition as quickly as practical, because a wet carpet left too long can wick, brown at seams and skirtings, delaminate at the latex bond, or become a mould risk.
  • To speed drying after hot water extraction, finish extraction passes dry, increase airflow with air movers positioned to sweep along the pile, run dehumidification where practical, and improve natural ventilation where weather and security allow.
  • All drying equipment must run from Australian 230 V / 50 Hz mains through a 10 A GPO protected by a safety switch (RCD), with leads and appliances tested and tagged in line with AS/NZS 3760 using AS/NZS 3112 plugs; keep the total running current on any one circuit below its rating and never daisy-chain power boards.
  • Watch for signs drying is not keeping pace (a musty smell, clammy carpet, damp underlay, or window condensation) and adjust; under the mould rule, if visible mould or confirmed microbial contamination is present you do not run air movers but contain the area, use air filtration devices and dehumidifiers, and only introduce air movers once it has been professionally cleaned and cleared, working to your organisation's current copy of the referenced standards (ANSI/IICRC S100, ANSI/IICRC S500 and AS/NZS 3733:2018).

courses.title: CARSI Timber Floor Restoration Practitioner
courses.description:
A foundational, Australian-produced course for restoration technicians and supervisors who assess and dry water-affected timber floors, and for cleaners, project managers and insurance-support staff who need to understand how a timber floor is assessed, dried and either restored or replaced. It is timber-floor assessment & restoration CEC training — it builds sound field judgement for a high-value, high-risk part of water damage restoration, and is designed to complement, not replace, a technician’s own certification, a manufacturer’s instructions, a Safety Data Sheet, or a site’s own rules.

Seven modules take a technician from the objectives of the course to a defensible restoration file: how Australian timber species (spotted gum, blackbutt, jarrah, brush box and Tasmanian oak) differ in hardness, movement and moisture response; solid, engineered and parquet floor construction over suspended timber subfloors and slab-on-ground; how water damages timber floors through cupping, crowning and buckling, and the critical difference between reversible cupping and permanent set; metric moisture meter assessment and setting a dry standard against unaffected boards; the interaction of subfloor ventilation, moisture and termite activity in Australian housing; a slow, controlled drying strategy using metric psychrometry and realistic 230 V / 10 A equipment budgets; and the restore-or-replace decision, coating behaviour in Australian humidity, and documentation. Every figure is metric, every power calculation assumes Australian mains, and the mould-remediation sequence is respected throughout. A ten-question knowledge check closes the course.

CARSI Timber Floor Restoration Practitioner is a CARSI Southern Hemisphere Restoration Designation. Completing this CEC-accredited training also earns IICRC Continuing Education Credits (CECs).
courses.shortDescription: Timber-floor assessment & restoration CEC training — Australian timber species and movement, floor construction types, cupping/crowning/buckling versus permanent set, metric moisture assessment and the dry standard, subfloor ventilation and termite awareness, slow controlled 230 V drying with psychrometry, and the restore-or-replace and coating decision, produced for Australian conditions.

meta.discipline: CARSI Timber Floor Restoration Practitioner — CARSI Southern Hemisphere designation (CEC-accredited)
meta.standardsReferenced: Nominative only (Australasian Timber Flooring Association (ATFA) guidance; AS 2796 timber flooring standards; ANSI/IICRC S500 water damage restoration principles). No standard text reproduced (IICRC AI Use Policy honoured).

modules.title: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works

lessons.title: What this course covers and who it is for
lessons.contentBody:
Welcome to Timber Floor Assessment & Restoration. This course is for restoration technicians and supervisors in Australia who assess and dry water-affected timber floors, and for cleaners, project managers and insurance-support staff who need to understand how a timber floor is assessed, dried and either restored or written off. By the end you will be able to:

  • describe how common Australian floor species — spotted gum, blackbutt, jarrah, brush box and Tasmanian oak — differ in hardness, dimensional movement and moisture response;
  • identify solid strip and board, engineered, and parquet timber floor construction, and recognise the difference between a floor over a suspended timber subfloor and one laid over a concrete slab-on-ground;
  • recognise cupping, crowning and buckling, and distinguish reversible moisture movement from permanent set;
  • take and interpret metric moisture meter readings and set a defensible dry standard against unaffected boards;
  • explain how subfloor ventilation, moisture and termite activity interact under typical Australian housing, and why that matters before you start drying;
  • plan a slow, controlled drying strategy for timber using 230 V equipment and metric psychrometry, understanding why rushed drying splits boards; and
  • make and document a defensible restore-or-replace decision, including how coatings behave in Australian humidity.

This is timber-floor assessment & restoration CEC training: it builds field judgement for the restoration and drying of timber floors. It is not installation or manufacturing guidance, it does not replace your own certification, a manufacturer’s instructions, a Safety Data Sheet (SDS), a relevant Australian standard, or a site’s own rules.

lessons.title: How timber floor assessment and restoration fits with water damage restoration work
lessons.contentBody:
Timber floors are one of the highest-value, highest-risk assets on a water damage job. Get the drying wrong and a floor that could have been saved with a modest, slow drying program ends up replaced at a much higher cost — or a floor that should have been replaced is re-coated over hidden permanent damage and fails months later. Timber floor work sits inside general applied structural drying, but it needs its own assessment logic because timber behaves differently from plasterboard, concrete or carpet underlay: it is hygroscopic, it moves dimensionally with moisture, and it can be damaged permanently in ways that are not visible until sanding begins.

Two Australian industry references sit either side of this course and are worth knowing by name, referenced here in name only. The Australasian Timber Flooring Association (ATFA) publishes trade guidance on installation, acclimatisation and finishing of timber floors, and AS 2796 (timber — hardwood — sawn and milled products, and the associated flooring standards) covers manufacture and installation quality. Neither is written for a wet, previously installed floor that a restoration technician is now trying to save. That gap — assessing and drying an already-installed, already water-affected timber floor — is what this course addresses; no text from ATFA guidance or AS 2796 is reproduced here, they are referenced by name only as context for where restoration practice sits.

Safety note that governs the whole course: if visible mould or a confirmed microbial contamination is present on or under a timber floor, do not run air movers across the affected area. Contain the area, use air filtration devices (AFDs) and dehumidifiers to control moisture, and only introduce air movers once the area has been cleaned and cleared. This applies to subfloor voids as much as to the room above.

Key takeaways

  • This CEC training is for Australian restoration technicians and supervisors who assess and dry water-affected timber floors, and for cleaners, project managers and insurance-support staff who need to understand the process.
  • Timber floors are one of the highest-value, highest-risk assets on a water damage job - getting the drying wrong can turn a savable floor into a costly replacement, or hide permanent damage under a new coat that fails later.
  • Timber behaves differently from plasterboard, concrete or carpet because it is hygroscopic, moves dimensionally with moisture, and can be damaged in ways not visible until sanding begins, so it needs its own assessment logic.
  • Safety rule for the whole course: if visible mould or confirmed microbial contamination is present, do not run air movers - contain the area, use air filtration devices and dehumidifiers to control moisture, and only introduce air movers once the area is cleaned and cleared.

modules.title: Module 2: Australian Timber Species, Floor Construction & the Standards Gap

lessons.title: Australian timber species and how they behave — hardness, movement and moisture response
lessons.contentBody:
Not all timber floors move or dry the same way, and knowing the species on a job changes your assessment and your drying timeline. Five species turn up constantly on Australian residential and commercial floors, and each has a distinct hardness and moisture personality:

  • Spotted gum — a very hard, dense hardwood with strong figure and generally moderate dimensional movement for its density; it can still cup noticeably if one face is wetted for an extended period.
  • Blackbutt — hard and pale, widely used, with moderate movement; a common floor to see in older Australian homes and one that generally tolerates careful drying reasonably well.
  • Jarrah — a dense, dark Western Australian hardwood, slightly softer than spotted gum but still very durable; it is prone to visible colour and tannin staining when wet for extended periods, in addition to the usual movement.
  • Brush box — hard and fine-textured, popular for its even tone; it can be sensitive to uneven drying and is a species where crowning after over-fast drying is commonly seen.
  • Tasmanian oak — a lighter, softer hardwood (a mixed-species group including messmate, mountain ash and alpine ash) with noticeably greater dimensional movement than the denser species above; it cups and swells more readily and needs a more conservative drying approach.

The practical point is not to memorise exact figures but to build the habit of identifying the species (or asking) before you commit to a drying timeline or a restore-versus-replace call. A denser, harder species generally gives you more time and more margin before permanent damage sets in; a softer, more moisture-reactive species like Tasmanian oak gives you less margin and needs closer monitoring from day one.

lessons.title: Floor construction types — solid strip and board, engineered, parquet, and what is underneath
lessons.contentBody:
How a timber floor is built changes how water gets into it and how it should be dried. The main surface types you will see are:

  • Solid strip or board flooring — single-species timber boards, generally tongue-and-groove, fixed directly to joists or battens (or occasionally to a slab via battens). Because the timber is solid through its full thickness, it holds more moisture and takes longer to dry than engineered or laminated products, but it can also often be sanded back further if it has been damaged.
  • Engineered timber flooring — a thinner timber wear layer bonded to a plywood or composite substrate. It dries faster near the surface but is far less tolerant of prolonged wetting: the adhesive bond line and the substrate can delaminate or swell irreversibly well before the timber wear layer itself looks obviously damaged.
  • Parquet — small timber blocks or panels, often adhered directly to a slab with a bonding compound. Individual blocks can lift, cup or shrink away from neighbours independently, and the adhesive layer itself can fail with sustained moisture, which changes the repair approach from “dry it in place” to “dry, then reassess block by block”.

What is underneath the floor matters just as much as the surface. A floor over a suspended timber subfloor has an air gap and (usually) subfloor vents below it, so moisture can move both up from the ground and down from the room, and drying often has to address the subfloor cavity as well as the room above. A floor laid over a concrete slab-on-ground behaves differently again: moisture ingress is more likely to come from a slab moisture problem (rising damp, a failed damp-proof membrane, or a slab that was never adequately cured or sealed before the floor was laid) than from the room side, and the slab itself needs to be checked and dried, not just the timber sitting on it.

Key takeaways

  • Five species appear constantly on Australian floors - spotted gum, blackbutt, jarrah, brush box and Tasmanian oak - each with a distinct hardness and moisture personality that changes your drying timeline.
  • A denser, harder species generally gives more time and margin before permanent damage sets in, while a softer, more moisture-reactive species like Tasmanian oak gives less margin and needs closer monitoring from day one.
  • Construction type changes how water enters and dries - solid strip or board holds more moisture but can be sanded further, engineered floors are far less tolerant of prolonged wetting and can delaminate, and parquet blocks can lift or shrink independently as the adhesive fails.
  • What is underneath matters as much as the surface: a suspended timber subfloor lets moisture move up and down and often needs the cavity dried too, while a slab-on-ground floor is more likely to draw moisture from a slab problem that must itself be checked and dried.

modules.title: Module 3: How Water Damages Timber Floors

lessons.title: Cupping, crowning and buckling — what is happening inside the board
lessons.contentBody:
Timber boards distort in predictable ways when moisture is unevenly distributed through their thickness, and recognising which distortion you are looking at tells you what is happening and what to do about it.

  • Cupping — the edges of each board sit higher than its centre, so the floor feels slightly concave along each board. This happens when the underside (or one face) of the board is wetter than the top: the wetter face swells more than the drier face, and the board bows away from the moisture. Cupping is the most common early sign of a wet timber floor and, if caught reasonably early, is often reversible.
  • Crowning — the opposite shape, where the centre of each board sits higher than its edges. Crowning is most often seen after a cupped floor has been dried, and especially after it has been sanded flat while still wetter underneath than on top: once the underside catches up and dries and shrinks, the board can crown. It is also seen when a floor is dried too aggressively from the top only, so the top shrinks while the underside is still swollen.
  • Buckling — a much more severe response where boards lift, arch or pull away from the subfloor entirely, sometimes across a wide area. Buckling generally follows sustained, significant water exposure (standing water under the floor for an extended period, or a slab moisture problem left unaddressed) and reflects a real loss of fixing or bond, not just board-level swelling.

All three are diagnosed the same practical way: look along the board run under raking light, feel the surface with a straightedge, and back it up with moisture meter readings across the board and against an unaffected reference area. Visual assessment alone under-calls the extent of the problem; moisture readings tell you how far the water has actually travelled.

lessons.title: Cupping versus permanent set — what recovers and what does not
lessons.contentBody:
The single most important distinction in timber floor restoration is between distortion that will relax as the board returns to its dry moisture content, and distortion that will not relax no matter how well you dry the floor. Ordinary cupping caused by a recent, moderate moisture gradient is usually reversible: as the wetter face dries down towards the drier face and the moisture gradient through the board flattens out, the board largely returns to flat, and a light sand once the whole board is uniformly at the dry standard can finish the job.

Permanent set is different. When timber cells have been under sustained compression or the moisture exposure has been severe or prolonged enough, the wood fibres themselves are crushed or damaged, and the board will not return to its original shape even once it is fully and evenly dry. A board with permanent set may still look cupped, flat, or even crowned once dry — the shape after drying is not a reliable test on its own. What distinguishes permanent set from ordinary cupping is that the distortion does not track the moisture reading: if the board is confirmed at (or very close to) the dry standard on a moisture meter and the deformation is still present, that is permanent set, not moisture-driven cupping, and no further drying will fix it.

This distinction is also why sanding a floor too early is a common and expensive mistake. Sanding a board that is still cupped because it is still wetter underneath removes timber unevenly (more from the raised edges), and once the board later dries and flattens (or crowns), the earlier sanding pattern is locked in as a genuine surface defect. The rule is straightforward: do not sand or refinish until moisture readings across the affected boards are at or near the dry standard, and the shape has been reassessed at that point — not before.

Key takeaways

  • Cupping (edges higher than centre) is the most common early sign of a wet floor and happens when the underside is wetter than the top - caught reasonably early, it is often reversible.
  • Crowning (centre higher than edges) most often appears after a cupped floor is dried or sanded while still wetter underneath, or when a floor is dried too aggressively from the top only.
  • Buckling is a much more severe response where boards lift or pull away from the subfloor, generally following sustained significant water exposure and a real loss of fixing or bond.
  • The key distinction is permanent set versus ordinary cupping: if a board is at or very close to the dry standard on the meter but the distortion remains, that is permanent set that drying will not fix - so do not sand or refinish until readings are at or near the dry standard and the shape has been reassessed.

modules.title: Module 4: Moisture Assessment & Setting the Dry Standard

lessons.title: Assessing timber floor moisture with metric moisture meters
lessons.contentBody:
Timber floor moisture is assessed with a moisture meter set to (or corrected for) the species being tested, reading in a metric moisture content percentage. Pin-type meters give a reading at a specific depth and are useful for checking whether moisture is concentrated near the surface or deeper in the board; non-invasive (pinless) meters scan a wider area quickly without marking the finish, which matters on a floor you are hoping to save and re-coat rather than replace. Many technicians use both: a pinless scan to map the affected area quickly, followed by targeted pin readings at problem spots and, where practical, from the underside via an access point or the subfloor.

A single reading tells you very little on its own. What matters is the pattern: readings taken in a grid across the affected room, compared board-to-board and edge-to-centre, and tracked over consecutive days. A board reading high at its edges and lower at its centre points to a moisture gradient consistent with cupping; readings that stay high at the same spot day after day point to an ongoing moisture source (a subfloor leak, rising damp through a slab, or a hidden reservoir) that needs to be found and stopped before drying will ever succeed. Always record species, meter type and correction setting alongside every reading, since the same raw number means different things on jarrah than it does on Tasmanian oak.

lessons.title: Setting a dry standard against unaffected boards
lessons.contentBody:
As with any structural drying, you cannot know a timber floor is dry without a reference. The dry standard for a timber floor is the moisture content reading taken from the same species, in an unaffected part of the same floor or building — for example, boards in an adjoining room the water never reached, or an area under a rug or cabinet that was not wetted. Where no unaffected reference of the same species and construction exists on site, a reasonable published moisture content range for that species in the relevant Australian climate zone can be used as a fallback, but a genuine in-building reference is always preferable because it accounts for the floor’s actual history and local conditions.

Set the dry standard at the start of the job, in writing, using the same meter and the same correction setting you will use for every subsequent reading. State the drying goal in terms anyone reviewing the file can check — for example, “return affected spotted gum boards to within a small margin of the 11–12 % reading taken in the unaffected adjoining room, on the same meter, before considering re-sanding” — rather than an unreferenced statement like “dry it out”. A dry standard set this way is defensible to an insurer, a loss adjuster, or a homeowner questioning why the floor took as long as it did, and it is the trigger point that tells you when it is safe to reassess for sanding and re-coating rather than continuing to run equipment.

Key takeaways

  • Assess timber floor moisture with a meter set or corrected for the species, reading a metric moisture content percentage - pin-type meters check specific depths while pinless meters scan a wide area without marking a floor you hope to save.
  • A single reading tells you little; what matters is the pattern taken in a grid across the room, compared board-to-board and edge-to-centre and tracked over consecutive days, with readings that stay high pointing to an ongoing moisture source to find and stop.
  • Always record species, meter type and correction setting with every reading, since the same raw number means different things on jarrah than on Tasmanian oak.
  • Set a dry standard from the same species in an unaffected part of the building (with a published species range only as a fallback), in writing at the start, using the same meter and correction setting - this makes the drying goal defensible to an insurer, loss adjuster or homeowner.

modules.title: Module 5: Subfloor Ventilation, Termites & Moisture in Australian Housing

lessons.title: Subfloor ventilation and moisture in suspended timber subfloors
lessons.contentBody:
A large proportion of older Australian housing is built with a suspended timber floor over a ventilated subfloor void, with airbricks or vents set into the perimeter footing to let outside air move through and carry subfloor moisture away. When a floor above is water-damaged, the subfloor void is often part of the problem: water can travel down through gaps and penetrations to pool or soak into bearers and joists, and a subfloor that was already poorly ventilated before the incident will hold that moisture far longer than a well-ventilated one, extending the drying timeline for the floor above.

Before starting to dry a timber floor over a suspended subfloor, check the subfloor void itself: look for existing vent blockages (garden beds built up against airbricks, paving laid over vents, or vents simply painted over), standing water, damp bearers and joists, and general ventilation condition. A drying plan that only addresses the room above while ignoring a wet, poorly ventilated subfloor will fail to reach or hold the dry standard, because moisture keeps migrating back up from below. In many jobs, subfloor dehumidification or increased subfloor airflow (again, only once any mould risk in the void has been addressed — see the mould rule below) is as important to the outcome as the equipment running in the room above.

lessons.title: Termites and subfloor moisture — what a restoration technician needs to recognise
lessons.contentBody:
Subfloor moisture and termite activity are closely linked in Australian conditions: damp, poorly ventilated timber subfloors are exactly the conditions termites favour, and a water damage event can create or worsen those conditions in an existing termite-prone property. This course does not train you as a pest inspector, and termite identification and treatment is a licensed specialist task — but a restoration technician working under a floor needs to recognise the signs worth flagging: mud shelter tubes on piers, bearers or the underside of the floor; timber that sounds hollow or is unusually soft when probed; frass (termite droppings) or damaged, honeycombed timber; and any existing termite management system (such as a chemical or physical barrier) that may be disturbed by drying works or by digging around footings.

The practical rule is straightforward: if you see anything suggestive of termite activity in a subfloor void during assessment or drying, stop, document what you saw (photographs, location, and a plain description), and refer the client to a licensed pest inspector or timber pest technician before continuing structural drying or restoration work in that area. Do not attempt to identify the termite species, assess the extent of damage, or recommend treatment yourself — that is outside a restoration technician’s scope and is a matter for the appropriately licensed trade.

Key takeaways

  • Much older Australian housing has a suspended timber floor over a ventilated subfloor void, and after water damage that void is often part of the problem - a poorly ventilated subfloor holds moisture far longer and extends the drying timeline for the floor above.
  • Before drying, check the void itself for vent blockages, standing water, and damp bearers and joists, because a plan that only dries the room above will fail to reach or hold the dry standard while moisture keeps migrating up from below.
  • Damp, poorly ventilated subfloors are exactly the conditions termites favour, so a restoration technician working under a floor should recognise signs worth flagging such as mud shelter tubes, hollow or soft timber, and frass.
  • Termite identification and treatment is a licensed specialist task - if you see anything suggestive of termite activity, stop, document what you saw, and refer the client to a licensed pest inspector rather than identifying the species or assessing the damage yourself.

modules.title: Module 6: Drying Strategy for Timber Floors

lessons.title: Why timber floors must be dried slowly — controlled drying, psychrometry and 230 V equipment
lessons.contentBody:
Timber is far less forgiving of fast drying than plasterboard or concrete. If the surface of a board is dried much faster than moisture can migrate up from its core, the board develops a steep internal moisture gradient: the outside shrinks while the inside is still swollen, and the resulting internal stress is exactly what produces splitting, checking (fine surface cracks along the grain) and the crowning described earlier. The drying goal for timber is not the fastest possible result — it is a slow, even, controlled reduction in moisture content across the whole thickness of the board, tracked with the same metric psychrometric approach used elsewhere in structural drying: temperature (°C), relative humidity (%), dew point and specific humidity (g/kg), read with a calibrated meter and logged at the same time each day.

In practice this means running dehumidification to keep the air in the room dry enough to pull moisture out of the timber, while deliberately moderating airflow rather than maximising it — strong, direct air movement across a wet timber floor can dry the surface too fast relative to the core. All equipment must sit within a realistic 230 V / 10 A general power outlet (GPO) budget for the site, protected by a working residual current device (RCD/safety switch), with every lead and appliance carrying a current test-and-tag record consistent with AS/NZS 3760 and AS/NZS 3112. Add up the running current of each machine at 230 V, keep the total on any one circuit below its rating, and spread machines across circuits rather than daisy-chaining power boards.

Mould rule: if visible mould or confirmed microbial contamination is present on the floor surface or in the subfloor void, do not run air movers across the affected area. Contain the space, run air filtration devices (AFDs) and dehumidifiers to control moisture, and only introduce air movers once the area has been cleaned and cleared. This applies equally to the room above the floor and to the subfloor void beneath it.

lessons.title: Staged drying and monitoring a timber floor to the dry standard
lessons.contentBody:
Because timber holds and releases moisture slowly relative to other building materials, a timber floor drying plan is usually staged rather than run flat-out from day one. An early stage typically favours dehumidification over aggressive airflow, giving the moisture gradient inside each board time to even out as the surface and core equalise; only once surface moisture has come down and the gradient has flattened does gentle, broader airflow become useful to keep pulling moisture from the whole floor area. Running maximum airflow from the first hour, before the internal gradient has had a chance to even out, is one of the most common causes of the splitting and crowning covered earlier.

  • Take moisture readings across the same grid points each day, at the same time, with the same meter and correction setting, and compare against the dry standard set in Module 4.
  • Track the trend, not just a single day’s number — a board that is falling steadily towards the dry standard is progressing normally, even if it is not there yet; a board that has stalled needs investigation.
  • If readings stop falling for more than a day or two, investigate before assuming the job is close to finished: check for a hidden moisture source, a subfloor ventilation problem, dehumidification not keeping up with the space, or airflow not actually reaching the affected boards.
  • Only when the affected boards are at or near the dry standard, and the shape (cupping or crowning) has been reassessed at that moisture level, should sanding or re-coating be considered.

Key takeaways

  • Timber is far less forgiving of fast drying than plasterboard or concrete - drying the surface much faster than moisture can migrate from the core creates a steep internal gradient that produces splitting, checking and crowning.
  • The goal is a slow, even, controlled reduction in moisture across the whole board thickness, tracked with metric psychrometry (temperature, relative humidity, dew point and specific humidity) logged at the same time each day, running dehumidification while deliberately moderating airflow.
  • All equipment must sit within a realistic 230 V / 10 A general power outlet budget, protected by a working residual current device, with every lead test-and-tagged consistent with AS/NZS 3760 and AS/NZS 3112, and machines spread across circuits rather than daisy-chained.
  • Drying is usually staged - dehumidification first to let the gradient even out, gentle broader airflow later - and readings should be tracked against the dry standard as a trend, with any stall investigated before assuming the job is nearly finished.

modules.title: Module 7: Restore or Replace, Coatings & Documentation

lessons.title: When a floor can be dried and re-sanded/re-coated versus when it must be replaced
lessons.contentBody:
The restore-or-replace decision should be made from evidence gathered during drying, not from a first-day visual impression. A floor is generally a reasonable candidate for drying, sanding and re-coating when: the moisture gradient has been shown (through repeated readings) to be closing rather than static; distortion present is consistent with ordinary cupping that is tracking the moisture readings down; there is enough remaining board thickness to allow a sand once the floor is at the dry standard; and there is no permanent set, no significant delamination (in engineered floors), and no structural loss of fixing to the subfloor.

Replacement (of the affected boards, a section, or the whole floor) should be considered when: moisture readings confirm the board is at or near the dry standard but the distortion has not relaxed (permanent set); an engineered floor shows delamination between the wear layer and substrate; boards have buckled and lost their bond or fixing to the subfloor; the timber itself is structurally compromised (rot, termite damage confirmed by a licensed pest inspector, or fibre so degraded it will not hold a sanding pass); or there is not enough serviceable thickness left for a sand after accounting for the existing wear history of the floor. Document the evidence behind whichever decision is made — this is what an insurer, loss adjuster or homeowner will want to see if the outcome is questioned later.

lessons.title: Coating behaviour in Australian humidity and documenting the job
lessons.contentBody:
Once a floor is confirmed at the dry standard and structurally sound, coating (sanding and applying a floor finish) still needs to account for Australian conditions. Coatings cure and cross-link at rates sensitive to ambient temperature and humidity, and a floor that is chemically “dry to touch” is not necessarily fully cured underneath; applying a second or third coat, or allowing normal foot traffic, before full cure in humid conditions is a common cause of coating failure (soft finish, tackiness, or poor adhesion) that gets mistaken for a moisture problem. Coatings applied over timber that is still marginally above the dry standard are also more likely to trap residual moisture beneath the film, leading to finish failure or renewed cupping later even though the drying equipment has been removed. Follow the coating manufacturer’s stated recovery and cure times for the ambient conditions on site, and do not compress them to meet a handover date.

Close every timber floor job with a documentation file that stands up to scrutiny: the dry standard and reference readings taken at the start; daily moisture readings across the grid with dates, meter and correction settings; photographs of any cupping, crowning, buckling or subfloor issues found, and of any termite-related referral made; the reasoning behind the final restore-or-replace decision; and confirmation of the moisture reading at handover against the dry standard. In the Australian insurance and dispute context, a floor job without this record is a floor job that cannot be defended if the outcome is later questioned.

Key takeaways

  • Make the restore-or-replace decision from evidence gathered during drying, not a first-day visual - a floor is a reasonable candidate to dry, sand and re-coat when the gradient is closing, the distortion tracks the readings down, enough board thickness remains, and there is no permanent set, delamination or loss of fixing.
  • Consider replacement when the board is at the dry standard but distortion has not relaxed (permanent set), an engineered floor delaminates, boards have buckled and lost their bond, the timber is structurally compromised, or too little serviceable thickness remains for a sand.
  • Coatings cure at rates sensitive to temperature and humidity, so a floor that is dry to touch may not be fully cured underneath - follow the manufacturer stated recovery and cure times and do not compress them, since coating over marginally wet timber can trap moisture and lead to finish failure or renewed cupping.
  • Close every job with a documentation file that stands up to scrutiny: the dry standard and reference readings, daily grid readings with dates and settings, photographs of any distortion or subfloor issues, any termite referral, the reasoning behind the decision, and the handover reading against the dry standard.

courses.title: CARSI Indoor Environment Assessment Practitioner
courses.description:
A foundational, Australian-produced course for restoration technicians, supervisors and assessors who need to read the condition of an indoor space before deciding how — or whether — to remediate it, and for cleaners and insurance-support staff who want to understand how a competent indoor environmental quality (IEQ) assessment is built. It is indoor-environment assessment CEC training — it aligns to the principles of professional indoor environmental assessment practice and is designed to build sound field judgement, not to replace a technician’s own certification, a manufacturer’s instructions, a Safety Data Sheet, or a site’s own rules.

Seven modules take an assessor from what indoor environmental quality means and the assessor’s observe-measure-interpret-recommend role, through viewing the building as a system of moisture, ventilation, HVAC and occupancy, metric psychrometry (temperature, RH, dew point and specific humidity in g/kg) and moisture-in-materials readings, a systematic visual and moisture inspection method with mapped findings, interpreting readings against unaffected reference areas and Australian climate conditions, recognising mould and the moisture–mould link, and knowing the limits of your own competency — including the absolute stop-and-refer rule for suspected asbestos — before producing a clear, defensible assessment report. Every figure is metric, every reference is Australian, and the mould-remediation sequence is respected throughout. A ten-question knowledge check closes the course.

CARSI Indoor Environment Assessment Practitioner is a CARSI Southern Hemisphere Restoration Designation. Completing this CEC-accredited training also earns IICRC Continuing Education Credits (CECs).
courses.shortDescription: Indoor-environment assessment CEC training — IEQ fundamentals, the building-as-a-system view, metric psychrometry and moisture readings, inspection and mapping method, reference-area interpretation, mould recognition, competency limits and referral, and defensible reporting, produced for Australian conditions.

meta.discipline: CARSI Indoor Environment Assessment Practitioner — CARSI Southern Hemisphere designation (CEC-accredited)
meta.standardsReferenced: Nominative only (e.g. ANSI/IICRC S500, S520). No standard text reproduced (IICRC AI Use Policy honoured).

modules.title: Module 1: Course Objectives & How This Training Works

lessons.title: What this course covers and who it is for
lessons.contentBody:
Welcome to Assessing Indoor Environment Conditions. This course is for restoration technicians, supervisors and assessors in Australia who need to read a space before deciding how — or whether — to remediate it, and for cleaners and insurance-support staff who want to understand how a competent indoor environmental quality (IEQ) assessment is built. By the end you will be able to:

  • explain what indoor environmental quality (IEQ) means and describe the assessor’s role — observe, measure, interpret, recommend — never guess;
  • view a building as a system, where moisture, ventilation, HVAC and occupancy behaviour interact to create the conditions you are assessing;
  • take and record metric psychrometric readings — temperature, relative humidity, dew point and specific humidity (g/kg) — and moisture readings in materials, with awareness of airborne particulate and mould indicators;
  • carry out a systematic visual and moisture inspection and map your findings so another assessor could follow your trail;
  • interpret readings against an unaffected reference area and against Australian climate conditions, rather than against a number pulled from memory;
  • recognise mould and the moisture–mould link, and apply the mould sequence — air filtration devices (AFDs) and dehumidifiers only, air movers only after clearance;
  • know the limits of your own competency and when to stop and refer — to an occupational hygienist, a licensed assessor, or in the case of suspected asbestos, to stop disturbing the material entirely; and
  • produce a clear, defensible assessment report that supports the next decision, rather than making that decision for someone else.

This is indoor-environment assessment CEC training: it aligns to the principles of professional indoor environmental assessment practice. It builds field judgement and does not replace your own certification, a manufacturer’s instructions, a Safety Data Sheet (SDS), a relevant Australian standard, or a site’s own rules.

lessons.title: How indoor environment assessment fits with restoration work
lessons.contentBody:
Every remediation decision in restoration — how much material to remove, how long to dry, whether a space is safe to reoccupy, whether to call in a specialist — rests on an assessment of the indoor environment. Get the assessment wrong and every decision built on it is wrong too: over-remediate and you waste the client’s money and gut a building that did not need it; under-assess and you hand back a space that still carries elevated moisture, hidden mould, or a hazard nobody has named. Assessment is not a formality before the “real” work starts — it is the work that makes every later step defensible.

Assessment sits at more than one point in a job. It happens at first contact, to scope the problem and identify hazards before anyone starts moving equipment or material. It happens daily during drying, to confirm the plan set from Module 1’s dry standard is actually working. And it happens at the end, to support the decision that a space meets a reasonable standard for reoccupation. In each case the assessor’s job is the same four steps: observe the space and occupant history, measure with calibrated instruments, interpret the readings against a reference and against Australian conditions, and recommend — in writing — what should happen next.

Safety note that governs the whole course: where mould is present or suspected, do not run air movers across affected surfaces. Use air filtration devices (AFDs) and dehumidifiers to manage moisture and airborne spores, and only introduce air movers once the area has been cleaned and cleared. And where a material is suspected to contain asbestos, the assessor’s job is to stop, not disturb it, and refer to a licensed assessor — never to test, sand, cut or vacuum it yourself.

modules.title: Module 2: What Indoor Environmental Quality Is & the Assessor’s Role

lessons.title: Defining indoor environmental quality
lessons.contentBody:
Indoor environmental quality (IEQ) describes the condition of the air, surfaces and moisture balance inside a building as they affect the health of the building and the health of the people in it. It is broader than “is it wet” — IEQ takes in temperature and humidity comfort, airborne particulates and bioaerosols, ventilation adequacy, odour, and the presence of moisture-related hazards such as mould. In a restoration context, IEQ assessment is the discipline of establishing, with evidence, what state a space is actually in before anyone decides what to do about it.

An IEQ assessment is not a single measurement. It combines a documented history (what happened, when, and what has been done since), a visual inspection, moisture and psychrometric readings, and — where warranted — awareness of airborne particulate and mould indicators. No single number tells the whole story; the assessor’s value is in weighing all of it together against a reference condition and Australian climate norms for the region and season.

  • IEQ assessment supports decisions about remediation scope, drying duration and reoccupation — it does not replace those decisions, and it does not replace a client’s or insurer’s own process.
  • Assessment is proportionate: a small isolated leak needs a simpler assessment than a multi-room event with a health complaint attached.
  • Every assessment should be repeatable — another competent assessor, given your notes, should be able to follow the same trail and reach a similar conclusion.

lessons.title: Observe, measure, interpret, recommend — not guess
lessons.contentBody:
The assessor’s role can be reduced to four disciplined steps, always in this order. Observe: walk the space, note the building type, finishes, occupancy, any visible staining, odour, or damage, and ask about history — when did the water event happen, has it happened before, has anyone reported symptoms. Measure: take calibrated readings — temperature, relative humidity, moisture content in materials — in a systematic pattern, not just where the problem looks obvious. Interpret: compare every reading against an unaffected reference area in the same building and against what is normal for the Australian climate zone and season, never against a number memorised from a different job or a different country. Recommend: state, in writing, what the findings mean and what should happen next — further drying, further investigation, referral to a specialist, or that the space meets a reasonable standard.

What separates a competent assessment from a guess is that every one of those four steps is visible in the record. A guess skips straight to a conclusion (“looks dry to me”, “smells fine”) without the observation, the measurement or the reference comparison that would let someone else check the work. In the Australian insurance and dispute context, an assessment that cannot show its working is treated the same as no assessment at all.

  • Never state a conclusion in a report that is not backed by a specific observation or reading recorded in that same report.
  • Where readings are inconclusive or contradictory, say so — do not round to the answer that is easiest to write down.
  • Recommend action in proportion to the evidence: a marginal reading warrants monitoring, not automatic full remediation, and a clear hazard warrants immediate action, not a “wait and see”.

modules.title: Module 3: The Building as a System

lessons.title: Moisture, ventilation, HVAC and occupancy together
lessons.contentBody:
A building is not a static box — it is a system in which moisture, ventilation, heating/cooling (HVAC) and the people who live or work inside it constantly interact. A leak is rarely the whole story: how quickly a space dries, whether mould takes hold, and how comfortable and healthy the air feels all depend on how these elements work together. An assessor who looks only at the wet patch and ignores the system around it will consistently misjudge the risk.

Moisture enters a building from plumbing failures, roof and envelope leaks, rising damp, condensation, and everyday occupant activity (cooking, showering, drying washing indoors). Ventilation — natural (openable windows, vents) or mechanical (exhaust fans, ducted systems) — determines how quickly moisture-laden air is removed rather than allowed to condense on cool surfaces. HVAC systems move conditioned air throughout a building and, if contaminated or poorly maintained, can distribute moisture or bioaerosols well beyond the original source room. Occupancy — how many people, how they use the space, whether they run exhaust fans, whether they have reported symptoms — shapes both the moisture load and the urgency of the assessment.

  • Always ask what the HVAC system does and where its return air comes from — a source of moisture or mould near a return can spread the problem building-wide.
  • Poor ventilation (sealed rooms, closed vents, disabled exhaust fans) can turn a minor moisture event into a mould problem far faster than the water volume alone would suggest.
  • Occupant behaviour observed on the day (windows shut, clothes drying on racks indoors, bathroom fan disconnected) is a legitimate and important assessment finding, not an aside.

lessons.title: Australian building stock and climate variation
lessons.contentBody:
Australian building stock varies enormously, and a competent assessor adjusts expectations accordingly. Brick veneer over a suspended timber sub-floor behaves differently to slab-on-ground; lightweight cladding over steel or timber frame dries and traps moisture differently again; older weatherboard housing has different envelope leakage than a modern, tightly sealed apartment. Roof space, sub-floor ventilation, and wall cavity construction all change how moisture moves through and out of a structure.

Climate matters just as much as construction. Tropical and subtropical northern Australia carries high ambient humidity for much of the year, which changes what a “normal” unaffected reading looks like and raises background mould risk. Arid and semi-arid interior regions run low ambient humidity, so even a modest moisture event stands out sharply against the dry background. Temperate southern and coastal regions sit in between, with seasonal swings that shift the reference reading from summer to winter. An assessor working, for example, in Cairns in the wet season and one working in Alice Springs in winter should expect very different baseline readings from the same instrument in a completely unaffected room — and both are correct for their conditions.

  • Always establish the local, current-season baseline on site — never assume a fixed “normal” humidity number applies nationally.
  • Note the building’s construction type and climate zone in the assessment record; both directly affect how readings should be interpreted.
  • Sub-floor and roof space conditions are frequently overlooked and can be a persistent moisture source even after visible water damage inside the living area has dried.

modules.title: Module 4: What to Measure and With What

lessons.title: Psychrometrics: temperature, RH, dew point and specific humidity
lessons.contentBody:
Reading the air is central to assessing an indoor environment, and every reading in this course is metric. Temperature (°C) sets the context for every other reading — the same amount of water vapour in the air reads as a different relative humidity as temperature changes. Relative humidity (RH, %) is the most commonly quoted figure but is the easiest to misread in isolation, because it moves with temperature even when the actual water content of the air has not changed. Dew point (°C) is the temperature at which the air’s moisture will begin to condense on a surface — it tells you whether a cool surface (a window, an external wall, a slab) is at risk of condensation right now. Specific humidity, expressed in grams of water per kilogram of dry air (g/kg), is the actual mass of water carried by the air and is the figure that lets you compare two rooms fairly, even if they are at different temperatures.

A hygrometer or a psychrometric-capable meter gives you temperature and RH directly; dew point and specific humidity are then read from the instrument or calculated. Take readings in the same units, with the same instrument, at the same height above floor level, in every room you compare — consistency is what makes readings comparable at all.

  • Never judge moisture risk from RH alone — always convert to or record specific humidity (g/kg) when comparing rooms at different temperatures.
  • A dew point close to a surface’s actual temperature is a condensation risk, even if RH in the room reads moderate.
  • Record ambient outdoor temperature and RH as well — it explains why an indoor reading looks the way it does.

lessons.title: Moisture in materials and awareness of airborne indicators
lessons.contentBody:
Air readings tell you about the atmosphere; moisture content readings in materials tell you what is actually happening inside the building fabric. A pin-type or non-invasive (pinless) moisture meter, used consistently across a material type, lets you compare an affected area against an unaffected reference of the same material — timber against timber, plasterboard against plasterboard, concrete against concrete — because different materials naturally hold different baseline moisture levels. Map every reading to a location so that a re-check days later is measuring the same spot.

Beyond moisture meters, an assessor should be aware of — without over-reaching into — airborne particulate and mould indicators. Visible growth, musty or earthy odour, and staining consistent with prior moisture are all legitimate observations to record. Where a health complaint, extensive hidden growth, or ambiguous results are present, that awareness is exactly what should trigger a referral to an occupational hygienist for air sampling or laboratory analysis — it is not the assessor’s job to run that testing themselves without the appropriate qualification.

  • Always compare a material moisture reading to an unaffected reference of the same material, not a generic number.
  • Record instrument type, reading, location and depth (where relevant) for every moisture reading — a bare number without location is not useful evidence.
  • Musty odour and visible growth are valid observations in your own right, but confirming species or airborne concentration is outside a restoration assessor’s competency — refer it.

modules.title: Module 5: Inspection Method and Mapping Findings

lessons.title: A systematic visual and moisture inspection
lessons.contentBody:
A defensible inspection follows a method, not a wander.
